column3: That is, as the Chaldean renders it, the Moabites rebelled against Israel, namely against Ochozias the son of Achab, who reigned in Israel. For when they saw that Achab had been slain in battle, his army scattered, and Ochozias a young man, they took courage and rebelled against him to whom they had hitherto been subject, refusing tribute and establishing their own king — just as at around the same time the Idumeans, subdued by David, rebelled against Joram king of Judah, as we shall hear in chapter 8. So says the Abulensis.

That is, through latticed and netted windows of a floor, as Pagninus, Vatablus, and others say. Or he calls the upper room a solarium or flat roof, which in Palestine was level, so that one might walk, dine, and sleep upon it. Now this roof was surrounded on all sides by a parapet or wall, lest anyone should carelessly fall from it, according to the law of Deuteronomy 22:8. This wall was latticed and grilled, and from it, when the railings gave way or broke, Ochozias fell. So says Sanchez. Tropologically, Eucherius says: Ochozias, king of Samaria, who separated himself from the house of David and fell through the lattices, seems to signify this: that heretics or schismatics, even if they appear to climb to some height of good action, yet because they lack the cohesion of the unity of the Church, as if supported by open and unsolid walls on their sides, will always relapse into the weakness of vices, and perish in the arrogance of their obstinacy, destitute of divine aid. CONSULT BEELZEBUB THE GOD OF ACCARON.] The Septuagint: Consult Baalmuia, the god of Accaron. Josephus: Consult the oracle of Myodes (for that was the name of the god): myia means fly, hence Myodes is the god of flies, who was invoked to drive away flies, which abounded at Accaron on account of the damp and warm soil near the sea. The same was called Jupiter Apomyios, as Pausanias attests in book 5, and Pliny in book 29, chapter 6, where he calls Myodes "Achares," perhaps because he was worshipped at Accaron, as is said here. In similar fashion the god Sminthus was worshipped to drive away mice, for the Cretans call mice sminthas; and Parnopius was worshipped to drive away parnopas, that is gnats, or, as others will have it, locusts, as Pausanias attests in his Attica. For the Gentiles placed gods over all things, and so they worshipped Fever, Cloacina, Panic, and Pallor as divine powers, as St. Augustine attests in book 4 of The City of God, chapters 16 and 23. Thus Beelzebub in Hebrew is the same as Baal or Beel and Bel combined with Zebub, that is, fly — or Baal of the Fly, as the Septuagint translates; for this idol was fashioned in the shape of a fly, says Nazianzen in book 2 against Julian, and Theodoret in question 3. Hence also Philastrius numbers among the heresies the Myolatrae, that is, the adorers of the fly, or the Muscaccaronites, at heresy 13. See what was said on Matthew 10:25. To which add that some, following Serarius, hold that Beelzebub or Myodes, that is, the God of the Fly, was a god or goddess of lust, on account of the wantonness and licentiousness observed in flies; whence Lucian in his Encomium of the Fly relates that there were once prostitutes who were called flies. Moreover, all gods (that is, demons) can be called flies on account of their multitude, insolence, impudence, restlessness, filth, and stench. Hence in this age a demon, flying in the form of a great fly or hornet upon one who had abandoned the orthodox faith and professed Anabaptism, immediately seemed to possess him and made him learned in Holy Scripture, and likewise deprived him of this knowledge if he returned from Anabaptism to the faith, as eyewitnesses report. Again, demons are called flies because, as Lucian says in his book On Sacrifices, they swarm over the blood of victims after the manner of flies. Add to this that demons sometimes flew into the prophets of the Gentiles in the manner of flies to deliver oracles, just as the Roman Ritual records that a demon sometimes approaches to obsess the energumens. Hence therefore among the Jews, one who was contemptible and detestable was called Beelzebub (Matthew 10). The prince of demons himself (Matthew 12) is so called as being the most wanton, most shameless, and most contemptible of all, says Serarius; or because Myodes was Jupiter Apomyios, as was said.

For Elias went about unshorn, with long beard and hair, clothed in rough and shaggy garment — that is, wrapped in a melote, or unshorn sheepskin or goatskin — girt with a leather belt, that is, with hide not dressed but still hairy and shaggy, like a hermit who despises the world and is a lover of poverty and ancient simplicity. See Gaspar Schatzgeyer, book 1 of the Institutes, chapter 1. Rabbi Eliezer fabulously relates in chapter 31 that this belt of Elias was made from the hide of that ram which Abraham sacrificed in place of Isaac, Genesis 22.

From the summit of Mount Carmel come down to us, that we may lead you as a captive to King Ochozias, to whom you foretold death, so that you may give account of this dire oracle and suffer your punishment. Therefore this captain of fifty sinned, first, by impiety, because he consented to the impiety of Ochozias who wished to punish Elias, and was his minister in this. Second, by arrogance, because he commanded him imperiously and proudly, as though the executor of a royal command, ordering him to come down; and therefore, struck down by Elias — or rather by God — by heavenly fire, he perished.

Elijah makes serious the ironic title given to him, and turns it back upon the head of the one who spoke it through solemn retribution. Hence note: for "God" in the Hebrew is *Elohim*, a word signifying God as judge and avenger — as if to say: "If I am a minister of *Elohim*, that is, of the supreme judge and avenger, as you have mockingly declared, then let *Elohim* himself defend me as His minister, and punish your insolence and violence, by which you seek to seize me, His minister, and drag me against my will before the king." For this violence redounds upon God Himself, who is my hero and Lord. Wherefore Elijah avenged this injury to himself — or rather, to God — calling down fire from heaven with vehement spirit. He will do the same at the end of the world together with Enoch, contending against Antichrist and his followers; whence it is said of both in Apocalypse 11:5: *And if any man will hurt them, fire shall come out of their mouth and shall devour their enemies*; and in Sirach 48:1: *Then Elijah the prophet arose like a fire, and his word burned like a torch*; and at verse 3: *He brought down fire from heaven three times*. See what is said there. Symbolically, Saint Ambrose, in his book *On Those Who Are Initiated into the Mysteries*, chapter 9, compares the priest consecrating the Eucharist with Elijah. For just as Elijah consumed the captain of fifty and his men with heavenly fire, so the priest by the divine power of Christ's words consumes the substance of bread and wine, and converts it into the Body of Christ. For, as Lanfranc says against Berengarius: "If the word of Elijah had such power as to bring down fire from heaven, shall not the word of Christ avail to change the species of the elements?"

This second captain of fifty was more impudent than the first: first, because having heard of the punishment of his predecessor he did not come to his senses; and further, because he increased his impudence by adding the word *hasten* — and therefore was struck with the same penalty and consumed by heavenly fire. *I beseech thee, have mercy on my soul.* This third captain, terrified by the punishment of the two preceding him and thus made wiser, prostrates himself before Elijah as a suppliant. Hence he does not dare to demand or say that he should descend, but entreats mercy — that Elijah might spare him, namely, and tell him what must be done: that is, what he ought to report back to the king. Wherefore Elijah not only spared him, but, at the admonition of the Angel, willingly joined the man as a companion on his way to the king.

— who, that is, while his father Jehoshaphat was still living but had gone with Ahab to battle against the Syrians and was therefore absent, reigned in Jerusalem — concerning whom see chapter 3, verse 1. For when Ahab was slain in battle, his son Ochozias immediately succeeded him in the kingdom; and when Ochozias died of his illness in the first year of his reign, his brother Joram succeeded him. COMMENTARY ON THE FOURTH BOOK OF KINGS. Chapter II. ON THE SECOND CHAPTER.

**Double** — that is, twice as great — so say some, with Theodoret (q. 7) and the Greek commentators. Blessed Peter Damian agrees (Serm. 2, on the Nativity of St. John the Baptist): "In Elisha," he says, "there was a double spirit of Elijah, not by a doubling of merits, but by a display of miracles; for he performed 24 miracles, whereas Elijah performed only 12, though others count fewer." Hence Anastasius of Antioch (q. 55) reckons the signs of Elijah as seven, and those of Elisha as fourteen. St. Ambrose also agrees (Serm. 2, on Elisha), who exclaims: *O precious inheritance, in which more is left to the heir than was possessed; he who receives acquires more than he who bestows could claim as his own! Truly precious is that inheritance which, while being transferred from father to son, is doubled by a certain interest upon its merits! Therefore Elijah, though he himself possessed the simple spirit of holiness, left to Elisha a double portion thereof. Wondrous indeed, then, that Elijah left behind more grace upon the earth than he carried with him to heaven: and though he himself was wholly taken up bodily to higher things, he yet remains in his son with a greater sanctity.* Others interpret **double** as referring to two-thirds of the remaining portion — that is, as if the spirit of Elijah were divided into three parts, Elisha would request two of them for himself. This seems to be suggested by the Hebrew word for "two," whence Pagninus translates: *Let a measure of two parts of thy spirit be upon me.* But the better interpretation attributes the **double** to Elijah himself — as if to say: I pray, O Elijah, that thy spirit, which is double, that is, manifold, powerful, and excellent, may flow down upon me; so that, with thy departure, I as thy son may succeed thee in thine excellent spirit as in a spiritual inheritance, and with the same zeal as thine, may defend the worship of the true God against the worshippers of Baal. Hence Angelomus says: "A double spirit is the spirit of prophecy and of miracles." This is confirmed, first, from the Hebrew, which has: *Let the mouth of two be in thy spirit for me* — for the firstborn had a double portion of the paternal inheritance, whereas the other sons had only a single and one portion; as if to say: I ask that I, as thy firstborn, O Elijah, may succeed thee in both thy spirits. Second, because in Ecclesiasticus 48:4 it is said of Elijah: *Who can glory as thou canst, Elijah?* Therefore he was of greater spirit than Elisha. Third, because in the same place at verse 13 it is said: *And in Elisha the spirit of Elijah rested.* See what is said there.

The reason for this condition proposed to Elisha by God and Elijah was to kindle more deeply Elisha's desire and earnestness, and thereby to prepare him for so great a gift as the double spirit of Elijah, and likewise that the prayers of Elijah himself on behalf of Elisha might increase, in order to obtain for him this spirit for the benefit of Elisha himself and of all Israel. For both were uncertain whether Elijah would be caught up while Elisha was present, so that he might become the heir and successor of that same spirit. So St. Chrysostom (Hom. 1 on Acts), who also adds that Elijah here prefigured Christ, who did not send the Spirit upon the Apostles immediately after the Resurrection, but willed first that they should see His Ascension into heaven, and then on the tenth day thereafter — namely, at the feast of Pentecost — sent the Holy Spirit upon them, so that through many days they might prepare themselves for Him. # GEM

This chariot was not truly fiery in its material substance — for had it been so, it would have burned Elijah — but it had the form and appearance of fire, as meteors formed in the air possess. For in truth these were Angels who, assuming the form of fire, were bearing Elijah upward, according to that text: *Who makest thy angels spirits, and thy ministers a flame of fire* (Ps. 103:4). This fire was therefore dense air, gleaming and red as fire, says Abulensis (q. 17), as do Serarius and others. This fiery chariot was formed by Angels out of the clouds, by which Elijah, as a triumphant conqueror and citizen of heaven, was caught up on high. For a fiery chariot befitted the fiery soul of Elijah, to carry him into heaven, says St. Chrysostom (Hom. 1 on Elijah). Likewise it befitted the office for which Elijah is being reserved — namely, to go before the second coming of Christ in judgment. I have reviewed many other literal, allegorical, tropological, and anagogical reasons for this chariot and rapture in my commentary on Ecclesiasticus 48:9, at the words: *Who wast taken up in a whirlwind of fire, in a chariot of fiery horses.* Moreover, Elijah by this fiery chariot triumphed not only over Ahab, Jezebel, and the idolaters, but also over death itself and over fire: for this was a chariot of triumph, as was the chariot of the Cherubim in Ezekiel 1. Our colleague Prado says there: It is fitting that thou, O Elijah, who in life didst command the fire from heaven, shouldst now be carried to heaven by fire as its commander and conqueror. Hence it is evident how ineptly Rabbi Kimchi claims that Elijah was consumed by fire in the sphere of fire. Hear St. Ambrose (Serm. 2, on Elisha): *Was not Elijah, the master of Elisha, caught up to heaven under the guidance of Angels, and set upon a fiery chariot, and did he not ascend as a victor in a kind of triumph? For he had been a conqueror not of barbarous nations, but of worldly pleasures. Since wicked habits are more grievous enemies than hostile foes, so that we may more easily understand that in this age the malice of enemies can be overcome more readily than the malice of morals.* **And Elijah went up by a whirlwind into heaven.** The Septuagint: *And Elijah was taken up in a commotion even unto heaven.* The Chaldean: *To the summit of the heavens.* In Ecclesiasticus 48:9 it is written: *Who wast received in a whirlwind of fire* — in Greek, *ἐν λαίλαπι*, that is, in a fiery tempest. It seems therefore that God sent forth lightning, flashes, and thunderbolts, and similar fiery storms, which caught up Elijah and conveyed him to heaven. Hence Vatablus at that same place translates: *Who wast lifted up in a fiery storm in a chariot of burning horses.* Thus Elijah, with a whirlwind or wind driving, a fiery chariot carrying, and an Angel as charioteer governing both, was conveyed into heaven — so say Abulensis, Vatablus, Serarius, Salianus, and others. Hear St. Ambrose (On the Apostles' Creed, near the end): *Enoch was translated that he should not see death; and likewise Elijah, while yet in this body, was taken up in a fiery chariot — that is, by the conveyance of Angels who are spirits and a flame of fire, who do the will of God — and was assumed in that commotion even unto heaven.* The author of the *Marvels of Scripture* (Bk. 2, ch. 22): *Now at last Elijah, received into the fiery chariot, is seen flying toward heaven, while Elisha watches, and is caught away.* Ambrose again (On Naboth, ch. 17): *When Elijah was being taken up, and was being borne in the chariot as toward heaven, Elisha cried out to him: Father, father, the chariot of Israel and the horseman thereof — that is, thou who didst govern the people of the Lord with good guidance; by the merit of thy constancy thou hast received these chariots, these horses running to divine things, because the Lord approved thee as a guide of human minds. Therefore, as a good charioteer of the contest, thou art crowned as victor with the eternal reward.* **Into heaven.** The Septuagint (v. 1) translates: *as it were into heaven.* From this, first, some consider that Elijah was truly caught up —

In the Hebrew: *The chariot of Israel and its horsemen.* The Septuagint: *And his horseman* — as if to say: Farewell, O Elijah, my father, who wast the whole strength of Israel, and didst benefit Israel more by thy zeal and prayer than a great multitude of chariots and horsemen could do. So Procopius, Vatablus, Cajetan, and others. Hence the Chaldean translates: *My master, my master, who wast mightier for Israel by thy prayer than chariots and horsemen.* So also King Joash, visiting the dying Elisha, cried out to him (ch. 13:14): *My father! my father! the chariot of Israel and the horseman thereof.* For such, after Elijah, was Elisha. Tropologically: Elijah — that is, a teacher and preacher correcting the morals of the people — is called a *chariot* because he bears the people by enduring; and a *charioteer* because he drives them by exhortation. Hear St. Gregory (Bk. 2 on Ezekiel, Hom. 21): *What does it mean that Elijah is called the chariot of Israel and its charioteer, unless it be that the charioteer drives and the chariot bears? Therefore the teacher who both sustains the morals of the people by patience and instructs them by the words of sacred eloquence is called both chariot and charioteer: a chariot, because he bears by enduring; a charioteer, because he drives by exhorting; a chariot, because he sustains evils; a charioteer, because he exercises the people with good admonitions.* Hence also St. Cyril says in his Collectanea: *The chariot signifies the lofty and illumined manner of life of Elijah.* St. Ambrose again (On Isaac, ch. 8): *The chariot is the holy soul; the charioteer and driver is Christ; the four horses are prudence, temperance, fortitude, and justice; these carry the soul to heaven. Prudence,* he says, *restrains the swifter horses; justice urges the more sluggish with its own whip; temperance makes them gentler, fortitude makes them more vigorous; it knows how to harness those that are at variance, lest perhaps they shatter their chariot. And so one may see, in this intelligible spectacle, each soul being carried to heaven with supreme effort — horses hastening, vying to arrive first at the prize of Christ, upon whose necks the palm may first be laid. These are horses subject to the yoke of faith, bound by the chain of charity, by the bridle of justice, by the reins of sobriety.* Politically: *the chariot of Israel and its charioteer* is the prince, who bears as a chariot the burdens of the commonwealth and the people, and directs as a charioteer the affairs of state; he steers and presides, and at the same time lifts and carries. The prince and prelate should therefore be as a mother who both governs and carries her infant. Hence the Hebrew word for prince is *נשא — nasi*, meaning *bearer* or *burden-carrier*, one who lightens the burdens of the people. Thus in Isaiah 9, it is said of Christ: *Whose government is upon his shoulder.* Morally, this signifies that if thou hast wholly subjected thyself to the divine rule, thou wilt easily obtain due obedience from those subject to thee. As Elijah departs to heaven in a whirlwind, Elisha cries out from the earth: *My father, my father! the chariot of Israel and the horseman thereof.* He calls him at once chariot and charioteer — a chariot, which is governed, and a charioteer, who governs — to show that he had fulfilled the duties of the best of princes: not only governing others wisely, but also readily allowing himself to be governed by God. Hence Lyra in his Moral Commentary on 1 Kings 9 says: *The chariot, which is led, is placed first; and the charioteer, who leads, second: to show that he governs well in ruling who was first docile in obeying.* One may ask in what year, month, and day Elijah was caught up into heaven. The answer is that he was caught up before the end of the reign of Jehoshaphat — for we have been hearing of his deeds until now, and shall hear again in the following chapter, where after the rapture of Elijah, Elisha prophesied in his presence. He seems therefore to have been caught up shortly after the death of King Ahaziah of Israel, which Elijah himself had foretold in chapter 1; for immediately after that death, his rapture in this second chapter is subjoined. Now Ahaziah died in the second year of his reign, which was the year —

That is, the mantle which Elijah, as he ascended, left behind for Elisha, as a memorial of himself and as a consolation and aid to Elisha. Abulensis judges this mantle to have been made of camel's hair, like the garment of St. John the Baptist. More probably, Serarius and others hold it to have been a melote, that is, a garment made from sheepskin. For the Septuagint renders it: *And he took the melote of Elijah, which had fallen upon him.* AND THEY WERE NOT DIVIDED.] So also the Septuagint. These words, however, are absent from the Hebrew and Chaldaic, but are understood by implication: for Elisha struck the waters a second time precisely because they had not been divided at the first stroke. God was unwilling that the waters should divide at the first blow, lest the new prophet Elisha, having performed the miracle, should glory in his own new spirit; but rather that through failure he might have recourse to God. Likewise, that he might know that this dividing of the waters, and the other miracles to follow, were obtained not by his own merits but by those of Elijah. So Abulensis and Cajetan. WHERE IS THE GOD OF ELIJAH, EVEN NOW?] In Hebrew and Septuagint: *even he himself* — meaning, he who is always the same, faithful, almighty, and hearing his servants who call upon him. Not that he supposed God to be absent, but because he was seeking the manifestation of his presence in benefits, as St. Ambrose says in his commentary on Psalm 37. Hence the Chaldaic renders it: *Receive my petition, O Lord God of Elijah, even thou thyself.* This is the first miracle of Elisha, namely, that by striking the Jordan with Elijah's mantle, he divided it and crossed over on dry foot. Note: for *even he himself*, the Hebrew is אַפִּי הוּא (*aphi ha*), a Hebrew word retained by the Septuagint, who render it ἀφφώ — which Theodoret wrongly takes as a Greek word and translates as *hidden* — as though it were a proper name of God. For this is indeed a name of God: *He himself it is who made us, he himself made heaven and earth, he himself is the Lord our God*; for הוּא (*hu*) is derived from הָיָה, and thus it is the same as Jehovah, or ὁ ὤν, that is, *he who is*, as in Exodus 3, verse 6.

Because this land is situated near pestilential waters; whence in Hebrew and Chaldaic it reads: *a land that bereaves*, or *the land bereaves* or *destroys*, namely, the seeds cast into it, by reason of its bitterness or rottenness. Wherefore, when the waters were healed by Elisha, the land likewise was healed and made fruitful at the same time: for otherwise the territory of Jericho was fertile and rich; it was only the brackish water that harmed the nearby land and rendered it barren. Moreover, Aristotle in his *Politics* teaches that in founding cities and houses two things above all must be observed: namely, whether the air is wholesome and the water healthful; for water, taken in drinking, and air, drawn in breathing, penetrate to the innermost parts of man, and accordingly affect him well or ill, and produce health or sickness and death.

The newness of the vessel signified that through the prayers and merits of Elisha there would henceforth be a renewal of the waters and the land, so that from barren they would become fruitful. It therefore appears that Elisha immersed the whole vessel together with the salt into the spring, as Josephus, St. Ambrose, Bede, Angelomus, and Eucherius assert.

For it seems that these waters, if not brackish, were certainly foul and fetid, and therefore he fittingly applied salt — though by miracle he purified the waters alone, and preserved them purified for so long, as Francisco Valles states in chapter 3 of his *Sacred Philosophy*. The Church imitates this in the blessing of salt and water, when holy water is blessed, with a prayer that the water so blessed may be the health of mind and body. So Abulensis. THE SPRING.] From this it is clear that not all the waters of Jericho were bad, but only those of this particular spring, which Josephus describes at length and embellishes with many additions after his custom of amplifying and accommodating to Roman ears. Hear him in Book 5 of *The Jewish War*, chapter 4: *They say this spring was once wont not only to blight the fruits of the earth and trees, but also to hinder the birth of women, and to corrupt all things alike with disease and pestilence; but that afterwards it was tamed and made most healthful and most fruitful by Elisha the prophet of old, who had been the disciple and successor of Elijah. For having been received as a guest by the inhabitants of Jericho, because he had found them to be of a kindly disposition, he rewarded them and all that region with perpetual gratitude: and proceeding to the spring, he cast into the flowing water an earthen vessel full of salt. Then stretching his right hand toward heaven and pouring gentle libations into the spring, he prayed to it that it might calm its streams and open sweeter veins of water; and he besought God that he might temper the river with more fruitful breezes, and grant to the inhabitants both abundance of fruits and succession of offspring, and that the water, mother of children, might not fail them, so long as they remained just. With these prayers, having also wrought much with disciplined hands, he changed the spring: and that which before was the cause of their bereavement and famine, the same became the author of their sustenance and fruitfulness.* And after several intervening passages: *It produces the finest and most luxuriant gardens, and varieties of well-watered palms, differing both in taste and in name; of which the richest, when pressed underfoot, yield most abundantly of honey not much inferior to other honey: and yet that region also nourishes bees and produces balsam, which is the most costly of all the fruits that grow there; and likewise cyprus and myrobalanum: so that he who calls that tract divine will not err, where both abundantly and excellently are produced things of greatest value. Nor in any other fruits could any region in the whole world easily rival it: so greatly multiplied does it render what is sown. The reason for this I believe to be the hidden power of the waters and the warmth of the air.* Here Josephus, in a manner ridiculous if not impious, asserts that Elisha prayed to the spring and to heaven that it might be calmed and become sweet — plainly wishing to accommodate himself to the Roman Gentiles, who superstitiously prayed to springs and to heaven, and indeed sometimes adored them as divinities. Moreover, what he says about these waters making women sterile he deduces from the Hebrew text, which has the land described as מְשַׁכֶּלֶת (*meschaccheleth*), that is, *bereaving*, which is said directly of the fruits of the earth. Josephus, however, extends this to women; for women are properly called bereaved when they are robbed of their children. Following Josephus, St. Ambrose in Sermon 2 on Elisha says: *See how great are the merits of Elisha, whose first reception in the city with hospitality is followed by great fruitfulness of children. For while he drives away the sterility of the waters, he bestows succession of heirs.* He therefore holds, together with Josephus, that Elisha gave fruitfulness not only to the land but also to barren women. Ambrose continues: *For by this deed Elisha did not heal one man, nor furnish remedy for one household, but for the whole city...*

where Jeroboam the king had set up one of his golden calves as an idol, so that he might fight against it. SMALL BOYS CAME OUT FROM THE CITY AND MOCKED HIM, SAYING: GO UP, BALDHEAD. Baldness is the loss of hair from the head, and therefore a deformity, just as abundant and flowing hair is its beauty and, as it were, its crown; especially among the Syrians and Palestinians, where, as in a region hot and dry, nearly all men are long-haired and have thick and coarse hair. Hence Ovid writes: "A shorn flock is unseemly, unseemly a field without grass, and a shrub without foliage, and a head without hair." And Aristotle, in Book 5 of On the Generation of Animals, asserts that baldness in a man is the same as the loss of feathers in birds, or the falling of leaves from trees. For this reason the Romans of old used to shave the heads of slaves; and the Emperor Caligula, as Suetonius records in his Life, chapter 35, whenever he encountered handsome and long-haired youths, would disfigure them in mockery by shaving the backs of their heads. On the other hand, how great an adornment flowing hair is to the head, St. Ambrose shows in Book 6 of the Hexameron, chapter 9: "How pleasing and agreeable is the very crown of the head," he says; "how beautiful the flowing hair, how venerable in old men, how worthy of reverence in priests, how terrible in warriors, how comely in young men, how elegant in women, how sweet in children!" He then adds what Aristotle likewise observed: "From trees one may estimate what grace belongs to the human head. In the crown of the tree is all its fruit; there is all its beauty: take away the foliage from a tree, and the whole tree becomes ungracious." This reproach and insult of the boys against Elisha, then — Go up, baldhead — is partly physical and partly ethical. It is physical, because they taunt him with a defect of nature, namely baldness; it is ethical, because they tacitly reproach him with lasciviousness. For bald men are by many accounted given to lust. Hence that passage of Juvenal, Satire 4, concerning the lustful and cruel Emperor Domitian: "When the last of the Flavians was lacerating a half-dead world, and Rome was enslaved to a bald Nero." And that saying of Suetonius concerning Julius Caesar: "Citizens, guard your wives; we bring you the bald adulterer." Indeed, Pliny and Aristotle assign lust as the proper cause of baldness. For Pliny, in Book 11, chapter 37, says thus: "Loss of hair is rare in women, never seen in eunuchs, nor in any man before the use of Venus." The same author, however, says in the same place: "Certain animals are bald by nature, as the ostrich, the camel, and certain aquatic birds." Aristotle, in Book 5 of On the Generation of Animals, chapter 3, says: "No one grows bald before he begins to have intercourse; but it comes rather from venereal intercourse, and that especially in those who by their nature are more inclined thereto." He adds the reason: "Since the brain is the coldest of all the parts of the body, the use of venereal intercourse cools it further, whereby the warmth needed by the brain to produce hair upon the head is diminished, and so baldness results." And shortly after: "Those whose seed is fervent grow bald at the very age at which they begin to emit seed." But these authors are to be interpreted as meaning that baldness arises in many from excessive venery, though not in all; and again, that those who are inclined to lust grow bald sooner than others; and furthermore, that no one grows bald before he is capable of emitting seed, that is, before puberty. Thus Cardanus explains Aristotle, in Book 4 On the Preservation of Health, chapter 15. For that chaste men also grow bald is plain from experience, as Elisha was bald, whom nonetheless St. Ignatius, in his Letter to the Philadelphians, numbers among the virgins. For addressing virgins he says: "Virgins, keep Christ alone before your eyes, and His Father in your souls, enlightened by the Holy Spirit. I am mindful of your holiness, as of Elijah, as of Joshua son of Nun, as of Melchizedek, as of Elisha—"

that is, he called down upon them from God a just punishment and vengeance. Elisha did this out of a righteous zeal for religion: first, because when the servants and prophets of God are mocked, God Himself is mocked. So St. Thomas, Summa Theologica II-II, question 108, article 1, ad 4. Second, he wished to punish the parents more than the boys; for the idolatrous parents had instilled into the boys their own idolatry and contempt for the true God and His prophets. For as St. Augustine says in Sermon 204 On the Seasons: "The insolent boys must be believed to have done what they did at the instigation of their parents. For they would not have cried out thus, if it had been displeasing to their parents." And St. Justin, in the question cited above (80): "God," he says, "chastised the parents by the destruction of the boys, and at the same time warned posterity to educate their sons in the true worship of God, in modesty and uprightness, lest they be similarly punished." Hence St. Gregory, in Book 4 of the Dialogues, chapter 18, relates how a certain small child, accustomed to blaspheme, was snatched away by demons from his father's lap: "So that," he says, "his father might acknowledge his own guilt, who, by neglecting the soul of his little son, had nourished a small sinner for the fires of hell." The author of the Wonders of Sacred Scripture, Book 2, chapter 22, adds that perhaps these boys had been consecrated by their parents to the golden calf and to idols. Third, Elisha served the boys' own good; for he brought it about that they should not become worse through going unpunished, as Theodoret says in Question 13. For had they lived longer, they would have become more wicked and more deeply immersed in the idolatry of their parents' teaching and example, and thus worthy of heavier condemnation. AND THERE CAME FORTH TWO BEARS OUT OF THE WOOD, AND TORE FORTY-TWO OF THE BOYS. God fittingly sent bears to devour the boys before the very eyes of their fathers, so that those whom they had neglected to imitate in forming their children might experience them as all the more savage in the slaughter of those same children. For He shows that these parents, who had been negligent — nay, impious — in forming their sons, were worse than bears. For bears, shapeless as their cubs are, form and fashion them by licking, as Pliny witnesses in Book 8, chapter 36, and Book 10, chapter 63. Hence St. Ambrose says: "The bear fashions her offspring by licking them into her own likeness; and you cannot instruct your sons to be like yourself?" Allegorically, Abulensis, Serarius, and others in various places say: Elisha ascending the mountain is Christ ascending the mount of Calvary and there crucified; the boys mocked him, that is, the Jews, saying: "If thou art Christ the Son of God, come down from the cross." Christ cursed them by ordaining as their punishment the destruction of Jerusalem, so that forty years after his ascent to Bethel — that is, to the house of God, namely to heaven — two bears should come from the forest of the Gentiles, namely Titus and Vespasian, who should tear them and destroy them. So Angelomus, Eucherius, Bede, Rupert, and St. Jerome in the title of Psalm 44. Elisha, he says, represents the figure of Christ; the boys represent the Jews who said: "Hail, King of the Jews"; the bears are properly understood as demons, because they devoured the hearts of those men; and therefore that Psalm is entitled "For the sons of Core," who are the sons of the Bald One. And St. Augustine, in Book 12 Against Faustus, chapter 35, says: "The beasts devour the boys who insult Elisha and cry out, 'Baldhead, baldhead'; the foolish little ones who mock Christ crucified on the place of Calvary are overwhelmed by demons and perish." Tropologically, Origen in his Homily on Ezekiel understands by the two bears the demons and the beasts of vice, which are sent upon the sinful soul. Finally, the arch-heretic Marcion, seeking to prove that Moses was contrary to Christ and the Old Testament to the New, and that the former was produced by an evil and cruel God, the latter by a good and benign one, argued: "Christ commands us to be as little children; but that God of the Old Testament sent bears against the little children who mocked Elisha."

You will say: This verse appears to conflict with verse 17 of chapter 1, where it is said that Joram king of Israel began to reign in the second year of Joram son of Jehoshaphat, who certainly reigned after his father Jehoshaphat. How then is it said here that he began to reign in the eighteenth year of Jehoshaphat? The answer is that Joram began to reign in Judah together with his father Jehoshaphat in the seventeenth year of his reign; wherefore the eighteenth year of Jehoshaphat was the second year of the reign of his son Joram, in which year Joram son of Ahab began to reign in Israel. For Jehoshaphat, setting out to war with Ahab against the Syrians, as was related in the final chapter of the third book, appointed his son Joram as his substitute and established him as king in Judah — both so that the kingdom should not be left without a king during his absence, and lest it be without a successor should it befall him to die in battle, as often happens. So say Abulensis, Cajetan, Salian, and others. Abulensis here debates in question 21 whether it is fitting for religious men, such as Elisha was, to frequent the courts of princes, and he answers that it is fitting, provided they are of great and solid prudence and virtue, as were Elijah and Elisha, whom God sent to kings to instruct them in the worship of God, to rebuke their vices, and to offer sound counsel. Thus Plutarch wrote a small work with this title: *That the Philosopher Ought Especially to Converse with Men of Power*. Hence Pericles made use of Anaxagoras, Dionysius the Tyrant of Sicily of Plato, the princes of Italy of Pythagoras, Cato of Athenodorus, Scipio of Panaetius. Moreover, it is highly expedient that religious men of proven virtue be sent especially to military camps, so that they may instruct and restrain soldiers and administer the sacraments; whence many soldiers are saved through them who would otherwise be damned, as is evident from the military missions in Belgium, Germany, Italy, France, and elsewhere. Add to this that the examples of holiness, the prayers, and the sacrifices of religious men benefit armies greatly, just as Elisha was of benefit here. Yet those religious men who seek out courts for the sake of honor, liberty, or vanity ruin both the courts and themselves, and create great difficulties for the superiors of their religious orders — so much so that it would be better for them to be laymen than religious, as experience teaches.
That is, he removed and took away from Samaria and the kingdom of Israel the idols and the worship of Baal, because he had seen his father Ahab slain in battle on account of his worship of that god.

By worshipping the golden calves erected by Jeroboam, and by driving the people to their worship, lest the people defect to Jehoshaphat king of Judah, should they go to Jerusalem, his capital city, to worship the true God in the Temple. Great was the perversity of judgment, the blindness, and the obstinacy of the kings of Israel in maintaining these idols of the calves — and therefore they all perished and were cut off.

That is, the king of Moab rebelled — as the Chaldean renders it — against Joram king of Israel, and refused him the accustomed tribute, because he despised him on account of his youth and on account of his father Ahab having been slain in battle.

This was an imprudent response and counsel. For the desert is an arid place, where water is lacking for such great forces as were gathered in the three armies — namely, of the king of Judah, the king of Israel, and the king of Edom — wherefore the soldiers soon labored under thirst and grew faint, as follows. God permitted this in order to punish Joram, king of Israel, for his idolatry, and also to prepare the way for the miracle of abundant waters to be given through Himself and Elisha; so that from this the king might acknowledge Him to be the true God, and renounce the idols of the calves.
That is, one who served Elijah as a minister: for it is the office of ministers to pour water over the hands of their masters when they wash them in the morning or before a meal.

As if to say: What fellowship or communion have I with you? For you worship the golden calves, whereas I worship the true God. Go therefore to the prophets of your father — that is, the priests and worshippers of the calves, whom you have hitherto taken into your counsel: consult them again now, since they will not be able to give you oracle or counsel in matters that are by nature desperate; hence in such straits you flee to me and to the true God, whom until now you have despised. Admirable was this freedom of speech and of rebuking the king in Elisha, worthy of so great a prophet, and to be imitated by apostolic men when they are summoned by heretical princes for temporal advantage. See on this matter the examples of Moses, Elijah, Paul, and others, which St. Gregory recounts in Book 7 of the Moralia, ch. 15. "As the Lord lives" — that is, I swear by the Lord — "in whose sight I stand" (whom I always hold present in my mind, revere, worship, and love, even as my master Elijah, from whom I learned this) — "were it not that I regard the presence of Jehoshaphat king of Judah" (who worships the true God together with me) "I would not look toward you, nor regard you" — but would despise you, and not deign to answer you, much less to give you an oracle — because you neglect and despise my God. For he who holds God as ever-present fears or heeds no man's presence. Wherefore Sextus the Philosopher, in his Sentences (which circulate under the falsely attributed name of Pope Sixtus), no. 293, says: "In all that you do, call upon God as witness." And no. 278: "Take your beginning in action from God; and hold God before your mouth and mind before you draw breath." And no. 277: "The souls of the wise are insatiable in the love of God." And no. 304: "The wise man is a partaker of God." And no. 450: "The mind of the wise is a mirror of God." And no. 368: "He honors God best who makes his mind, so far as he is able, like unto God." # GEM

One who strikes a musical instrument with his hand — for this is what the Hebrew word מְנַגֵּן (menaggen) signifies — and accordingly the Chaldean renders it: "Bring me a man who knows how to play the harp." Elisha commanded the musician to play, so that by the sweet sound and harmony of music he might collect and calm his spirit, which had been somewhat stirred by indignation against the king of Israel, raise it toward God in prayer, and dispose it to receive prophecy from God. For this musician was a Levite, says Theodoret, accustomed to sing the devout Psalms composed by David. So likewise Rupert, Tostatus, and others. Hear St. Gregory, Homily 1 on Ezekiel: "When Jehoshaphat inquired of him concerning things to come, and the spirit of prophecy was absent from him, he caused a musician to be brought near, so that through the praise of psalmody the prophetic spirit might descend upon him and fill his mind with knowledge of things to come. For the voice of psalmody, when it proceeds from the earnest intention of the heart, thereby prepares a way through that same intention to the heart of the almighty Lord, that He may pour into the attentive mind either the mysteries of prophecy or the grace of compunction." Thus St. Francis, upon hearing the harmony of a lyre, was raised in mind to God. St. Augustine confesses the same of himself in the Confessions, when he heard the singing of psalms. "AND WHEN THE MUSICIAN PLAYED, THE HAND OF THE LORD CAME UPON HIM" — that is, the operation and illumination of the Lord — as if to say: while the musician played and the music resounded, God illumined the mind of Elisha, revealed to him things to come, and promised him water and victory. Thus frequently concerning Ezekiel and other prophets, when they prophesy, it is said: "The hand of the Lord came upon him" — that is, God imparted to him the prophetic light.
As if to say: Make many and deep trenches in the bed of this torrent, so that in them you may receive the abundance of waters which God is about to give you.

That we may understand, says Cajetan, that the virtue of the morning sacrifice, which Jehoshaphat continually offered to God in the temple according to the law, cooperated toward this miracle. AND BEHOLD, THE WATERS (brought by Angels) CAME BY THE WAY OF EDOM, into the camp of Jehoshaphat and of the allied kings, that they might quench their thirst from them.

# GEM By the reflection of the rays of the morning sun, which, on account of the nocturnal vapors through which they shone, appeared red — just as those same rays appear red when they pass through red glass, and likewise appear red to us when they are reflected back to the eye from a cloud or from the earth. For this is an optical principle, as is evident from optics and from daily experience. Hence the Hebrew has: *The sun shone upon the waters, and they saw the waters red.* And Josephus says: *The waters appeared red, because at that time* (in the morning) *water struck by the rays of the sun shines with a ruddy glow.* The waters, therefore, were not truly red, but appeared red on account of the reflection of the rays. So say Abulensis and others. Now, deceived by this redness of the waters, the Moabites supposed them to be stained red with the blood of enemies fighting among themselves, and therefore rushing upon them, were slain by them to utter destruction.

The Hebrew has: *Until they had cast down its tiles into the wall of Kir-hareseth* — that is, of the earthen or brick city, namely one built of bricks. So the Septuagint, Pagninus, and others. Secondly, the Rabbis and Vatablus translate: *They left only the stones of it in Kir-hareseth* — as though Kir-hareseth were the chief city of Moab, which had walls of brick, and to which, after all the other places had been laid waste by the Hebrews, the king of Moab had fled with his people; whence the text immediately adds: *And the city was surrounded by slingers.* Both versions, therefore, are in harmony; both are true, as I said on Isaiah 16:7 and Jeremiah 48:31.

— that the propitiatory gods might, by so beloved and distinguished a holocaust, protect the wall; and that he might move the Israelites besieging the wall to compassion, when they saw the king reduced to such extremity that he would sacrifice his own firstborn son, and so might cease to besiege and pursue him, and leaving him behind, return home — as indeed they did. That the kings of Phoenicia were accustomed to do this same thing in extreme straits, namely to sacrifice to their god the thing most dear to them, that is, their son, is recorded by Porphyry, as cited by Eusebius, book 1 of the *Praeparatio Evangelica*, chapter 7. The Phoenicians, he says, when they labored under severe distress, whether from war, famine, or even pestilence, would sacrifice to Saturn some one of their nearest kindred, chosen by common vote — of which sort of examples the history of the Phoenicians, which Sanchoniatho wrote in the Phoenician tongue, is full, etc. So likewise Joseph., Theodor., Procop., Abulens., and others. Moreover, R. Solomon, and with him certain others, rendering the Hebrew as "his son," spin the fable that the king in question was not the king of the Moabites but of the Edomites: for God, he says, had seized the son of the king of Edom as he burst forth from the city with seven hundred men, and led him back into the city as a captive; wherefore the king of Edom, in order to free his son, abandoned the siege, and departed thence, indignant at the king of Israel for pressing the siege more stubbornly against him; but as he departed, the king of Moab slaughtered and sacrificed his captive son upon the wall. But this has no semblance of verisimilitude. AND THERE WAS GREAT INDIGNATION IN ISRAEL, AND STRAIGHTWAY THEY DEPARTED FROM HIM — that is to say, the Israelites were indignant among themselves, because by their own practice of sacrificing sons to the idol Moloch they had themselves given the Moabites the example of doing the same. So also Lyran. understands the indignation to be God's, not that of the Israelites. For he says that God was indignant with the Hebrews, because an Angel presented before Him the sins of the Hebrews and showed them to be graver than the crimes of the Moabites; and therefore God sent a pestilence into the camp of the Hebrews, so that they were compelled to abandon the siege of the city of Moab. Thirdly, Cajetan, and following him Pererius in chapter 22 of Genesis, question 105, hold that the indignation belongs to the Israelites, who were indignant on account of so cruel and unworthy a deed — namely, that the king of Moab had sacrificed his own son — and therefore took pity on him, seeing that he had been reduced to such necessity and misery as to immolate his own son. So Josephus: the kings, he says, seeing this, took pity on so great a necessity, and mindful of the vicissitudes of human fortune, raised the siege and each returned to his own home. Theodoret follows him: when the Moabites were being utterly destroyed, their king attempted to flee; but when a strong and vigorous cohort prevented him, he offered his firstborn son and heir to the kingdom as a holocaust upon a certain tower. When the multitude of enemies beheld this, they pitied that calamity and raised the siege. So also Abulens., Serar., Vatabl., Salian., and others. For this reason the Moabites afterwards exhumed the bones of the king of Edom and burned them — because he himself had driven their king to the immolation of his son — as Amos says in chapter 2, verse 1. See the commentary there. Now hear the opinions of others. First, R. Solomon, and following him Lyranus, think that the king of Moab, in such great necessity, invoked the true God and sacrificed to Him, because neither men nor gods could deliver him from that peril, and that he was moved to this by the example of Abraham, who, wishing to immolate his son Isaac to God, wonderfully pleased Him and received great gifts from Him. But Abraham did this at God's command, without which it would have been unlawful — indeed, homicide and parricide. So Abulensis and Burgensis. Add to this: the king of Moab did not worship the true God, but idols, that is, the demon who ruled over him…

Josephus (lib. 9, c. 2), whom the Hebrews, Theodoret, Lyra, Abulensis, and others follow, asserts that this woman was the wife of Obadiah, the steward of Ahab (3 Kings 18:3), and that she obtained this benefit and miracle of the multiplied oil from Elisha on account of the merits and almsdeeds of her husband, who during the persecution of Jezebel had hidden and provided for a hundred prophets of the Lord. For God repays liberality with liberality, and is supremely merciful toward the merciful, according to that saying: *Blessed are the merciful, for they shall obtain mercy* (Matt. 5).

The Hebrews relate that this creditor was Joram, king of Israel, who had given money to Obadiah, which he had spent in secretly feeding the hundred prophets, and that this money was now being reclaimed from his widow by the king. But Abulensis refutes this (q. 11). *That he may take my two sons to serve him.*] It was customary among the Hebrews to sell their sons or carry off servants in payment of a parent's debts, as is evident from Matthew 18:25 and Isaiah 50:1. The Romans did the same thing, as Dionysius of Halicarnassus attests (lib. 2), and the Athenians likewise, as Plutarch records in his Life of Solon; indeed even Christians did so, as is apparent from law 2, concerning fathers who have sold their children, lib. 4, Codex, tit. 43. That this was done in his own time St. Ambrose bitterly laments and deplores in his book *On Job*, ch. 8. *Save only a little oil with which to anoint myself.*] So also the Septuagint. The Hebrew has: *save only a flask of oil.* Our version and the Septuagint add: *with which to anoint myself* — that is, with which I may anoint myself but once, to signify that the vessel is small and the oil little. For the proper use of oil is for anointing, which among the Hebrews was customary and frequent. Others, following Sanchez, take *with which to anoint myself* to mean: immediately after death, which threatens me on account of hunger. For it was the custom of the Hebrews, as of the Gentiles, to anoint the dead before burial.

For I will bring it about that all the vessels shall be filled; and the more empty ones you bring, the more you shall receive filled with oil. *Tropologically*, it is signified that one must be emptied of earthly desires, so that God may fill that emptiness with the oil of His grace. Hear St. Bernard, sermon 6 on the Ascension: *Whence is it that some suffer so great a poverty of spiritual grace, while it overflows in such abundance in others? Surely the distributor of grace is neither miserly nor lacking; but where empty vessels are wanting, the oil must needs stand still. On every side the love of the world presses in with its consolations — or rather its desolations — watches the entrances, rushes in through the windows, takes possession of the mind; but not of him who said: My soul refused to be comforted; I remembered God and was delighted. For a soul already preoccupied with worldly desires is turned away from holy delight; nor can true things be mingled with vain, eternal with passing, spiritual with bodily, highest with lowest, so that you may equally savor the things that are above and the things that are upon the earth.* St. Gregory, however, in homily 3 on Ezekiel, takes the oil to signify doctrine, which is poured through teachers into the empty minds of men. For, as he says, *while from the mouth of a single teacher some small spark of love for the divine is heard by many empty minds, and grace overflows, they are filled to the very brim with the ointment of divine love.* Also many hearts now, which before were empty little vessels, are filled with the ointment of the Spirit — hearts that seemed, from the scantiness of the oil, to have received only a little. Whence St. Augustine, sermon 206 *On the Seasons*, teaches that the apostolic man must seek out empty vessels — that is, men in need of instruction — into whom he may pour the oil of doctrine; for in this way it will grow as the number of men grows. *And shut the door behind you.*] This is: first, lest anyone coming upon you disturb, delay, or halt your pouring of oil into the vessels; second, that this miracle and benefit may be secret, and that you may see God alone working it, with no man cooperating with Him; third, that you alone may enjoy the oil, lest anyone demand or take some part of it. *Mystically*, St. Augustine, sermon 206 *On the Seasons*: It is signified, he says, that each person should give alms with the door closed — that is, for the love of God alone, not in order to be praised by men, but so as to deserve to find favor with God. For he who gives alms for human praise gives with the door open, because what is open is plain to all. But he who does good works solely for eternal life and the remission of sins, even if he does so publicly, has given with the door closed; because from that almsgiving he seeks not what is seen, but what is not seen: for human praise is seen, but the eternal reward is not seen.

# GEM As it was poured, therefore, the oil increased. For the angels continually supplied and poured fresh oil from the flask, until all the vessels were filled; and when this was done, the oil ceased, the angel withdrawing himself. *Allegorically*, St. Augustine, sermon 206 *On the Seasons*: In the oil, he says, mercy is understood. Therefore the debt of that widow had grown because she had lost the oil of mercy through sin. Consider, brethren: the oil failed, and the debt grew. The oil grew, and the debt perished. Covetousness had grown, and charity perished; charity returns, and iniquity perishes. When Elisha — that is, Christ the Lord — draws near to the widow, that is, to the Church, through the increase of oil — that is, the gift of grace and mercy, or the richness of charity — she is freed from the debt of sins. *Tropologically*, the same St. Augustine in the same place takes the oil to signify charity, which by being given away is not diminished but always increases by its own diffusion. Consider, he says, dearest brethren, how long as that widow kept the oil in her own little vessel, it neither sufficed for herself nor could she pay the debt. It is true, most beloved: he who loves himself alone neither suffices for himself nor pays the debt of his sins. But when she began to pour the oil of charity into other vessels — that is, into all her neighbors and kinsfolk, and into all men without exception — then she was able both to suffice for herself and to free herself from all debts. And truly, brethren, such is the nature of holy love and true charity, that by being given away it grows, and the more it is spent upon others, the more abundantly it accumulates within oneself. He confirms the same from a contrast between food and oil, or charity: If you wish to give bodily food to someone in need, at that moment you cannot yourself possess what you have given to him. But if you give the bread of charity to a hundred men, it remains whole. If you wish to bestow it upon the whole world, nothing is lacking to you; nay, not only is nothing lacking, but the profit of all those upon whom you have bestowed it multiplies and grows for you. For example: you had one loaf of charity; had you given it to no one, you would have that one loaf alone. You gave it to a thousand men; you have acquired a thousand loaves. He then adds the reason from first principles: Because so great is the possession of charity that it is wholly present to each one, and can remain whole for all. Therefore you have given to others, and you yourself have lost nothing whatsoever; nay, not only have you not lost, but, as I have already said, whatever has been bestowed upon others from you, you have acquired a hundredfold. Whereupon he adds that men must be sought out who are in need of bodily or spiritual bread, so that by showing them charity we may cause it to grow continually both in ourselves and in them. These same thoughts have been drawn from St. Augustine and transcribed by Eucherius, Angelomus, Bede, Rupert, and others.

She was distinguished both in wealth and reputation — that is, prosperous, honored, and celebrated — and likewise in uprightness and piety. Hence the Chaldean version renders it: *There was a woman fearing sins*. Rabbi Solomon fables that this Shunammite woman was the sister of Abishag the Shunammite, who was given as a wife to the aged David to warm him (3 Kings 1); for from the death of David to the time of Elisha, more than eighty years had elapsed. Wherefore this woman would have had to exceed the hundredth year of her age.

She discerned this from Elisha's modesty, abstinence, gravity, speech, assiduous prayer, and other heroic virtues. Hence Rabbi Solomon fables that she came to know it from the fact that, on the table which she had as often as she spread it for Elisha, never had a fly settled upon it, nor had anything unclean adhered to it.

The Hebrew and Chaldean read: *A little chamber of the wall* — that is, built of solid masonry and wall, and not of light planks — or, as Vatablus explains, *of the wall*, meaning enclosed and walled off, and separated from the other parts of the house, so that Elisha might dwell there alone in quiet and seclusion, might pray and meditate. *And let us place therein for him a little bed, and a table, and a chair, and a candlestick* — that is, only what is strictly necessary; for she knew that Elisha, being devoted to poverty, would not admit cushions, tapestries, and other ornaments as superfluous things.

The sense is: I live peaceably among my fellow citizens and neighbors; I love all, and am loved by all. Therefore I have no quarrel with anyone, such that I should need to implore the help of the king or of a prince on account of it. Indeed, if I stood in such need, my fellow citizens would assist me and would intercede with the king on my behalf; for I am known and dear to all.

Do not lie to your handmaid. — The word *lie* is used here not formally, but materially: for she knew that Elisha, being a holy man, would not wish to deceive her or speak contrary to his mind by falsely promising her a son. Rather, she means it materially, as if to say: Would that your word prove true and not false in the event! Would that your word be not merely a kindly wish, but also an efficacious prophecy, so that in very deed a son might be born to me in the coming year!

By way of application, hear from the *Lives of the Fathers*, Book 6, Chapter 1, on contemplation: "A certain elder once said: The Shunammite received Elisha because she had no attachment to any other man. The Shunammite is said to represent the soul, Elisha the Holy Spirit: therefore, in whatever hour the soul withdraws from worldly confusion and disturbance, the Spirit of God will come upon it, and then it will be able to conceive, though it be barren." **Verse 23. Why are you going to him today? It is neither the new moon** (that is, the Neomenia, which was the first day of the moon and of the month, and therefore kept as a kind of feast day among the Hebrews) **nor the Sabbath.** Hence it is clear, as the Abulensis notes in Question 30, and Vatable, Serarius, and others observe, that the Hebrews were accustomed on feast days to visit Elisha and other holy prophets, in order to receive their exhortations and pious counsel, and to seek their prayers and blessing. **And she answered: I will go** — as if to say: I utterly desire, wish, and resolve to go to Elisha. She suppresses the reason, lest her husband, upon hearing that their only son was dead, be overwhelmed with grief. **Verse 24. Saddle** — Drive the ass, and carry me on the ass to Elisha.

It is well — though my son is dead, to my great sorrow, yet I know he has died by the will and decree of God, which is most righteous and most holy. Or perhaps she says this because matters will shortly be set right for her: for she was fully confident that he would be raised from the dead by Elisha. In this manner Christ spoke of the dead girl: *She is not dead, but sleepeth* (Luke 8), because, namely, I am about to raise her from death, so that she seems not so much to have died as to be sleeping. The Shunammite, moreover, offered this pretext before Gehazi, in order that through him she might gain access to Elisha; for Elisha, being a virgin and chaste, and avoiding the company of women, if he had heard that her son was already dead, would have sent his servant Gehazi to raise him — as indeed he afterwards did in very fact.
For Gehazi knew the humility, religious devotion, and chastity of Elisha, who would not wish to be touched and honored by this contact with his feet, and as it were adored by a woman.

From this passage we learn that Elisha carried a staff, and Cassian teaches that ancient monks carried one for this reason, in book 1 of the *Institutes*, chapter 9. He adds a mystical significance to it, saying: The carrying of it spiritually admonishes them that they ought never, among so many barking dogs of the vices and the invisible beasts of spiritual wickedness, to go forth unarmed — concerning which the Blessed David, seeking deliverance, says: *Give not over, O Lord, the soul that confesseth Thee to the beasts* — but rather to beat back those that rush upon them with the seal of the cross, and to drive them further away, and to extinguish those that rage against them by the constant memory of the Lord's Passion and by the imitation of that mortification. *If thou meet any man, salute him not.* The first reason was lest Gehazi, by greeting those he met along the way and conversing with them, should cause delays on the road, but should straightway restore the boy by the application of the staff, before the husband could learn that he was dead — as St. Augustine says, in sermon 106 *de Tempore*: Walk so swiftly that you presume not to be detained or delayed by any idle talk. The second reason was that the matter might remain secret. The third, because Gehazi, as Theodoret and Procopius say, was greedy of praise, and would therefore have boasted to those he met, saying: *My master Elisha has sent me to raise the dead, and so I am going to him for this purpose* — by which vainglory he would have offended God, and thus impeded the miracle, so that God would not have raised the boy through him. The fourth reason was that Gehazi might avoid all distraction of eyes and ears, and dispose himself wholly through prayer for the accomplishment of so great a miracle. So Cajetan, Abulensis, and St. Ambrose in chapter 10 of Luke, book 7, on those words *Salute no man by the way*: For He commanded him, says Ambrose, to hasten with speed, that he might execute the office of the resurrection to be celebrated, lest, through conversation with someone he met, he should be turned aside from the duty enjoined. Therefore here too the duty of salutation is not abolished, but the obstacle that hinders devoted attention is removed: so that, when divine commands are given, human concerns may for a time be set aside.

St. Augustine gives an allegorical meaning in sermon 106 *de Tempore*, saying: This boy (Gehazi) bore the type of the blessed Moses. For God sent him with the staff into Egypt; but the staff without Christ could scourge Egypt, yet could not free it from original or actual sin, nor could it raise the dead. For, as the Apostle says, *the law brought nothing to perfection*. It was necessary that He who had sent the staff should Himself come down. The staff without Elisha was of no avail, because the cross without Christ could accomplish nothing. St. Peter, by a staff sent by him, raised from death St. Maternus, his disciple and the first Bishop of Trier, as his life records; and St. Macedonius by his staff restored the nearly lifeless Theodoret, as Theodoret himself relates in the *Philotheus*, chapter 3. *The boy did not rise.* Why? Rabbi Solomon, as cited by Lyra, gives this reason: This was on account of the demerit of Gehazi, who did not keep the command of Elisha, but boastfully told those whom he met that he had been sent to raise the dead. For he is unworthy to work miracles who glories in working them. This is precisely what Elisha had foreseen and guarded against when he said to the servant he was sending: *If thou meet any man, salute him not; and if any man salute thee, answer him not.* For, as Theodoret rightly says in question 17: He knew, says he, that Gehazi was eager for honour and would give an account of his errand; but vainglory would be an impediment to his working the miracle. Rabbi Eliezer, chapter 33, gives another reason: Gehazi was mocking, he says, telling those he met: Do you suppose this staff will restore life? *And he lay upon the boy, and put his mouth upon his mouth*, etc. Francis Vallesius, in *Sacred Philosophy*, chapter 85, says: If it were permissible to suggest it, one might say the boy was not altogether dead but had suffered a syncope; in that case this treatment by Elisha would have been natural: for those seized by syncope are in danger of death from the extinction of the natural heat, and they are commonly restored by warmth and by the application of heat — above all by human breath, for that warmth is akin to and partakes of vital heat. Furthermore, Elisha walked up and down in order to stir the heat within himself, which he would then breathe into the boy; for heat is most powerfully excited by exercise; so that the boy, receiving Elisha as he lay upon him, began to yawn, the warmth now beginning to agitate and dispel the torpor, and, as vigour increased and the animal spirits moved, his eyes were opened, and then the organs of the other senses, and the boy was recalled to the functions of life. But from the narrative of Sacred Scripture it is clearly evident that the boy was truly dead and had not merely suffered a syncope. Elisha therefore lay upon the boy for the same reasons for which his master Elijah had lain upon a similar dead person, which I have set forth in 3 Kings 17:21. # GEM The allegorical and principal reason was that Elisha might represent, both by his name and by his action, the Incarnation of the Son of God, in which He clothed Himself with our flesh and, as it were, fitted mouth to mouth, hands to hands, feet to feet. So St. Augustine in sermon 11 *de verbis Apostoli*: What, he asks, did the dead boy signify, but Adam — and the son of Adam dead through sins? The staff was placed upon the dead boy, and he did not rise. For *if a law had been given that could give life, righteousness would indeed be by the law*. The law therefore could not give life. He Himself came, the great One to the little one, the Saviour to one to be saved, the Living to the dead — He Himself came. And what did He do? He contracted His majestic members, as though emptying Himself, that He might take on the form of a servant. He contracted His majestic members; He fitted Himself, the small One, to the small; that He might make the body of our humility conformable to the body of His glory.

He walked up and down in order to stir up, first, the greater heat which he would then breathe into the boy by lying upon him, and second, the greater fervour of prayer for the boy's resuscitation. Allegorically, Elisha walking up and down represented Christ walking through Judaea and everywhere scattering the seeds of the Gospel. So Eucherius. *And the boy sneezed seven times and opened his eyes.* Vatablus and others say: he sneezed. The Septuagint has: *he was strengthened*. Since the boy had suffered in the head, as was said at verse 19, he appears to have had his head oppressed by humours which, blocking the passages of the vital spirits, suffocated him. Wherefore, when by Elisha's action warmth, life, and vigour had been restored to him, his head being strengthened, he expelled by sneezing the humours that had weighed upon him; thus he purged the brain and was restored to perfect health. *And he gathered colocynths from the field.* The colocynth is a wild gourd used by physicians for the vigorous purging of phlegm; it is most bitter, and is therefore called *the gall of the earth*, as Vallesius says in *Sacred Philosophy*, chapter 36. He adds: it is not only bitter but also harmful; so much so that if more than four obols of the prepared form be taken, it can produce, beyond purgation and ulceration of the bowels, death itself — and moreover applied externally it can kill a foetus. Hence learn the virtue of poverty. Hear Theodoret: That the life of the prophets possessed no material goods, he says, the history makes plain. For he who was their chief (Elisha) now entertained them with a banquet of wild herbs; now set before a hundred men twenty loaves of barley, brought by others. And upon these he bestowed so great a blessing that he satisfied those hundred men, and after their satisfaction much remained over. *Death is in the pot.* That is to say: the broth of this pot is most bitter and vile, so as to seem a deadly poison. Tropologically: death is in the pot of worldly pleasures, which provoke gluttony and lust, and thus bring on various diseases and both temporal and eternal death; they are therefore bitter as colocynths. *Bring meal*, said he. For meal, being glutinous and soothing in the manner of starch, says Vallesius, tempers the biting sharpness and acrimony of the colocynths; and it was therefore prudently applied here by Elisha, although the principal cause of the correction of the broth was the divine and miraculous power. In a similar manner, in the cure of Naaman's leprosy, the water of the Jordan contributed something in chapter 5; and in the healing of Hezekiah's ulcer, the figs of Isaiah contributed something, chapter 20, verse 7; for they cleanse and disperse swellings when used as a poultice with barley or wheat flour. So Vallesius and Serarius, though Angelomus and others hold the contrary view — namely, that these remedies were of no benefit but rather a hindrance, in order to show that the cure was effected not by natural but by supernatural and divine power. *Pour out for the people.* Theodoret notes in question 19 that the sons of the prophets possessed nothing of their own, but lived in common, as religious do today. Tropologically, St. Bernard in sermon 65 among the minor works: A superior, in fulfilment of the duty of his office, sets before his subjects a great pot containing wild herbs — that is, an admonition treating of serious matters, full of bitterness, yet kindled by the fire of the Holy Spirit. The subjects therefore, shuddering at the austerity of the discourse, cry out: *Death is in the pot*, and cannot taste it. The wise dispenser therefore does not bring, but rather commands to be brought, the flour — for he does not himself supply, but exhorts his flock to possess, charity, by the seasoning of which those things are made sweet that formerly seemed bitter. For the preacher can sound words of saving counsel in the ears of those around him; but none save God alone is able to pour the savour of charity into the palate of the heart.

That is, soldiers sent to plunder the hostile land of Israel. In this way soldiers were once commonly called brigands, for the reasons I gave above; now they are often not called such, yet in reality they are brigands. THEY HAD LED AWAY CAPTIVE A LITTLE GIRL: through whom God healed Naaman, the commander of Syria, both in body and in mind, so that he might become a believer and a worshipper of God. Thus under the Emperor Constantine, a captive Christian woman, by her constant prayers, fasting, and exercises of piety, drew the Iberians to the faith of Christ, as Nicephorus attests, Book 8, Chapter 34.

So that he might bring them to Elisha, who would use them when he wished to change his everyday garment. He understands by this the finer and more costly garments which we wear in public and on solemn days, as Vatablus remarks.

That you might see to it that he be freed from leprosy through the prophet whom I hear to be a wonder-worker in your company. For the king did not know Elisha, and therefore he wrote not to him but to the king of Israel, who was known to him, so that through Elisha he might arrange for Naaman to be healed.

The king of Israel receives the words of the king of Syria with dismay: for he did not know what the little girl had said to the king of Syria concerning Elisha, nor that the king was seeking the cure of Naaman's leprosy not through his own power but through Elisha. Hence he suspected that the king was laying a trap for him, contemplating war, and using this matter as a pretext for hostilities.

Why did Elisha himself not go out to Naaman, to receive him honorably as one might receive a prince? Theodoret and Procopius answer first that Elisha was so devoted to the Law that he was unwilling to endure even the sight of an unclean man, much less his touch — so that we might understand how greatly we ought to flee the company and customs of those whom the leprosy of grave sins, of unbelief, and of heresy defiles. Second, and more truly, in order to show that he was withdrawn from courts and courtiers, devoted to God alone in the manner of a religious, and therefore making little account of the presence of princes, their servants, horses, and pomp, which the world admires. He did not do this out of pride, seeing that he permitted himself not only to be seen but even to be touched by a woman, as St. Gregory says in Book 7 of the Moralia, chapter 15. Gregory, praising at the beginning of that chapter the loftiness of soul in Elisha and similar saints, says: *All things that are outwardly eminent lie low and despised before their inward eyes. For, rapt inwardly above themselves, they fix their mind upon another object; and whatever things they suffer in this life they behold as flowing far below them and alien to themselves; and, if I may so say, while they strive in mind to pass beyond the flesh, they are scarcely aware of what they endure. In the eyes of such men, whatever is temporally prominent is not lofty. For, as from the summit of a great mountain, they look down with full contempt upon the joys of the present life; and, transcending themselves through spiritual elevation, they see within themselves as subject to them whatever swells outwardly with carnal glory. Hence they spare no powers that oppose the truth; but those whom they see lifted up through elation, they press down by the authority of the Spirit.* Thus, St. Antony, when saluted by Constantine the Great through letters, was scarcely willing to reply; yet he replied, compelled almost by his disciples, and gave him counsels of salvation, as St. Athanasius attests. Third, as the Abulensis says, Elisha wished to magnify the greatness of the miracle and the coming healing, performing it through a servant as intermediary — and not by touch but by word alone, as the Author of the Wonders of Holy Scripture says in Book 2, chapter 28.

Naaman speaks out of the pride of heathen princes, who presume to prescribe for themselves, according to their own judgment, the manner in which a miracle is to be worked; but God, wishing to humble them, ordains a different manner, so that they may learn to submit themselves and their own judgment to God, to receive a law from God rather than to give one, and to obey Him simply in all things.

Hear Benjamin in his Itinerary: *The city of Damascus itself is very great and most beautiful, girt with walls; the whole surrounding region is most richly furnished with gardens and paradises, extending fifteen miles on each side. Nowhere else in all the earth is there a fruit-bearing region like unto this city, which two rivers descending from Mount Hermon bless — Amana and Pharpar. For the city is situated beneath Mount Hermon: Amana flows through the city itself, whose waters are carried by pipes into all the houses of the great, and also into the forums and streets; and the region itself is frequented as the marketplace of all lands. Pharpar, however, flowing past the city itself, irrigates all the gardens and paradises.* **Verse 13.** [*Father, even if the prophet had bid thee do some great thing, surely thou oughtest to have done it; how much more now that he hath said to thee: Wash, and thou shalt be clean.*] Naaman was kindly toward his servants after the manner of a father, so much so that he was called father by them and was loved as a father — and so they charitably suggested to him a saving counsel, which he listened to peaceably and accepted. So too did Elisha call his master Elijah father, saying: *My father, my father, the chariot of Israel and the driver thereof.* Even now the Dutch, gentle by their nature, call their masters and lords *Baer*, that is, father. [*He went down and washed seven times in the Jordan.*] Why seven times? So that the number seven, which is reckoned the number of totality, might signify that all the capital sins — which are numbered as seven — and all of time, which revolves through seven days of the week, must be utterly eradicated from souls.
