Tertullian, in Book 4 against Marcion, chapter 9, of the nations, says: *Which shudder at the seven stains of capital offences. Therefore he washed seven times in the Jordan, as it were for each separate charge; and at the same time, that he might celebrate the expiation of the whole week, since the power and fullness of a single washing was reserved for Christ alone, who was to accomplish on earth a washing as compendious as His preaching.* Second, seven times, to signify the perfect cleansing and health of body and soul: for bodily health consists of the four elements and is produced by their proper balance, while health of soul is effected in baptism by the invocation of the name of the Holy Trinity. So Theodoret and Procopius. Third, the sevenfold washing prefigures the seven Sacraments of Christ's Church, by which every leprosy of sin is wiped away; these are therefore, as it were, seven baptisms and ablutions of the soul. [*And he was made clean.*] Behold, here is the fruit of simple obedience. So too was Constantine the Great cleansed of leprosy by the water of baptism administered by St. Sylvester — not by the power of the water, nor even precisely by the power of baptism (for the effect of baptism is to wash away the sins of the soul, not the leprosy of the body), but because that water was the instrument of the divine will and of the prophetic command, as St. Ambrose says. In the allegorical sense, the washing of Naaman in the Jordan signifies first baptism, which washes away the leprosy of the soul. For the Jordan was, as it were, consecrated to this purpose when Christ was baptized in it. Second, it signifies the sacrament of penance; for Jordan in Hebrew means the same as river of judgment, which is what is effected in the Sacrament of Penance. So Angelomus, Prosper, and others. Hear St. Ambrose, Book 4, chapter 4 on Luke: *Learn that spiritual sacraments are signified. A remedy for the body is sought, one for the mind is obtained. The flesh is washed, the affections are cleansed. For I see that the leprosy of the mind was cleansed no less than that of the body — when, after baptism, with the filth of his former error washed away, he refuses to offer to the foreign gods the sacrifices which he had vowed, declaring he would offer them to the Lord.*

That is, the gifts which he had brought with him, as described in verse 5. For these are called by the Hebrews *benedictio* — a blessing — because they are signs of love and goodwill. Moreover, this blessing is real and effective, being the same as a benefaction. So too when God blesses, it is efficacious and equivalent to conferring a benefit.

That is to say: Even if I and my disciples were in the utmost poverty and want, I swear nonetheless that I will not accept your gifts, so that I may show this miracle to be not mine but God's; and that I may demonstrate that I performed it purely out of love of God, not for the sake of gifts; and that you may learn that servants of God seek not the gifts of men, but of God. St. Hilarion did the same. Hear what St. Jerome writes in his Life concerning Orion, who had been freed from a legion of demons through him: "When he was healed," he says, "he came to the monastery with his wife and children, bringing very many gifts, as though to render thanks. To him the saint said: 'Have you not read what happened to Gehazi and to Simon? One of them received a price, the other perished: so that the one might sell the grace of the Holy Spirit, and the other traffic in it?' And when Orion, weeping, said: 'Accept it and give it to the poor,' he replied: 'You can better distribute your own possessions, who walk through cities and know the poor; but I, who have left behind what was mine—why should I grasp at what belongs to others?'" Perfect holiness therefore refuses gifts of whatever kind and however great, as Abraham did, Genesis 14:22, of whom St. Ambrose, Book 1 on Abraham, chapter 3, says: "How great is that act, that of the spoils of victory he wished to touch nothing, nor to accept what was offered? For the receipt of a reward diminishes the fruit of triumph, and gnaws away the grace of a benefit. For it matters greatly whether you have dedicated yourself to money or to virtue." Hence he merited to hear from God: "I am your protector, and your reward exceeding great," Genesis 15:1. Where St. Ambrose, cited above, says: "Because he did not seek a reward for himself from man, he received it from God." Hence Elisha here was a type of Christ commanding the Apostles: "Freely you have received, freely give," as St. Augustine says, Sermon 208 on the Season.

Israel. "Burdens," that is, of mules, as the Chaldean, the Septuagint, Vatablus, Pagninus, and others translate. For the Hebrew פֶּרֶד, Pered, is a mule; and perhaps the word burdo is derived from Pered, by changing the consonant P into the neighboring labial B. In the Digest, Book 32, Law 49, mention is made of burdones, where Accursius incorrectly understands them to mean walking sticks, which are called bourdons in French. Hence Cujas, Book 11 of Observations, chapter 16, asserts that burdones are mules, which are called manni and burici, bred from a donkey and a mare, or a horse and a she-donkey; and therefore in Hebrew they are called פְּרָדִים peradim, that is, "divided" or "separated," because they are of a kind divided and different from their progenitors: for פָּרַד parad means to divide and to separate. The sense is: Grant that I may be able to take from your land as much as two burdones, that is, mules, can carry, so that from it I may, as from holy ground, build in my homeland an altar to the God of Israel, whom alone I acknowledge as the true God from this miracle of my healing, and whom I venerate and love. Hence it is very probable that Naaman built a temple or chapel around the altar, and in it worshipped God with his whole household, and led many others to worship Him there. Naaman could have built an altar from Syrian soil. He preferred Israelite soil, however, because—as Theodoret, question 19, and Abulensis, question 22, note—he believed it to be holy ground, which God had chosen before all others, in which He had placed His people and His temple, which was renowned for the dwellings and virtues of so many most holy men. Note this passage in support of the veneration of the relics of the Saints, for the prophet Elisha, man of God, approved Naaman's vow. "FOR YOUR SERVANT WILL NOT OFFER HOLOCAUST OR SACRIFICE TO STRANGE GODS, BUT ONLY TO THE LORD"—Hebrew: Jehovah, who is the true and only God of Moses and Israel. Behold, to this point the holiness of Elisha and his disdain for gifts had brought Naaman. Hence, says Mendoza on Book 1 of Kings, chapter 12, verse 14, he judged that land more holy, in which the desire for money is buried, than that water in which the leprosy of bodies is cleansed. For this reason, when Elisha healed his leprosy, he offered him gold as to a physician; when Elisha refused the gold, he offered him prayers as to a God; and not by his own authority, but only after first obtaining his permission, did he dare to carry away a portion of the earth. Verse 18: "BUT IN THIS THING ALONE MAY THE LORD PARDON YOUR SERVANT: WHEN MY MASTER THE KING OF SYRIA GOES INTO THE TEMPLE OF REMMON TO WORSHIP THERE, AND HE LEANS UPON MY HAND, IF I BOW DOWN IN THE TEMPLE OF REMMON WHEN HE BOWS DOWN IN THE SAME PLACE, THAT THE LORD PARDON YOUR SERVANT IN THIS MATTER."

Gregory of Valencia, in his tract on faith and the profession of faith, disputation 1, question 3, point 2, answer 3, holds that this petition of Naaman contains something illicit, namely a profession of idolatry; that Naaman had cooperated in the idolatry of the king in the adoration of the idol Remmon, and that therefore Elisha neither assented nor replied directly, but merely permitted it—that is, maintained a negative stance—because he saw that Naaman was at that moment incapable of being corrected so as to lay aside this simulation of idolatry, and therefore deferred that matter to another time, it having sufficed for the present that he had brought Naaman to the knowledge of the true God. But if Naaman was cooperating in idolatry, Elisha ought to have instructed him on this matter and not remained silent. For from the words "Go in peace," Naaman would altogether conclude that Elisha assented to his petition and conceded that, as the king worshipped, he too might worship in the temple of Remmon; and so Elisha would have fostered and confirmed Naaman in his idolatry—which God forbid. Worse still, the Priscillianists, whom Machiavelli follows, said it was lawful to simulate outwardly heresy or idolatry, provided one retains true faith in the soul; hence they said: "Swear, forswear, but reveal not the secret." More mildly, yet still erroneously, Hadrian, Book on Baptism, question 1, response to article 5, asserts it is lawful to simulate idolatry externally, if there is no scandal and no precept of confessing the faith presses upon one. Note therefore that this adoration of Naaman was not adoration in the proper sense—for in that case it would have been impious and idolatrous—but in an improper sense, namely a bowing of the body or genuflection performed for the sake of duty, that is, so that he might support and sustain the king in the temple, as was his office, and as he was accustomed to do elsewhere; which he could not do without himself also genuflecting when the king genuflected. This is a catachresis. This is clear from the Hebrew שָׁחָה, sacha, which properly signifies to bend or bow oneself, as Arias translates here. The adoration of the king was therefore one thing, and the adoration of Naaman another: for the king properly adored his idol Remmon; but Naaman adored improperly, that is, he bowed himself so as to accommodate himself to his king who was bowing, and to support and honor him. His adoration was therefore not religious, but political and civil; just as Abraham adored, that is, showed reverence to, the sons of Heth, Genesis 23:7, and Jacob adored, that is, venerated, his son Joseph the prince of Egypt, Hebrews 11:21. For the king of Syria, when he walked about or prostrated himself for any reason, was accustomed to lean upon either the hands or the shoulders of Naaman; wherefore it was necessary that Naaman accommodate himself to the movement and bearing of the king. From this it followed that when the king stood, he stood; when the king walked, he walked; and when the king proceeded, he too proceeded. For had he omitted this, he would have been considered uncivil and boorish, indeed unfaithful. You will say: Naaman cooperated with the king in adoring Remmon; therefore he cooperated in his idolatry. I answer by denying the consequence. For he cooperated with the king in a natural action, namely in the mere bowing of the body, but not in the moral action, nor in the intention of the king, which was to adore Remmon by bowing. Thus, says Cajetan, nobles who accompany their king going to a concubine do not sin; because they do not accompany him in order that he may go to commit fornication and sin, but simply that he may go wherever he pleases: for they accompany him only for the sake of honor and duty, as servants their master. The meaning of Naaman was therefore this: I ask, O Elisha, for permission that, when the king bows in the temple of Remmon to adore it, it may be lawful for me—

That is to say: I swear by God that I will ask of Naaman some gift or reward for the healing given to him by my master Elisha. From this it is clear that Gehazi was not properly speaking a simoniac, because he did not sell the miraculous healing to Naaman for a price, nor did he make any agreement with him concerning it; but after the healing had been freely given to him by Elisha, he asked some gift from him. The first true simoniac was Simon Magus, who wished to buy the Holy Spirit from the holy Peter for a price, Acts 8; from whom this sin received the name of simony. Nevertheless, Gehazi sinned gravely: first, because he asked and accepted this gift from Naaman without the knowledge and against the will of Elisha; second, because he lied by saying it was Elisha who sought it; third, because he branded Elisha with this mark of covetousness, which greatly detracted from his holiness and honor. For Elisha, in refusing the gifts of Naaman, had been regarded by him as a divine man and a heavenly prophet; but now in seeking gifts he might appear to him a needy and greedy man, like the false prophets who accepted gifts in return for their oracles. Wherefore it is very probable that Elisha took care that Naaman should not know that Gehazi had asked these gifts from him without his knowledge, and that he had therefore been punished by him with leprosy; so that in this way his own integrity in contemning gifts might be clearly evident to Naaman, and Naaman might thereby be confirmed in the opinion he had formed of him and in his true faith in God. Fourth, Gehazi sinned by theft, because Naaman was sending these gifts to Elisha, on whose behalf Gehazi had requested them; but Gehazi intended to convert them to his own use. Hence he hid them in verse 24, lest Elisha should know anything about them. Wherefore Elisha soon upbraids him in verse 26: "You have received silver," he says, "that you might buy olive yards and vineyards." Fifth, he sinned by mental simony; for he had a simoniacal intention: he sought gifts from Naaman with a view to the healing miraculously bestowed upon him by Elisha, as though this reward and this price were owed to Elisha for the healing. Hence St. Ambrose, in the book on the Dignity of the Priesthood, chapter 14, says that simoniacs are followers of Gehazi and Simon Magus, and that therefore the leprosy of simony cleaves not to their bodies, but to their souls.

My heart was present, that is, my mind's eye was supernaturally elevated and assisted by God, so as to know and perceive, as though present, your distant and remote acceptance of gifts from Naaman — as if to say: You thought me absent when you sought the gifts, but in spirit I was present with you; for by the gaze of my mind I saw all that you did. Much more so, says St. Augustine (City of God, Book 22, Chapter 19), can the Saints in heaven perceive, though distant and remote, the things that occur on earth. In a similar manner, St. Benedict saw many things that were far away, as St. Gregory narrates (Dialogues, Book 2, Chapters 13, 18, 19). Hence St. Gregory adds that he possessed the spirit of Elisha.

A talent of silver contained six thousand shekels of silver, that is, six thousand Brabantine florins or francs, as the French call them. Therefore the two talents which Gehazi received were worth twelve thousand francs, which make four thousand French crowns, each of which contains twelve Julios or Reales. This sum, moreover, in that era, when all things were of cheap price and silver was scarce, would have been sufficient to purchase all these things. Others explain it disjunctively, as if to say: either fields, or olive groves, or vineyards, or sheep and oxen. From this it appears that Elisha granted and gave to Gehazi himself the gifts that Naaman had sent to him through Gehazi, lest by possessing them unjustly he should continue to sin.

Not absolutely, as is clear, but meaning: for the longest and most immemorial span of time — for example, for three hundred years, or unto the third and fourth generation, as in Exodus, chapter 7. So hold Serarius, Abulensis, Sanchez, and others. The silver, therefore, which Gehazi received unjustly was infected with poison and made pestilential, after the manner of garments and coins to which, in a plague-infected house, the plague adheres so as to invade and infect whoever receives them. For thus the leprosy of Naaman, as though it had clung to the silver, infected Gehazi as he received it, and made him leprous. Wherefore from that time forward Gehazi no longer lived in company with Elisha, but, as a leper, was obliged by Levitical law (chapters 13 and 14) to avoid the company of men; nor indeed would Elisha, most zealous for the law, have admitted him to his presence. So holds Abulensis, question 10. As for the fact that Gehazi is said in chapter 8 to have conversed with King Joram and to have recounted to him the miracles of Elisha, this appears to have occurred before he was struck with leprosy. Hence Abulensis, Serarius, Salianus, and others judge that there is here a hysteron-proteron, and that the leprosy of Gehazi, and consequently the coming of Naaman to Elisha and his healing, occurred later — namely, in the final year of the reign of Joram, shortly after which Joram himself was slain by Jehu. If, however, anyone wishes to retain the order of Sacred Scripture, he must say that Gehazi did penance, and so appeased both God and Elisha, and put away the leprosy from himself — if not from all his posterity — as Serarius holds; and that being now cleansed he conversed with King Joram. Here ends the Commentary on the Fourth Book of Kings, Chapter 5. CHAPTER SIX follows.

These were the huts in which so great a multitude of religious men dwelt. See here the poverty, labor, and unanimity of the ancient religious. Hear Theodoret: "The choir of prophets embraced extreme poverty willingly and voluntarily; for they had no houses, but used huts. Wherefore they humbly petitioned that great prophet to go with them to cut timber for building them. Such was the poverty they practiced that they could not even bear to own an axe." And hear St. Jerome, Epistle 4 to Rusticus: "The sons of the prophets, whom we read of in the Old Testament as monks, built themselves little cottages near the streams of the Jordan, and, having left the crowds of cities, lived on porridge and wild herbs." The monks and hermits after Christ did exactly the same, in the time of St. Anthony, St. Basil, and St. Jerome; whence near the Jordan they built for themselves little huts and cottages, as is plain from the Lives of the Fathers. Hence St. Jerome himself, through St. Paula, built five monasteries, to which he summoned the Roman nobility, and over one of them he himself presided. Wherefore Francis Suarez, in volume 3, On Religion, book 3, chapter 1, numbers 6 and following, drawing on St. Ignatius, Jerome, Ambrose, and Damascene, teaches that these sons, that is, the disciples of the prophets, were the type and prelude of the Religious of the New Testament, and that after their fashion they cultivated chastity, poverty, and obedience, but without vow.

Tropologically, Eucherius says: "Rightly did he who had lost the axe-head cry out: 'Alas, alas, alas, my lord, and this too I had borrowed.' For the elect have this as their own characteristic: if at any time their own knowledge is stolen away by the guilt of furtive vainglory, they swiftly return to their heart, and whatever they find in themselves worthy of condemnation before the eyes of the strict judge, they pursue with tears. Those who weep not only carefully examine what they have done amiss, but also, from the gift they have received, what good things they ought also to have rendered back." Verse 6: "And the man of God said (that is, Eliseus, the servant of God): Where did the iron fall? And he showed him the place; and he cut off a piece of wood and threw it in there, and the iron floated." St. Benedict performed this very same deed for a certain poor Goth who, as St. Gregory says in Dialogues, book 2, chapter 6, came to the place — to the lake into which the iron had fallen from the scythe — took the handle from the hand of the Goth, cast it into the lake, and immediately the iron came up from the depths and entered back into the handle. He at once returned the tool to the Goth, saying: "See, work, and do not be troubled." You may ask whether this was a true miracle. Vallés, Sacred Philosophy, chapter 37, affirms it was, saying that God took away the heaviness from the iron and added lightness to it, so that by swimming up from the bottom it might be carried to the surface. But I say that this was not, properly speaking, a miracle, which must exceed every force of nature and natural causes, so as to be capable of being performed by God alone. For all these things were accomplished through an Angel, who took the piece of wood cut off by Eliseus and fitted to serve as a handle, carried it to the bottom of the water, inserted it into the iron, lifted it to the surface, and kept the axe floating there until the woodcutter grasped it. So says the Abulensis. Allegorically, Tertullian, in his book Against the Jews, chapter 13, takes the waters of the Jordan to signify baptism; the wood, the Cross of Christ; the iron, the hardness of sinners' hearts. "What," he says, "is more manifest than the mystery of this wood? That the hardness of this world, sunk in the deep of error, is freed both from the wood of Christ — that is, His passion — in baptism; so that what was once lost through wood in Adam, might be restored through the wood of Christ." So also St. Ambrose, book 2, On the Sacraments, chapter 2: "Behold," he says, "another kind of baptism. Why? Because every man before baptism is pressed down and submerged as iron. When he has been baptized, he is raised not as iron, but as the lighter form of a fruitful tree." And shortly after: "You see, therefore, that in the Cross of Christ the weakness of all men is lifted up." And Theodoret, Question 19: "This," he says, "prefigured the dispensation of our Savior; for just as wood, which is very light, was submerged, while iron, which is very heavy, floated to the top; so the descent of the divine nature brought it about that human nature ascended." St. Augustine, however, in Sermon 210 On the Season, takes the river to signify flowing pleasures and concupiscences, from the midst of which, when we are submerged, Eliseus — that is, Christ — frees us through the wood of the cross: "The axe lay in the deep," he says, "because the human race had fallen by its unhappy ruin into the abyss of all crimes, as it is written: 'I am stuck fast in deep mire.' And again: 'I am come into the depth of the sea, and a tempest has overwhelmed me.' For that river, where the axe fell, signifies the passing, fleeting, and into-the-abyss-descending pleasure, or the luxury of this world. For a river takes its name from flowing. And because all sinners clinging to transitory pleasures are said to flow along, therefore that axe lay oppressed in the river and in the mire." He then proceeds to Eliseus, that is, Christ the Savior: "But Eliseus came and cast in the wood..."

The Abulensis thinks this man was Naaman, whom Eliseus had cured of leprosy; but it seems incredible that he who had received so great a benefit from Eliseus would have betrayed him to the king — as the Abulensis himself acknowledges at the beginning of chapter 8. "Eliseus the prophet, who is in Israel, tells the king of Israel all the words whatsoever thou speakest in thy private chamber." For it belongs to the spirit of prophecy to know things as secret, absent, and remote, as to foretell things future; for both require the same power, namely, divine assistance and revelation. # GEM

They that are with us are more, namely the Angels, who on behalf of God and His saints, as innumerable and most mighty heavenly armies, fight on every side — on account of which hosts God himself is called the Lord of Sabaoth, that is, the Lord of armies — according to that word of Psalm 23: "The Angel of the Lord shall encamp (in Hebrew, *chone*, that is, shall pitch camp) round about them that fear him." Thus of Jacob fleeing from Esau it is said, Genesis 32:1: "And the angels of God met him, and when he saw them he said: These are the camps of God." Thus the Angels fought for Moses and the Hebrews against the host of Pharaoh in the Red Sea, Exodus 16. Hence St. Ambrose, in his first sermon on Eliseus, says: "Divine fear drives away the terror of the enemy from us. These moreover are our weapons, with which the Savior has equipped us: prayer, mercy, and fasting. For fasting fights better than a wall; mercy delivers more readily than plunder; prayer wounds at a greater distance than an arrow. For an arrow strikes the enemy only when he is near and in sight, but prayer wounds the enemy even when he is placed far away." And a little after: "A wonderful thing! Sanctity deserves more defenders from heaven than wickedness has brought assailants on earth." He adds the reason: "For one must know that adversaries are overcome more by merits than by valor, and are vanquished not so much by strength as by holiness; just as the holy Eliseus overcame his enemies not by arms, but conquered them by prayer." Verse 17: "And the Lord opened the eyes of the servant." Not surprising, says St. Ambrose in his sermon on Eliseus, "if prayer opened the eyes to behold the army; not surprising, I say, if he who has deserved new assistance is granted new eyes. Or why should he not extend the sharpness of his gaze to behold the armies, who had marshaled the armies of the Angels? Why, I say, should he not by his prayer wipe away the dimness of the pupils, who by his merits had pierced through the darkness of the clouds? Necessarily therefore the holy Eliseus by this deed gave security to his servant who was terror-stricken, to whom he had already restored clear sight." "And behold, the mountain was full of horses and chariots of fire round about Eliseus." The Angels formed these horses and chariots in the appearance of fire, fashioning them from the air and clouds and rays of the sun passing through them suitably to display the color of fire, so as to represent to Eliseus their own heavenly and fiery nature, swiftness, strength, force, and power — even as they had represented these things to Elias at his rapture. For Eliseus was the successor and heir of the spirit of Elias; in him therefore was fulfilled that promise of God: "I will be to him," saith the Lord, "a wall of fire round about, and I will be in glory in the midst of him," Zechariah 2:5. See what is said there. St. Ambrose gives the reason in his second sermon on Eliseus: "What wonder," he says, "if he deserves assistance from heaven whose soul is always in heaven, as the Apostle says: 'But our conversation is in heaven'? If therefore our conversation is in heaven, the conversation of heavenly beings can be with us — that is, we who live this life may rightly merit the fellowship of Angels. There is therefore a certain kinship, union, and fellowship among those who live holy lives, nor does it matter whether they dwell in heaven or on earth, whether their manner of life be angelic or human, provided that the same life or holiness be in them. For manner of life connects those whom their element separates, and though they may be divided in their different bodies, they are nevertheless joined together by the same acts. And therefore it comes about that the saints, clinging together in a certain fellowship, do not depart from one another, and that now Angels descend to earth, now men are translated to heavenly things, and that for those whose life is shared in common, by an ever advancing progress, even the very elements of life begin to be common between them."

The Septuagint renders this as *aorasia*, that is, blindness of perception, so that seeing they might not see, nor take notice. Hence the Syrians, struck with this blindness, could indeed see Elisha and the city, but did not recognize them nor take heed of them. With a similar blindness and *aorasia* were the men of Sodom struck when they assaulted the house of Lot (Genesis 19). Hear Saint Ambrose, in his sermon on Elisha: *Then therefore Elisha prayed, and struck the entire hostile army with blindness. It is no wonder that he brought blindness upon his adversaries, who had bestowed clarity of sight upon his servant; and that he takes away vision from enemies, who grants sight to his minister. Where are those who say that the arms of men avail more than the prayers of the saints? Behold, a single prayer of Elisha wounded an entire army, and by the merits of one prophet the whole number of the enemy was taken captive. What hosts of kings, what multitudes of soldiers ever achieved such a victory, as to so utterly prostrate their enemies that not one of them fell dead? This is the true, this is the bloodless victory, where the adversary is so conquered that none among the conquerors is harmed.*

Abulensis supposes that Elisha here told an officious lie. For the Syrians could see the city of Dothan, in which, or near which, Elisha was then dwelling — and this Elisha seems to deny when he says: *Neither is this the city which ye seek.* Lyra, however, defends Elisha, saying that he spoke not of the city of Dothan where he was then residing, but of Samaria, where he had his house and had established his seat, as is evident from verse 32. For Elisha does not here respond to what the Syrians had asked — since they had asked nothing — but to their intention, as though he were saying: You seek Elisha, O Syrians! Come with me; I will show you the way to Samaria, where he dwells, and there I will point him out to you, and bring it about that you recognize the one whom you now have present before you, yet, being struck with *aorasia*, do not know. For he did not wish to be recognized by them in Dothan, but in Samaria, so that he might lead them to the king and receive them at a banquet. In these words, then, there was no lie, but merely a concealment of the truth, which is lawful and often necessary; for had he disclosed the truth and said, *I am Elisha whom you seek*, he could not have delivered them to the king of Israel, nor displayed equally his power and his beneficence toward them. But if the Syrians had asked Elisha: *What is the way to Dothan, where Elisha is?* — as some scholars hold, and as Elisha's response seems to suggest — then Elisha was not bound to answer according to the intention of his questioners, who were enemies, but according to his own meaning, namely, that this was not Samaria, his homeland, where they would come to recognize the Elisha they were seeking, as I have said.

That is: you shall not kill the Syrians whom I have brought to you. For, as Theodoret says, *if they are slain, the greatness of the miracle will not be known; but if they are sent back to him who sent them, he too will learn the power of our God.* — *But set bread and water before them, that they may eat and drink.* Elisha was unwilling that the Syrians, though enemies, should be slain by the king of Israel, but rather that they should be treated generously and sumptuously, so that he might overcome them with charity and make friends out of enemies, so that henceforth they would not invade nor plunder the land of Israel — which indeed came to pass, for they were conquered by Elisha, both by his power and, more especially, by his kindness. See Saint Chrysostom (or whoever the author may be — for the elegance of the Latin style argues that he was a Latin, not a Greek writer), in the homily on Elisha and the detected ambushes of the Syrians, tome 1, where among other things he says of the Syrians: *They marvel and are astonished that in place of death, food is prepared for them; in place of destruction, a meal; in place of punishment, a banquet.* And further below: *Then the most holy prophet is glorious among his own people, and yet more glorious among strangers. To his own he displays signs of power; to strangers he furnishes occasions of glory. He was the first to teach that enemies should be cherished, the first to teach that foes should be spared, the first to teach that feasts should be spread for adversaries in place of punishment.* He then teaches that Elisha was a type of Christ, and adds: *O Lord Christ, both new in ancient things and ancient in new examples prefigured! Most holy Elisha closes sight, and opens it again; makes men blind, and illuminates them; takes away vision, and bestows it; takes it from those who pursue him, and grants it to those who follow him; makes strangers blind, and makes those enclosed in the city to see. Those who came to take the prophet captive are blinded; and those who had already been captive receive sight. The truth of both of these things is fulfilled, that both the figure of Christ and the example of the future people might be shown forth; so that henceforth they who were strangers might become his own, enemies become friends, and foes become kinsmen. Elisha dismisses these armies to their own people — dismisses, I say, as many preachers as he had enemies, as many reporters of his mighty deeds as there were men in the hostile host.* Finally he concludes by saying that what was then lacking was afterwards fulfilled through the Apostles at Pentecost and thereafter, according to the word of Scripture in Psalm 18: *Their sound went forth into all the earth, and their words unto the ends of the world.* # GEM

That is to say, during the lifetime of Elisha, or for a long period of time; for that they came again afterward is clear from what follows. This was the fruit of Elisha's beneficence toward the Syrian marauders, as I have already noted. So also Saint Chrysostom, homily 1 on Elisha.

This amounts to 80 shekels — that is, approximately 80 Brabantine florins, or 80 francs. For the shekel was a silver coin, although some hold it was worth only half a shekel, in which case 80 silver pieces would equal 40 francs. Some interpreters take "the head of an ass" to mean the whole animal, understanding it as a synecdoche, by which the head signifies the whole body or creature, as when we say: "What does that head of a man want?" or "That mad head?" — meaning, that man. Others more simply take it to mean only the head of the ass; for this best illustrates the enormity of the famine, and the ass has a notably large head in proportion to the rest of its body. So too, when Hannibal besieged Casilinum, a single mouse was sold for two hundred denarii, as Valerius Maximus attests in Book 7, chapter 6. **And a quarter of a cab of dove's dung for five pieces of silver.** The cab, or choenix, was the measure of a day's sustenance, containing four pounds and five ounces, as I noted at the end of the Pentateuch; a quarter of a cab was therefore a pound and an ounce, or about thirteen ounces. Lyra, Hugo, and Dionysius understand by "dung" the grains of wheat found in the crop or stomach of doves. But others take it plainly as dung, as the word sounds. Hence Josephus and Theodoret hold that this dove's dung was used in place of salt, for it has a sharp and caustic property, as Galen testifies. Others more plainly consider the dung of doves to have served as food, both because it is composed of grains and other fairly clean substances, and because it is more moist and made up of less corrupted matter, and so is edible for human beings. Pliny adds, in Book 29, chapter 6, that dove-dung dissolved in vinegar is efficacious for treating fistulas, white spots, and scars; and in Book 30, chapter 10, for soothing roughness of the throat and catarrh. Furthermore, pearls, which are celebrated as remedies against cardiac and melancholic conditions, and whose dryness, once imitated, consumes the body's superfluities and renders the humors purer — so writes Francis Ruaeus in his book *On Gems*, chapter 13. There is in the Vosges mountains of Lorraine a river rich in pearls, though they are not so very lustrous; yet it is remarkable that what they lack in natural beauty is supplied through doves, which, after swallowing them, render them at last purer and restore them along with their excrement — so says Serarius, himself a Lorrainer.

That is, as Vatablus translates: *Since the Lord does not help you, from where should I help you? From the threshing floor, or from the wine-press?* — meaning: Since the Lord has sent this famine upon the whole city, unless He Himself relieves you in it, I cannot relieve you, for I have no grain in the storehouse nor wine in the press; that is to say, I myself lack bread and wine for sustenance. Hence the Chaldean renders it: *If the Lord does not save you, from where shall I save you?* From this also the Royal editors in the Vulgate edition read it thus: *The Lord save you not* — meaning, not I, but let the Lord save and feed you, for I have nothing to give you. Josephus, Abulensis, and Sanchez interpret it differently: the king, they say, angered by the famine and by the woman's importunate pleading, responded indignantly: *God save you not* — meaning, may God afflict you. So too the pagans, when angered and pronouncing curses, would say: *May the gods destroy you! May the gods do you ill!* Hence Vatablus also translates: *Perish, why do you ask food of me, when you know I am destitute of all food?*

This was a barbarous feast wrung from a mother by extreme famine, such that, forgetting her motherhood, she became the slaughterer and devourer of her own son. A similar thing occurred during the siege of Jerusalem under Titus, as Josephus testifies; and in these last years, when the Swede was ravaging Germany, many driven by famine devoured human flesh. God permitted the Samaritans to be reduced to these extremities: first, to punish their schism and idolatry; second, so that His deliverance of them might be all the more glorious, along with the prophecy of Elisha who promised it. So God at times permits men to come to the very threshold of death, that they may call upon Him with all the greater fervency, and that God, hearing them, may receive from them the greater gratitude and praise. Wherefore in situations that are by nature desperate, one ought to hope most in God, who is accustomed to come to the rescue in precisely such cases, according to that saying: *Deus ex machina*. Hear Saint Augustine, Sermon 211 *On the Season*: *He converted into food one whom God had fashioned as a consolation. But after they had come to that dreadful banquet, one woman urged the other: Receive, she said, receive back, wretched mother, into your womb the son lately brought forth from it; be his tomb, be the dwelling-place of his body; be, if I may so say, his monument. Let the infant, consumed a little while within you, return — he who could no longer live because of hunger; let the womb now receive him eaten which had delivered him alive; let him return in death to the habitation from which he lately went forth in life.*

This king, though impious and an idolater, judged that God must be appeased by penance and sackcloth; he was therefore more devout than our heretics, who, caring only for their skin, reject haircloth and assign it to superstition. Observe here how ancient is the use of the haircloth in penance. So the king of Nineveh, together with his whole people, clothed himself in sackcloth and sat in ashes (Jonah 3:6), and thus escaped by repentance the destruction threatened by God through Jonah. So too an Arian king put on haircloth and by it and by his prayers appeased God, as Salvian testifies in Book 7. Moreover, the use of haircloth is far more ancient still: the first to be recorded as using it is the patriarch Jacob, upon hearing that his son Joseph had been devoured by a beast (Genesis 37:34), concerning which I have spoken at length in that place.

That is to say: "Today I will cut off the head of Elisha, because he himself persuaded us to endure this siege at Samaria, promising us God's help in relieving it, while we suffer extreme famine within it and are driven to despair: for without him we would have timely made terms with the king of Syria who besieges us, and thus we would have escaped this famine which Elisha sees and ignores, and takes no pains to avert by his prayers." So Theodoret.

KNOW YE NOT THAT THE SON OF A MURDERER (namely Joram, king of Israel, who is the son of Ahab the murderer; for Ahab slew Naboth) HATH SENT HITHER TO CUT OFF MY HEAD? — that is to say: Joram takes after his father and imitates him; for just as Ahab his father slew Naboth, so he now wishes to slay me, even as he himself slew his six brothers, as is recorded in 2 Chronicles 21:3 and following. SEE THEREFORE THAT WHEN THE MESSENGER COMETH, YE SHUT THE DOOR AND SUFFER HIM NOT TO ENTER IN: FOR IS NOT THE SOUND OF HIS MASTER'S FEET BEHIND HIM? — that is to say: the king himself, repenting of the command he sent and wishing to revoke it, is immediately following after the messenger he dispatched to kill me. So Josephus. That is to say: Fear not to resist the king's messenger, for the king himself will straightway recall him; for so hastily does he follow after him that one may almost hear the sound of his hurrying feet. From this it is plain that Elisha had persuaded the king not to surrender the Samaritans to the Syrians, but to hope for help and relief from the Lord. This king Joram was the son of Ahab who succeeded him; for his deeds are described here through to chapter 8, verse 24, where his death is recorded. Therefore Joram had in a burst of rage hastily sent a messenger to kill Elisha; but presently, as his anger subsided and he returned to himself, he repented; and fearing lest the messenger should slay Elisha as he had commanded, he immediately ran after the messenger to prevent the killing. For he knew that Elisha was a prophet, a holy man and a benefactor of Samaria, and had often proved by experience that his oracles were faithful and most true — so that concerning this matter also, namely the lifting of the siege, he could scarcely doubt. So Josephus, Theodoret, and Abulensis state explicitly. CHAPTER SEVEN

That is, it shall be sold for one Julius, or Royal coin—that is, tomorrow there shall be such an abundance of grain that all the want and famine of Samaria shall be lifted, and all things shall be obtained at a cheap price. Allegorically: Benhadad is the devil, who was besieging Samaria—that is, the ancient Church—and was causing a great spiritual famine of the word of God, of salvation, and of heavenly things. Elisha came, that is, Christ, who by preaching everywhere through himself and the Apostles, lifted this famine and brought it about that everywhere there should be teachers to preach the word of God, and to teach the way of salvation, virtue, and perfection. So say Eucherius and Angelomus.

So that through them wheat might rain down, just as the waters rained down in the flood of Noah, and manna in the desert (Exodus 16). "Can it be that what you say is possible?"—that is to say: it is impossible that in so great a scarcity of things there should suddenly be so great an abundance, that wheat should be sold at so cheap a price, and a single measure obtained for a single shekel. "YOU SHALL SEE IT WITH YOUR OWN EYES, BUT YOU SHALL NOT EAT THEREOF"—because you shall be crushed by the press of the people in the gate; and this shall come to pass by the just judgment of God on account of your unbelief, in that you do not believe this oracle of mine which comes from God; because you seem to think that what has been foretold is impossible even for God, to whom all things are possible. Therefore you deny to God His own omnipotence. So says the Abulensis.

Of the enemies rushing in upon them; and so God struck the Syrians with terror, so that in their consternation they fled and left Samaria free. So says Josephus. Observe here how divine power plays with human affairs. For He could have sent Angels against the Syrians who would have slaughtered them all at once; He could have blasted them with lightning; He could have destroyed them with heavenly fire and in a thousand other ways. But He willed it not, and chose rather to crush their pride through an empty sound, through a false imagining, through a ridiculous fear—whereby they trembled with terror where there was no cause for terror, and casting away all their possessions, they fled with the sole aim of saving their lives. A like panic fear upon a host of enemies advancing against them was sent by God into the camp of Midian, when Gideon with three hundred soldiers dashed together their flaming jars and sounded their trumpets and attacked it. Judges 7:19.

That is, throughout all Samaria, which is the head and metropolis of the kingdom of Israel, or of the ten Tribes. The Interpreter reads *bechal*, that is, "in all"; but others read in its place the cognate form *techalleh*, that is, "as all." Hence the Chaldean, Pagninus, and Vatablus translate: "Behold, they are like all the multitude of Israel"—that is to say: just as the whole people in Samaria are lean and consumed with famine, so also these five horses that remain to me are lean and wasted with hunger.

Because the gate was narrow, and the crowd was enormous: a crowd which, maddened by hunger, rushed almost in a frenzy toward the food that was in the camp of the Syrians. Hence the commander of the gate himself, wishing to restrain this crowd and divide it into orderly sections, lest they crush one another, was overwhelmed and trampled by the same crowd as it burst through violently. Thus the oracle of Elisha was proved true and certain in every particular. CHAPTER EIGHT.

This is a case of hysteron proteron; for this prophecy of Elisha concerning the seven-year famine was made earlier, namely five or six years before; but it is recounted here because, upon the return of the woman who had been his hostess — to whom Elisha had foretold this famine — to her own land after the seven years of famine, there befell her those events which are narrated here in verse 5 and following. This is confirmed further by the fact that Gehazi speaks here in verse 4 with the king. He was not yet therefore a leper; for lepers were excluded from human intercourse. These events therefore occurred before the coming and healing of Naaman; for it was at that time that Gehazi was struck with leprosy, as I noted above. So argue Torniellus, Salianus, and others.

At Damascus. Ben-hadad fell into this illness — of which he died — shortly after the lifting of the siege of Samaria, brought on by terror and grief. Hear Josephus, Book 9, Chapter 2: "The king of the Syrians, returning to Damascus and taking up residence there, when he learned that by a divinely sent terror he and his army had been put to flight, and that what they had believed concerning the approach of enemies had proven vain, being utterly persuaded that the divine power was against him, fell from distress of mind into bodily illness as well." God clearly willed to punish Ben-hadad, who had brought so many disasters upon Israel, with sickness and death; and for this purpose He sent Elisha to Damascus, that he might foretell Ben-hadad's death to Hazael and declare him the successor to the kingdom.

Hazael offered so many and so great gifts to Elisha on behalf of the health of his master King Ben-hadad; but it is certain that Elisha did not accept them — both because he did not heal Ben-hadad but foretold his death, and because he had steadfastly refused the gifts of Naaman in chapter 5, whom he had cured of leprosy, and had punished Gehazi with leprosy for accepting them. So argue the Abulensis and others. **Verse 10. Go, say to him: Thou shalt recover. Nevertheless the LORD hath shown me that he shall surely die.** This is a difficult passage; for it appears that Elisha is commanding Hazael to tell a lie — namely, to tell King Ben-hadad that he would recover, when in fact he was about to die shortly. First, the Rabbis, and following them the Abulensis, read it in the contrary sense — that is, *thou shalt not recover* — for in place of *lo* written with *vav*, meaning "to him," they read *lo* written with *aleph*, meaning "not." But the Chaldee, the Septuagint, and all Latin codices read it affirmatively: *thou shalt recover*. Second, others answer that Elisha spoke ironically, as if to say: yes, indeed thou shalt recover — so thou hopest; but thou art mistaken, for thou shalt not recover, but shalt die. Third, and most genuinely: *thou shalt recover* — that is, insofar as the disease itself is concerned; as if to say, thy disease is not fatal, thou shalt not die of this illness — which was true, for it was not the disease but the violence of Hazael that killed and suffocated Ben-hadad. So argue Cajetan, Serarius, Salianus, and others.

Hazael denied that he was a dog — not merely in respect of worthlessness, but more particularly in respect of canine shamelessness and ferocity — as if to say: I am not so shameless, barbarous, and savage as to rage like a dog against human flesh and devour it. Why then, O Elisha, dost thou assert and declare to me: *Thou shalt dash in pieces their little ones and rip open their women with child*? Such indeed was Hazael's sentiment at that moment; but once made king of Syria he changed his mind, and did what Elisha here foretells and what he himself here regards with abomination. For honours change character — but rarely for the better. The reason he adds in the words: **THE LORD HATH SHOWN ME THAT THOU SHALT BE KING OVER SYRIA** — as if to say: thou shalt be king of Syria, and therefore, once made king, thou shalt take upon thee the innate hatred of the Syrians toward the Israelites, and shalt dash in pieces their little ones and rip open those with child, as I have foretold. This hatred had its origin in Hadad, when he rebelled against Solomon and made himself king of Syria; and it was drawn from him as from a father by his son Ben-hadad, and from him by the subsequent kings of Syria. See what was said at 1 Kings 11, verse 14. Made bolder by this prediction of kingship, Hazael suffocated King Ben-hadad and seized his kingdom, as follows; and, heedless and ungrateful toward Elisha, he made himself an enemy of Israel, raged against it, and fulfilled the barbarity that Elisha had foretold of him.

The Hebrew, Chaldean, and Septuagint add "and he died," which our text expresses, subjoining: *And when he was dead, Hazael reigned in his place.* It appears that Ben-hadad, burning with the heat of fever, had asked for water to refresh himself; Hazael extended it to him in a cloth soaked in water, and so either through carelessness, or more likely through treachery, with water dripping continually from the cloth into the mouth and throat of Ben-hadad, he suffocated him, in order to succeed him in the kingdom. Serarius adds that this water and cloth were perhaps infected with some poisonous liquid, as the ancients relate concerning the garment of Hercules given by Deianira, and concerning the cases of the Emperor Otto III, of Ladislaus king of the Hungarians, and of the tunic of Suleiman. Josephus says simply that he was strangled. Thus indeed did God punish Ben-hadad for the cruelty he had exercised against the Israelites. Furthermore, Ben-hadad appears to have reigned for fifty-six years; for so many years intervene between the seventeenth year of Asa king of Judah, in which Ben-hadad began to reign, as is clear from 3 Kings 15:18, and the first year of the reign of Hazael. So says Salianus. Some, however, think that several kings intervened in Syria, and that all were called Ben-hadad, that is, sons and successors of Hadad, the first king of Syria. Hence the Hebrews relate that Ben-hadad was the common name of the kings of Syria. Finally, Josephus in Book 11 of the *Antiquities*, chapter 2, asserts that both Ben-hadad and Hazael are held in the highest veneration among the Syrians, indeed that both are worshipped with divine honours, and that the image of each is honoured in daily processions, both on account of their other benefactions and because by magnificently constructing temples they made Damascus more glorious.

That is, upon the throne after the death of his father Jehoshaphat. You will object that this seems to have occurred in the seventh year of Joram, not the fifth; for in chapter 3, verse 1, it is said that Joram king of Israel began to reign in the eighteenth year of Jehoshaphat king of Judah; but Jehoshaphat reigned twenty-five years; from the eighteenth year to the twenty-fifth there are seven years, after which Jehoshaphat dying, his son Joram succeeded him. Lyran and Cajetan respond that Joram son of Ahab reigned after the death of Ahab together with his firstborn brother Ahaziah for two years—namely, from the eighteenth year of Jehoshaphat until his twentieth year, in which Ahaziah died—and then Joram his younger brother began to reign alone. Therefore where it is here said *in the fifth year of Joram*, understand it of the time when Joram began to reign alone after his brother's death, which was in the twentieth year of Jehoshaphat. Yet this is opposed by what is said in 3 Kings last chapter, verse 32, namely that Ahaziah began to reign in Israel in the seventeenth year of Jehoshaphat, and died in the eighteenth year of the same, for he reigned only two years; wherefore since Ahaziah died in the eighteenth year of Jehoshaphat, and his brother Joram immediately succeeded him, it follows that Joram began to reign in the eighteenth year of Jehoshaphat, not the twentieth. Abulensis answers more aptly that the eighteenth year of Jehoshaphat is not to be counted, because Joram began to reign only at its completion, nor the nineteenth, because he began to reign only near its end, nor the twenty-fifth, because it was incomplete; with these three years removed, from the eighteenth to the twenty-fifth year of Jehoshaphat there remain only five complete years, of which it is here said: *In the fifth year of Joram son of Ahab*, etc., *reigned Joram son of Jehoshaphat*—namely after the death of Jehoshaphat, who died in the twenty-fifth year of his reign; for then his son Joram succeeded him. Most excellently do Richard of St. Victor in his *Conciliation of the Books of Kings and Chronicles*, Arias in his commentary on Daniel, Salianus, Torniellus, Sanchez, and Serarius respond here that Joram king of Judah was twice named king by his father, on account of two of his wars: first, in the seventeenth year of Jehoshaphat, when he himself was about to go with Ahab to war against the Syrians (3 Kings 22), at which time Joram appears to have received from his father Jehoshaphat the title of king alone, so that if any misfortune should befall him in battle, Joram might succeed him. Second, after six years, namely in the twenty-third year of Jehoshaphat, when he set out to war against the Moabites, as is described in chapter 3 of this book; for then Jehoshaphat again declared his son Joram king, and entrusted to him the administration of the kingdom during his absence. From this point the eight years during which Joram is said to have reigned are to be counted, namely such that Joram reigned three years while his father Jehoshaphat was still living, and four further years after his father's death; the eighth and last year was incomplete, and therefore is not counted. Wherefore, although some hold that Joram reigned eight years after the death of his father Jehoshaphat, it is more truly as I have said, namely that he reigned only four complete years and an incomplete fifth after the death of Jehoshaphat, while during Jehoshaphat's lifetime he reigned in his last three years—as is clear from chapter 9, verse 29, where Ahaziah son of Joram king of Judah, upon the death of his father Joram, is said to have begun to reign in the eleventh year of Joram king of Israel. Now count the years from the fifth year of Joram king of Israel to his eleventh, and you will have only five complete years, in which alone the other Joram reigned in Judah after the death of his father Jehoshaphat. Conversely, when in chapter 1, verse 17, it is said that Joram king of Israel began to reign in the second year of Joram son of Jehoshaphat king of Judah, understand the second year from that time when Joram first...

That is, the royal stock and lineage, namely of grandchildren succeeding one another continuously upon the throne of Judah. *Verse 21: And Joram went to Seir*—that is, into Idumaea. For this land, just as it was called Idumaea after Esau its founder, who was also called by another name Edom, so also from the same man who was likewise called Seir, meaning hairy or shaggy, it was named *Seir*. *Verse 23: And the rest of the acts* (that is, of the deeds and exploits) *of Joram, and all that he did, are they not written in the Book of the Words* (i.e., chronicles) *of the Kings of Judah?*—to wit, in the diaries and annals of the kings of Judah. Note that many things concerning Joram are here passed over in silence, which are therefore supplied in 2 Chronicles chapter 21, where at verse 3 king Jehoshaphat is said to have given to his sons, who were the brothers of Joram, *pensions* (which some incorrectly read as *compensations*), that is, annual revenues. Again, that Joram slew all his brothers, six in number, so that he alone might reign in security. Moreover, that Elijah—already taken up nine years before—sent to him a letter from paradise, in which he threatened him with God's vengeance on account of these fratricides and his idolatry, which vengeance soon overtook him; for God struck him with an incurable disease, so that his bowels fell out; he also slew all his sons through the Philistines and Arabs, with the sole exception of Ahaziah who, though the youngest born, succeeded him—and this as a punishment of retaliation, so that just as he himself had slain his brothers, so too his sons should be slain. Josephus adds that his wives were also killed. Thus did these barbarians by the just judgment of God appease the shades of the slain sons of the king, the righteous brothers, by their own hands; yet the foolish king did not say in his heart: *Thou art just, O Lord, and thy judgment is right.*

You will say that in the following chapter, verse 29, Ahaziah is said to have begun to reign in the eleventh year of Joram, not the twelfth. The answer is that he began to reign in the eleventh year together with his father Joram, who was tormented by his illness; but in the following year, which is the twelfth, with his father dead, he reigned alone in Judah. So say Abulensis, Sanchez, Salianus, and others.

You say that in 2 Paralipomenon 22:3 he is said to have been twenty-two years old? This contradiction is exceedingly difficult and almost irreconcilable; I shall examine it in its proper place in the commentary on Paralipomenon. AS FOR THE NAME OF HIS MOTHER, ATHALIA, DAUGHTER OF AMRI: that is, granddaughter of Amri; for Athalia was the daughter of Achab, as was said at verse 18, and Achab was the son of Amri. So in Matthew 1:8, Ozias is called the son of Joram, though he was in fact his grandson, indeed his great-grandson; for three generations are omitted there, and three intermediate sons, namely Ochozias, Joas, and Amasias, because Joram had joined himself to the impious lineage of Achab, and together with his wife Athalia, daughter of Jezabel, had brought idolatry into the kingdom of Juda. The Hebrews, as cited by St. Jerome in his Traditions, and Cajetan, answer differently, namely that Athalia was truly the daughter of Amri, yet is called the daughter of Achab either by way of imitation or of upbringing, in that she was raised by Achab and therefore imitated his idolatry.

Ochozias therefore appears to have taken as wife a daughter of Joram the son of Achab, just as Joram the father of Ochozias had taken Athalia the daughter of Achab; or at least he took the daughter of a brother or sister of Joram. He is therefore called the son-in-law of the house of Achab because his father-in-law was Joram, or a brother of Joram. Indeed, this entire progeny of impiety and idolatry was brought into the kingdom of Juda through the wicked Jezabel; for her daughter was Athalia, and her granddaughter was the wife of Ochozias, both of whom, having been raised by Jezabel — who was the mother of the one and the grandmother of the other — absorbed her impiety and her pagan ways. Observe here how dangerous and harmful it is to take wives who are unbelievers or heretics. CHAPTER NINE

For that city, after King Joram had besieged it and received his wound, had been taken either wholly or in part, as Abuleius, Sanchez, and Serarius affirm; and this is evident from verse 15. "THOU SHALT RAISE HIM UP FROM THE MIDST OF HIS BRETHREN" — that is, as Vatablus explains, bid him rise from the midst of his brethren, that is, of the princes his colleagues; for those who are here called brethren are shortly after called princes. "AND THOU SHALT BRING HIM INTO AN INNER CHAMBER" — so that thou mayest anoint him king in secret; lest if thou do so publicly, a sedition be stirred up by the adherents and partisans of King Joram. And therefore, when thou hast anointed him secretly, thou shalt immediately flee and not remain there.

This appears to have been the second anointing of Jehu; for the first seems to have been performed by Elijah, since God had commanded this of him in 3 Kings 19:15, and had therefore bidden him go to Damascus, there to anoint Hazael as king of Syria, and then to Samaria, to anoint Jehu as king of Israel. But that first anointing by Elijah was preparatory and prophetic of the kingship, whereas this second anointing, performed by Elisha, was the actual conferral of the kingdom and his creation as king. So hold Cajetan, the Historia Scholastica, Sanchez, and others.

Observe here how the princes of idolatry and men of military life mock the prophet and call him mad and a fanatic. For the austere life of the prophets, their rough garb, and their prophetic speech seemed to these men to be madness; and the prophets appeared to them to be out of their minds, for they were as it were melted in spirit and breathed upon by the higher spirit of the divinity. Hence Plato in the Phaedo says: "Some men are mad by divine beneficence, as are the prophets and the Sibyls." And Cicero in book 2 of On Divination asks: "What authority has that frenzy which you call divine, that a madman should see what a wise man does not see, and that he who has lost his human senses should have attained to divine ones, and that to prophesy should be the same as to be mad?" For the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God, 1 Corinthians 2 and 3. The false prophets of the devil likewise behaved in this way, for they performed disordered gestures and appeared to rage; whence Virgil, concerning the Sibyl consulted by Aeneas, sings in Aeneid 6: "Her face and colour are not one and the same; her hair remains not bound; but her breast heaves, and her wild heart swells with frenzy, and she appears greater and sounds not mortal, being inspired by the breath of the now more present god." "YE KNOW THE MAN AND WHAT HE HATH SPOKEN" — as if to say: ye know him to be mad, and consequently to have spoken madly, and therefore his words are not to be believed nor to be related by me. Jehu says this lest, if he were to say that he had truly been anointed king by him, he should bring danger upon himself — namely, lest someone fighting for Joram or envying Jehu's kingship should fall upon him and slay him. When they pressed him further, asking, as Josephus relates, what reason the young man had come and saying that he seemed to be mad, Jehu replied: "You conjecture rightly, for he spoke the words of a madman." But when they asked still more earnestly that he explain the matter to them, Jehu said that the prophet had declared that the sovereignty of the people had been committed to him by God. "But they said: It is false; but rather TELL US." — as if to say: the prophet spoke not madly but seriously and on a matter of great moment, and we altogether desire that thou relate it to us. Whence the Chaldean renders: "Thou speakest falsely; tell us now." They suspected by divine instinct what was in fact the truth, namely that Jehu had received from the prophet an oracle of kingship, and they press to extract it from him; whence follows: "THEY HASTED THEREFORE, AND EVERY MAN TAKING HIS CLOAK" (his outer garment) "PUT IT UNDER HIS FEET IN THE MANNER OF A TRIBUNAL." The Septuagint has: upon the ascent of the steps; Vatablus: at the top of the steps, that is, on the highest and most elevated place — as if to say, on a lofty place they erected for him with their garments a kind of royal throne, that he might sit upon it as a king; for by this rite they made him king. Indeed, at the word of the Prophet which the princes had heard from Jehu, these men and the soldiers, divinely stirred, conferred the kingship upon him, and therefore hurriedly constructed certain steps which they covered with their cloaks as with tapestries, and placed Jehu at the summit of them, and acclaimed him: "Jehu reigneth" — as if to say: Jehu is our king; long may he live and reign prosperously for many years. Thus Plutarch writes of Cato of Utica in his Life, that garments were strewn by the soldiers along those places which he was about to tread with his feet — a form of honour which he records as customarily paid to generals. And in certain of the sacred rites of their gods, when they were led forth in solemn procession, garments were accustomed to be strewn, as the same Plutarch writes in his Life of Alcibiades. The same regarding the rites of Juno is reported by Athenaeus, book 12, chapter 9. So also to Christ, as Messiah and king, when he entered Jerusalem in solemn procession on Palm Sunday, the crowd strewed the way both with palm branches and with garments, Matthew 21.
As if to say: I bring not peace but war; for I have conspired against thy king Joram, to slay him and to seize his kingdom.

This judgment of God was just and fitting, who gathered them into the field of Naboth so that they might be struck down there — in the very place where Ahab, their father, had stripped Naboth of his vineyard and had him killed, as God himself had foretold through Elijah (3 Kings 21:23).

He means, namely, the idolatry and sorcery of Jezebel. For idolatry is a mystical fornication, by which one abandons God the spouse and cleaves to idols, as to adulterers. Sorcery is of the same nature, because the priests of idols are magicians; for they consult the demon and receive oracles from him. Jehu says this, meaning as it were: I come on behalf of God the judge, to punish your idolatry and sorcery, and that of your mother Jezebel.
Burden (onus) is the name given to a threatening and burdensome prophecy, by which God had foretold through Elijah that the posterity of Ahab would be slain on account of his crimes (3 Kings 21:23).

This refers to the extermination of the house of Ahab, foretold and decreed by God. For Ahaziah, king of Judah, was, through his mother Athaliah, a grandson of Ahab, and he imitated his crimes and idolatry. You may object that in 2 Chronicles 22:9 it is said that Ahaziah fled and hid in Samaria. The answer is this: on this occasion he fled and hid in Samaria, but, as the same passage adds, he was afterwards discovered in Samaria, brought to Jehu at Gaver, and having been struck there he fled to Megiddo, where he died. So Lyranus.
He began to reign, that is, he began his reign, and he reigned scarcely one year; for in the twelfth year he was slain together with Joram by Jehu.

Not in order to entice Jehu into love for her. For she was an old woman, and she knew that Jehu was her sworn enemy, but rather to display boldness of spirit, as though she neither feared nor cared about Jehu; hence she rebuked him and called him the murderer of her lord (King Joram), so as to strike him with dismay. For she supposed that Jehu would not dare to lay hands upon her, being as she was a woman and a queen, nor to stain himself with her blood. But if he should dare, she wished to die with courage and nobility, as one who despised death. This is the vanity of the world and of worldly people, just as the vanity of women is their stibium and rouge — which Magdalene, when she repented, accordingly despised; for as St. Jerome says in his Letter to Furia: "She had no crimped head-dresses, no creaking sandals, no eyes ringed with sooty stibium." Hence St. Cyprian, in his tract On Works and Almsgiving, says: "Anoint your eyes, not with the devil's stibium, but with the eye-salve of Christ."

As if to say: what peace or fellowship can there be with Jehu, who is like another tyrant Zimri? For just as Zimri slew Elah his king (3 Kings 16:10) and seized his kingdom, so Jehu has slain Joram his king and seizes his kingdom. # GEM

He alludes to the etymology of the name Jezabel. For Jezabel in Hebrew is the same as *hy zebel*, that is, "Where is the dung?" For *zebel* in Chaldaic, Syriac, and Arabic means the same as dung. Or Jezabel is the same as *zebel*, that is, "island of the dunghill," or "woe to the dunghill," as Pagninus says. Hence some think that *Baelzebul*, that is, dung, is the name by which the devil is contemptuously called *Beelzebul*, that is, lord of the dunghill, and that the Greeks call him Beelzebub, as though "god of dung"; for upon dung sits the *zebub*, that is, the fly. Moreover, St. Ambrose, in his Exhortation to Virgins, says: "Jezabel is the same as an overflow of vanity, or vain and empty excess, such as is found in avarice, ambition, and all manner of concupiscence, of which Jezabel is the very embodiment." — "Is this that Jezabel?" — as if to say: Has fortune, reversing all things, cast Jezabel down from the summit of glory into the depths of ignominy? She was once the beauty and splendor of the world; now she rots like vile dung. She was once the wife and mother of kings, indeed their mistress and ruler; behold, she is now torn apart by dogs. She was once the terror of Elijah and the prophets, indeed of all Israel; see now how she is trampled by the hooves of horses. St. Chrysostom notes, in Homily 25 on the Epistle to the Romans, that Jezabel was punished more severely than Ahab, who fell honorably in battle pierced by an arrow, because Jezabel had been the occasion of all the evil, indeed its suggester and instigator: "Jezabel," he says, "paid a greater penalty than Ahab, the ravisher of the vineyard; for she herself had woven the whole affair and had given the king occasion to fall." So too the Blessed Francis Borgia, formerly Duke of Gandia, when he was escorting the body of Isabella, wife of the deceased Charles V, to the royal tomb at Granada, beheld it so disfigured, foul, and horrible that he could not recognize it, nor dare to swear that this was the body of the Empress. Wherefore, gazing upon it with fixed eyes in wonder, he said: "Is this Isabella, Empress of the world? Is this that wise and holy Isabella, the joy of Spain, the glory of the Empire, the hope of the world? Where is that former beauty of countenance, where the splendor of her eyes, where the majesty of the royal crown? Has her flesh thus been turned to corruption, her beauty to foulness, her splendor to horror?" Therefore God illumined his mind, and he entered the Society of Jesus, a man of singular virtue, carefully considering how great was the vanity of all kingdoms — both for religious and for secular persons alike — and of all the goods of the world; he resolved to bid them farewell and to serve God alone, and he gave an example even to princes and kings, as is clear from his life.

Not from Jezebel alone, but from many other wives were these children begotten. "TO THE GUARDIANS OF ACHAB" — that is, as the Septuagint renders it, to the foster-fathers of the sons of Achab, namely to the chief men who had raised the seventy sons of Achab. "BEHOLD, TWO KINGS (Joram king of Israel, and Ochozias king of Juda, for Jehu had slain both) WERE NOT ABLE TO STAND BEFORE HIM" — that is, to resist him; whence it follows: "And how shall we (private men and commoners) be able to RESIST?"
Who were, that is, set over the royal palace which Omri had built in Samaria, and in which Achab had dwelt, and his son Joram, who was slain by Jehu.

Jehu did not sin in commanding the seventy sons of Achab to be slain, because he did this by the command of God, who is the sovereign Lord over the life of the guilty as well as the innocent, on account of their being the sons of the wicked Achab, whose entire seed and whole lineage the Lord wished to destroy root and branch, inasmuch as he was most villainous. For it is probable that among these seventy there were some who were younger and innocent, and neither capable of nor participants in Achab's idolatry. Nevertheless the Samaritans sinned in carrying out this command of Jehu concerning their execution, not as ministers of a Judge, but out of fear of Jehu, whom they did not know to be a king chosen by God and anointed by Eliseus, and therefore they did not know him to be the Judge of Israel, nor that he held from God the power to put to death these sons of Achab. Perhaps also Jehu sinned in commanding them to commit this deed, if he had not first sufficiently demonstrated to them his authority and the right of kingship given to him by God — unless they could easily have come to know this from another source. So Abulensis. "AND THEY PLACED THEIR HEADS IN BASKETS." This is fittingly related to the guilt of Achab, who had gathered grapes in baskets from the vineyard of Naboth whom he had slain; therefore, as a punishment for the sin of his sons, their heads are carried to Jehu in baskets. In that very thing, then, in which both he and his sons had sinned, both he and his sons are punished.
So that all the people entering and going out through the gate might see this just judgment of God against the house of Achab, and thereby turn away from his idolatry and the worship of Baal.

That is, the just vengeance of God — you have fulfilled it, though unknowingly and without deliberate intent. For they thought they were fulfilling Jehu's command to kill those men, not the command of God. "IF I CONSPIRED AGAINST MY LORD" (Joram, king of Israel) "AND KILLED HIM, WHO STRUCK DOWN ALL THESE?" — as if to say: not I, but you, even if at my command and out of fear of me. As if to say: from this act of vengeance, the judgment of God against the impious house of Ahab is made plainly manifest; namely, that God used both me and you to carry it out. For although I killed Joram, I could not have killed his seventy brothers, had God not cast into you a fear of me, so that at my command you would slay them. Otherwise you could have resisted my command — either by force, or by some honorable excuse, or by concealing these sons of Ahab whom you killed, or by sending them away elsewhere. God therefore took away your better judgment, lest you do any of these things, but rather that you should kill them.

All — that is, nearly all, because a few, and those nearly all poor, abject, and unwarlike, were left behind. There also remained the brothers of Ochozias, king of Judah, born of Athalia the daughter of Ahab, whom he kills a little later at verse 14. There also remained Joas, who succeeded his father Ochozias in the kingdom of Judah. In like manner Scripture said that none should be left from the house of Jeroboam, Baasha, Elah, and others — because all those of greater standing were removed, and only a few of the lowest sort were left. "THE BROTHERS OF OCHOZIAS" — that is, the sons of the brothers of Ochozias, as is explained in 2 Chronicles 22:9; for the brothers of Ochozias were killed by the Arabs, as recorded in 2 Chronicles.

Namely of Ochozias, king of Israel, and of his wife and queen. "THEY SLAUGHTERED THEM" — as being the descendants of the impious Ahab, born of an impious mother, Athalia. Jehu did this by the impulse of God, whence he says to Jonadab: "See my zeal for the Lord." So Abulensis. "NEAR THE SHEEPFOLD" — of the shepherds, as was said at verse 12. The Chaldean renders it: at the house of the assembly of shepherds; the Hebrew: at the house of binding, in which, that is, sheep were tied for shearing — as if to say, at the sheep-shearing house. So Pagninus. Perhaps at that time sheep were being shorn there, and therefore joyful banquets were being arranged in the customary manner, to which these sons of the brothers of Ochozias were invited, and were killed there amid the feasting — just as in similar circumstances Amnon was killed by his brother Absalom (2 Kings 13:15). "JEHU MET JONADAB THE SON OF RECHAB COMING TO MEET HIM, AND HE BLESSED HIM" — that is, he wished him well, saluted him after the Hebrew custom by saying: peace be to you. This Jonadab is the one who is praised in Jeremiah chapter 35:6, where Jeremiah, offering wine to the Rechabites, the descendants and disciples of Jonadab, hears from them: "We will not drink wine, because Jonadab the son of Rechab our father commanded us, saying: You shall drink no wine, neither you nor your sons, forever; and you shall build no house, and sow no seed, and plant no vineyard, nor have one; but you shall dwell in tents all your days, that you may live many days in the land in which you sojourn." John of Jerusalem, in his book On the Institution of Monks, chapter 25, asserts that this Jonadab was a disciple of Elisha, and that he followed the pattern of his religious life — with the exception of continence — together with his sons and grandsons. These Rechabites were Kenites, descendants of Jethro the Midianite, father-in-law of Moses. See what was said on Jeremiah 35:6. "IS YOUR HEART RIGHT, AS MY HEART IS WITH YOUR HEART?" — as if to say: do you love me sincerely and from the soul, as I love you? From this it appears that Jehu, before his reign, was an upright man, being a friend and intimate of Jonadab, a man of probity and piety. For friendship does not take root between the upright and the wicked, since it requires those who are like unto itself. "AND HE TOOK HIM UP INTO HIS CHARIOT AND BROUGHT HIM TO SAMARIA" — so that he might win for himself authority among the Samaritans and a reputation for integrity through Jonadab as his companion, whom all regarded as a man of wholeness and holiness; and thus that he might more easily carry out the slaughter of the Baalites which he was planning, and that no one would dare resist. # GEM

Jehu lies, but it is an officious lie, and directed toward a good end — namely, to gather together all the worshippers of Baal in one place and destroy them, which God had commanded him, and which could scarcely have been accomplished by any other means. Hence he himself believed this lie to be lawful for him, indeed necessary, honorable, and pious. So Theodoret and Procopius. For although this lie of his was, by its very nature, pernicious — being a simulation, indeed a profession of idolatry, and therefore a public scandal — as St. Thomas teaches (II-II, q. 111, art. 1, ad 2), Francisco Suárez (Tract. 1, De Fide, disp. 14, sect. 4, n. 9), and Gregory of Valencia (Tract. De Fide, disp. 1, q. 3, puncto 2) — indeed St. Augustine, in his book Against Lying, chapter 2, expressly calls this lie of Jehu impious, and therefore asserts that Jehu was praised and rewarded by God not for that simulation, but for his obedience, because he killed the posterity of Ahab at God's command. He adds: "It seemed to certain Catholics that they ought to simulate being Priscillianists in order to penetrate their hiding places; which I, forbidding," says Augustine, "wrote the book Against Lying." Nevertheless there are many things which excuse Jehu, and which show that his lie was rather officious than pernicious. The first is the intention of the speaker — namely, that he said what he said not in earnest, for he did not intend to worship Baal, but fictitiously and by simulation, so as to gather all the worshippers of Baal into one place, and having gathered them, to slaughter them all as victims of divine justice, as God had commanded him — which he could not otherwise have accomplished.
