Hence St. Jerome, in his commentary on chapter 2 of the Epistle to the Galatians, entirely excuses Jehu, and teaches that simulation is useful and sometimes to be employed, provided that lying be carefully avoided: "That useful simulation," says Jerome, "ought to be assumed in its proper time — this the example of King Jehu teaches us." Abulensis, Cajetan, and others are of the same opinion; indeed Adrianus, in this question on Baptism, response to the fifth objection, says it is lawful to conceal the faith by professing a false religion, if there be a just cause — though others commonly disapprove and refute this, and rightly so. The second consideration is that this simulation and feigning of Jehu lasted for the briefest possible time; hence the scandal was slight, for all would very shortly see that the mind of the speaker was utterly contrary to what he had expressed: Jehu therefore counted the delay of a short moment as nothing. The third consideration is that he did not command or exhort the Baalites to sacrifice to Baal, but only said: "I WILL WORSHIP HIM MORE GREATLY" — by which words he intended only to give the Baalites occasion to open their minds and to reveal the malice of the idolatry which they harbored in their hearts, so that he might then cut it down entirely. Thus physicians administer drugs which stir up corrupt humors, so as afterward to expel them as they break forth, and thereby restore health to the man. Thus princes send spies to their enemies, who feign themselves their friends, so as to discover their counsels and report them back to the princes — as Chusai did when he went over to Absalom and there dissipated the counsels of Achitophel (2 Kings 15:14). Thus masters test the faithfulness of their menservants and maidservants by placing coins here and there, to see whether they will take them and steal them, or faithfully bring them back to their master. These, then, do give occasion for theft, yet they do not incite or invite to it. You will say: Jehu by his own example implicitly invited the Baalites to sacrifice to Baal. I reply: Jehu did not invite them, but only gave them occasion for sacrifice (as I have already said concerning masters), which he knew they would seize upon — and he permitted this, so that what they did daily in private, they might now do publicly, and thus expose themselves, that they might be punished and chastened by him. For it was better that one act...

That is, he proclaimed a solemn and sacred day, on which we shall solemnly worship Baal: but you, O worshippers of Baal, shall worship him formally with sacrifices of oxen, while I shall worship him materially only by slaughtering and delivering you yourselves as victims to the demon to whom you have dedicated and enslaved yourselves. And truly the demon held a solemn feast when he seized the souls and bodies of so many idolaters, slaughtered like his own servants by Jehu. They came therefore, having been summoned, not to a banquet but to the slaughterhouse of their solemn feast. Thus did the devil, at the death of Luther, proclaim a solemn festival for the demons subject to him, and so summoned all from round about to escort his soul in triumph to the depths of hell. This was confessed by the demons who possessed the demoniacs at Gheel, a city in Brabant between Antwerp and Louvain, where the holy Dymphna, virgin and martyr, slain by her father, the king of Ireland, for the sake of her virginity, rests in peace and shines forth with many miracles, especially in the freeing of demoniacs from the demon — as I myself once witnessed with my own eyes when I visited the sacred relics of this holy virgin. At Gheel, I say, on the very day that Luther died, all the demoniacs were freed from their demons; but on the following day they were again possessed and tormented by the same. When the demons were asked the reason, they said that on the previous day they had been summoned to the funeral of their great friend Martin Luther, had attended it, and had celebrated a feast; for the prince of their number, the archdemon, had commanded that all evil spirits should gather for the obsequies of their prophet and faithful fellow-worker Luther — for it was fitting that one who had led so very many down to hell should be solemnly conducted thither by the greatest possible number. This is related, among others, by Tilmann Bredenbachius, in book 7 of his *Collationes Sacrae*, chapter 39, who also adds that an innumerable flock of crows, croaking in a horrible manner (these were the demons), had flown over Luther's corpse and accompanied it all the way from Eisleben to Wittenberg to the tomb. A similar festival was celebrated by the demons at the death of Udo, Archbishop of Magdeburg, as the same Bredenbachius relates at length in book 8, chapter 14, drawing on Nauclerus, Fulgosus, and Father Canisius.

By "servants" he means the devoted worshippers of Baal, especially the priests, as is clear from verse 19; hence also in the following verse he commanded that garments be given to them, namely priestly and sacred vestments. For it does not seem that all those who worshipped Baal came, or that a single temple could have contained them all. So in Acts 17:4, "worshippers" denotes devout and religious men who piously worship God. Abulensis, however, holds that all who professed the worship of Baal were summoned and came to the solemn day of Baal; for although in the time of Ahab they were very numerous, yet shortly afterward, under the pious King Jehoshaphat, many of them returned to the worship of the one true God. *And the house of Baal was filled from one end to the other* — that is, from the farthest point to the farthest. The Septuagint reads: "And the house was filled to the brim"; the Chaldean: "from corner to corner."

That is, priestly or sacred garments, such as were used by the Levites and the Nathinaeans, or ministers of the priests; he did not therefore give them secular clothing as a reward. For this would have been a great enticement to idolatry and sin — that men should falsely claim to be worshippers of Baal in exchange for garments. Abulensis adds that Jehu supplied them with victims for sacrifice and invited them to sacrifice, and that this was without fault. But this cannot be maintained: for to invite someone to sacrifice to an idol is a fault and a crime, whatever the end in view. It sufficed for Jehu, therefore, that he permitted those who were already prepared to sacrifice to go ahead and do so.

He declared, says Josephus, that no one alien and profane with respect to their rites should intrude among them. Jehu provided for this wisely, so that no innocent worshipper of God should be slaughtered, but only the guilty worshippers of Baal. *AND HIS LIFE SHALL BE FOR THE LIFE OF THE OTHER.* Whoever, says Josephus, permits any one of them to escape shall die in his place as a substitute.

The Hebrew, Chaldean, and the Roman Septuagint have it in the singular: *When he had finished*, namely the high priest of Baal. The Royal and Complutensian Septuagint have it in the plural: *And it came to pass that when they had completed the offering of the holocaust* — both readings are true, for several priests were offering several holocausts upon several altars. *And the soldiers and commanders of Jehu cast out their bodies. And they went into the city of the temple of Baal*, verse 26, *and they brought forth the statue from the shrine of Baal and burned it.* There appears to have been a certain city properly dedicated to Baal, from whose temple they brought out the statue of Baal and burned it. Vatablus and Salianus understand by "city" multiple cities, so that it is an enallage of number, as if to say: By the command and commission of Jehu they went into whatever cities of Israel and brought out from them the idols of Baal and burned them. That they did this is altogether probable; for as it follows: *Jehu therefore destroyed Baal out of Israel* — which he would not have done had he destroyed him in one city alone. Finally, it could be a hypallage: *they went into the city of the temple*, that is, they went into the temple of the city. For this temple, built by Ahab in Samaria, was vast and spacious, having various attached dwellings in the manner of a small city — as if to say: After all the worshippers of Baal had been slain in the temple, Jehu with his men entered the temple itself, brought out the statue of Baal and burned it. Hence the Royal and Complutensian Septuagint do not have "the city," but only *they went as far as the temple of Baal* — which reading is very apposite and fitting to this passage. Verse 29: *Nevertheless from the sins* (namely, the adoration of the golden calves) *of Jeroboam, etc., he did not depart.* Remarkable was this blindness, or rather this folly, of Jehu and of the other kings of Israel: that while they saw themselves commanded by God to destroy the houses of Ahab and of other kings on account of idol-worship, they themselves nonetheless worshipped idols of the same kind — if not the same in number, certainly the same in species — and therefore felt the same avenging hand of God, and were cut off together with their posterity. The cause was a political one: they feared that if the people returned to God to be worshipped in Jerusalem, they would likewise return to the house of David and to the kings reigning in Judah. But this fear of theirs was vain; for they had God as their guarantee. He had promised that if they worshipped Him alone, their kingdom would be stable and would be propagated to their posterity for many ages.

The event corresponded to the promise of God: for Jehu reigned twenty-eight years; his son Jehoahaz succeeded him for seventeen years; his son Joash for sixteen; his son Jeroboam for forty-one; his son Zechariah for six months. Moreover, the Hebrews are of the opinion that the prophet who made this promise to Jehu was Jonah, the fourth of the minor prophets. But Jonah flourished not under Jehu, but under Jeroboam, the grandson of Jehu. Therefore Nicephorus in his *Chronicle* and Salianus more correctly hold that this prophet was Elisha, who had first anointed Jehu as king, as was stated in chapter 9, verses 1 and 2. It appears that Jehu received this promise from God as a reward immediately after the slaying of the Baalites and the overthrow of Baal — although Abulensis holds that it was given after Jehu began to worship the calves, and that therefore God limited this promise to the fourth generation. But God punished the worship of the calves forthwith through Hazael, king of Syria, who invaded the land of Israel, as is immediately added below: that time, therefore, was a time not of reward but of punishment. Finally, against the idols and calves of Jehu, God sent prophets who dictated and wrote their oracles and threats against him and against Israel, which applauded him in his wickedness. The first of these was Hosea, who began to prophesy under Jehu or shortly after Jehu. Hence in chapter 1 he says: *Yet a little while, and I will visit the blood of Jezreel upon the house of Jehu*; and in chapter 7, verse 1: *When I would have healed Israel, the iniquity of Ephraim was revealed* — meaning Jehu and the ten tribes, of which Ephraim was the head. He assigns as the cause the people's propensity toward idols, hardened by custom, which Jehu's idolatry nourished and promoted. Hence he says in verse 3: *In their malice they made the king glad, etc.; the city rested a little from the mingling of the leaven, until the whole was leavened, etc. Because they applied their heart like an oven, while he lay in wait for them.* See the notes there.

— burning cities, slaying young men, dashing infants to pieces, ripping open pregnant women, as Elisha had foretold them in chapter 8, verse 12. COMMENTARY ON THE FOURTH BOOK OF KINGS. Chapter XI. CHAPTER THE ELEVENTH.

That is, let him guard the gate of the temple facing toward the palace. So Abulensis, Cajetan, and others. Joiada divided the soldiers into three parts, so that they might guard three places necessary for the protection of the new king and for the seizure of Athaliah. Moreover, Joiada drew the greater part of these soldiers from the Levites, over whom he himself presided as High Priest, so that the conspiracy might be more secret and more secure; for if he had enlisted laymen, Athaliah would immediately have learned of it and suppressed them. For this reason too he detained on the Sabbath those Levites who, having completed their weekly rotation, were about to return home by custom, so that he might assemble from them a greater number of soldiers. For this reason likewise he took five centurions from the Levites, who would gather to him the remaining Levites scattered throughout the cities of Judah — namely Azariah the son of Jeroham, Ishmael the son of Johanan, Azariah the son of Obed, Maaseiah the son of Adaiah, and Elishaphat the son of Zichri, as is said in 2 Chronicles 23:1. Moreover, these Levites came to the temple without arms, as though about to minister there; but Joiada the High Priest gave them on the spot spears, lances, shields, and bucklers, as is likewise said in verse 9 there and verse 10 here.
This is the gate by which one descended from the house of the Lord, that is from the temple, into the palace, as is clear from verse 3; which in 2 Chronicles 23:20 is called the upper gate.

Note that on each Sabbath the ministers — namely priests and Levites — were accustomed to be changed in their service at the temple, so that week by week each in his turn might minister in succession, according to the order prescribed by David in 1 Chronicles 24:3 and following. Joiada therefore detained both groups — that is, the ministers of both weeks, namely the one finishing its service and the other beginning — and the newly incoming ministers for the service of the temple he divided into three parts or companies, assigning to each the guarding of one gate; while the outgoing or departing ministers he distributed into two parts, which were to guard the young boy Joash the king, as is stated in this verse. So Abulensis.

For the strictest military discipline of the guard had to be maintained here, lest either the ranks be thrown into disorder by a crowd pressing in, or Athaliah send her own soldiers or servants to intercept or kill the boy king.

Concerning the word "diadem," the Hebrew is נֵזֶר (*nezer*), which the Septuagint renders as *ἁγίασμα*, that is, "sanctification," by which Theodoret understands the chrism. But the matter of the chrism and the anointing of Joash follows a little further on; and *nezer* signifies a diadem or royal crown, which the Septuagint calls a "sanctification" because it was preserved in the holy temple, to be placed upon the head of a faithful king of Israel as one chosen and sanctified by God. So Abulensis. By "testimony," Pagninus and Vatablus understand the royal garment, namely the purple robe or purple military cloak. Others more correctly take it to mean the book of Deuteronomy: for God had commanded in Deuteronomy 17, verses 18 and 19, that this should be given to the new king, so that from it the king might learn that it was his duty both to worship God with piety and to govern his subjects with benevolence after the manner of a father. So Abulensis, Cajetan, and others, and St. Jerome in his commentary on 2 Paralipomenon. That this is so is clear from 2 Paralipomenon 23, verse 11, where it is plainly explained thus: *They placed in his hand*, it says, *the Law to be held*. For this ceremony at the inauguration of a king had been prescribed by God in Deuteronomy 17. For the Law, or Deuteronomy (that is, the second law, being the repetition of the law made by Moses in Deuteronomy), is called a "testimony" — namely, of the divine will — because in it God declared and made known what He wished the Hebrews to do. All these things Jehoiada rightfully carried out: first, because he was the uncle of the boy Joash to whom the kingdom was owed, and therefore, as his guardian, was bound to take care that it be wrested from Athaliah, who had usurped it, and restored to Joash as king, and was obliged to administer it in the place of Joash his ward; secondly, because he was the High Priest, to whom, in the absence of a king or prince, belonged the care not only of the Synagogue but also of the commonwealth. It was therefore his responsibility to ensure that Athaliah, the enemy of Israel, who was subverting both the commonwealth and religion, be removed from the kingdom, and that another prince or king, to whom it rightfully belonged, be installed, who would preserve and advance both. **The people of the land** — that is, the common people; for this is the lowly sort, cast down as it were to the earth, and given over to earthly things.
That is, with hands applied by force they dragged her out of the temple, so that they might kill her outside, lest the temple be defiled by the slaughter and blood of so impious a woman.

Who these men were I have explained in Book 2, Chapter 8. The Hebrew has *commanders and runners*, that is, lightly armed soldiers, such as skirmishers. Our text names them the Cherethites and Pelethites, because these were the praetorian guard and the most valiant soldiers, who were most necessary for this undertaking.

That is, near the house or palace of the king, as was said at verse 16. It was fitting that she be slain there where she had sinned; for she had seized the palace and the kingdom. Therefore what Josephus says in Book 9, Chapter 7 — that Athaliah was dragged to the torrent of Cedron and slain there — is not accurate, though it is possible that the body of the slain woman was cast into the torrent of Cedron. Similar to Athaliah and Jezebel in impiety and equally in the vengeance of Heaven, in the time of St. Gregory the Great, Pontiff, was Brunhilda, mother and grandmother of many kings of Francia. For in order that she might reign perpetually and die as queen, she kept Theuderic the king — her own grandson, entangled as he was in the loves of concubines — under her influence, and on this account expelled St. Columbanus from Gaul when he rebuked him, and killed Desiderius, Bishop of Vienne, and Delphinus, Bishop of Lyons, and through her agency and counsel extinguished ten kings. But at last she was captured by King Clothar, and first placed upon a camel and led about as a spectacle for all the people; then, tied by her hair to the tail of an unbroken horse and dragged along, her brains were scattered by the horse's hooves, and her remaining limbs, drawn over rough rocks, thorns, and crags, were torn asunder — as Baronius recounts from Aimoinus, under the year of Christ 614.

That is, of God's temple, so that they might restore those things which had been consumed by the long passage of time and decay, corroded and fallen into ruin, and which Athaliah had torn down and carried away. For as it is said in 2 Chronicles 24:7: *Athaliah, that most wicked woman, and her sons destroyed the house of God, and with all the things that had been sanctified in the temple of God they adorned the shrine of Baalim.*

The priests were sinning in two ways: first, in that they were negligent in collecting the money owed as tribute to the temple according to the law of Exodus 30:12; and second, in that they were spending it not on the maintenance and repair of the temple, but on other purposes, and indeed on private and personal uses. For this reason King Joash rightly remonstrated with them, and took from them the duty of collecting and safeguarding the money for the building work of the temple, and commanded them to bring the money into the treasury, as stated in verse 9.

Not unwillingly, but willingly and with consent. Hence they were prohibited. The Hebrew is *וַיֹּאֹתוּ*, that is, they agreed, they were willing, they assented. The Chaldean renders it: they were content; because, as Josephus says, they knew that many among the people were reluctant to pay the tribute, and that no one willingly gives out silver.

That is, for as long as the restoration of the temple continued — for this, being the more necessary work, had to take precedence; once that was completed, the remainder of the money was spent on ewers and other vessels of the temple, as is clear from 2 Chronicles 24. So say Abulensis, Cajetan, and others.

That is to say, no account or reckoning was demanded of the receivers of the money, because all knew that they were handling it in good faith — namely, that they were expending everything on the fabric of the Temple, and were taking nothing for their own private uses. So the Chaldean paraphrase, Vatablus, and others.

Vatablus explains: The money that remained after the purchase of a victim for a trespass offering or sin offerings was not brought into the treasury of the Lord, because it belonged to the priests. For those who dwelt far from the Temple would send not a victim but money to one of the priests, who would purchase the victim; and whatever money remained after the victim had been bought would accrue to the benefit of the priests. So Vatablus. The Abulensis, however, explains it thus — as though the text here treats of a trespass or sin committed against a priest: for instance, if someone had taken away what belonged to a priest by right of the sacrifices; for in that case, beyond the restitution of the loss sustained and the offering of a victim for that sin, one was also obliged to offer the priest a fifth part in addition, in money. Hear the law in Leviticus 5:15: *If a soul shall sin through ignorance in the holy things of the Lord, he shall offer for his trespass an unblemished ram from the flocks, worth two shekels according to the weight of the sanctuary; and he shall make restitution for the harm he has done, and shall add a fifth part thereto, giving it to the priest.* The same regarding the trespass offering is decreed in Numbers 5:6. What the difference is between a trespass offering and a sin offering I have explained at Leviticus 7:1.

Because God wished to chastise King Joash by means of Hazael — for Joash, after the death of Jehoiada, had been corrupted by the counsellors of Athaliah and had turned aside to idols, and had accordingly slain Zechariah the son of Jehoiada because he rebuked him for his idolatry — and on account of this sacrilegious murder Joash himself was slain by his own servants and was denied royal burial; all of which is related at length in 2 Chronicles 24. Furthermore, to accomplish this chastisement of idolaters, God had commanded Elijah to anoint Hazael as king of Syria, in 1 Kings 19:16, 18. *Wherefore Joash king of Judah took all the sanctified things which Jehoshaphat* etc. *had consecrated, and all the gold that could be found in the treasuries of the Temple of the Lord and in the royal palace, and sent them to Hazael king of Syria; and he withdrew from Jerusalem.* From this example, Cajetan observes that the money and treasures of churches may lawfully be expended to purchase relief for a people from oppression, such as was the siege of Jerusalem here — yet always together with the entire royal treasury. For it would be unjust to begin the ransom by plundering the churches; but where the public and royal treasury does not suffice, the wealth of the churches ought to come to the aid and make up the deficiency. So Cajetan. Hear also Saint Ambrose, in *On Offices*, Book 2, chapter 28, where he enumerates three cases in which it is lawful to convert sacred vessels to lay uses: *Certainly, if one diverts them to his own profit, it is a crime; but if he distributes them to the poor, or ransoms a captive, it is an act of mercy. For no one can say, why does the poor man live? No one can complain that captives have been ransomed; no one can accuse, because the Temple of God has been built; no one can be indignant because space has been enlarged for the burial of the remains of the faithful; no one can grieve because rest has been provided for the deceased in the graves of Christians. For these three purposes it is lawful to break, to melt down, and to sell even the consecrated vessels of the Church.* These words of Saint Ambrose have been transcribed into Canon Law, and are found at Cause 12, Question 2, canon *Aurum*, §*Nemo potest*.

In the time of Joash king of Judah — namely, in the tenth year of his reign — Carthage, the rival of Rome, is said to have been founded by Dido, who sailed from Tyre during the reign of Pygmalion and established that city in Africa. This is recorded by Josephus from the annals of the Tyrians, in Book 1 against Apion, and he is followed by Torniellius, Gordonus, Salianus, and Scaliger, though others assign its founding to a different time. There are indeed various opinions among various scholars on this matter. According to this reckoning, Carthage was founded one hundred and thirty years before Rome. Again, in the fourteenth year of Joash, Lycurgus gave his laws to the Spartans — laws which the Gentiles celebrate, but which are far inferior to Christian laws; for they permit women to live licentiously and to commit adultery, and allow boys and young men to steal, so that they might become cunning, and so that each person might guard his own property more diligently. For this reason Aristotle disapproves and censures those laws in Book 2 of the Politics, chapter 9. In that same year of Joash, the Olympic games began, and from them the Olympiads were named and calculated — instituted, or rather restored, by Iphitus king of Elis, who was a contemporary of Lycurgus; as Salianus teaches from Plutarch's Life of Lycurgus, from Aristotle, and from Pausanias. The names of the victors in the Olympic games, however, began to be recorded only after 27 Olympiads, namely in the 28th Olympiad, in which the first recorded victor is Coroebus. For this reason, the Olympiads are commonly numbered from this 28th Olympiad, and what is called the first is that which, according to Salianus's reckoning, falls in the first year of Jotham king of Judah, which was the year of the world 3278, and 775 years before Christ — though others assign these years differently; for nowhere is there greater diversity of opinions than in chronology.

Who is this saviour? Some hold that it was the guardian Angel, the tutelary protector of Israel, who fought and conquered on his behalf against the Syrians, just as an Angel fought for Hezekiah against Sennacherib, slaying 185,000 Assyrians. Others maintain it was a man and a specific commander sent by God, but unnamed in Scripture, and therefore unknown and uncertain to us. More plausibly, others hold that it was Elisha — so Cajetan — or at least Joash king of Israel, who was strengthened by Elisha and overcame the Syrians on three occasions, as follows. Wherefore throughout all the time of Jehoahaz, Israel was afflicted by the Syrians, as is said in verse 22. But after Jehoahaz, his successor Joash subdued the Syrians and restored freedom to Israel. Hence Jehoahaz, praying to God for the salvation of Israel, obtained it — not for the time of his own reign, but for the reign of his son Joash. So Abulensis.

This verse confirms what I have already said; wherefore it must be connected with verse 3, and placed before the verse where, by hyperbaton, the supplication of Jehoahaz is interposed, along with its being heard, and the promise of the deliverance to be accomplished through Joash. So Sanchez. *And he had reduced them like the dust in threshing* — that is, Hazael had crushed and ground down the Israelites, as chaff and dust are wont to be crushed and ground upon the threshing floor. So Abulensis.

Jehoahaz, although conquered by Hazael as by a more powerful adversary, nevertheless conducted himself bravely in battle and defended himself as best he could. **Verse 14: But Elisha was fallen sick of his sickness whereof he died; and Joash the king of Israel came down unto him, and wept over his face, and said: O my father, my father, the chariot of Israel and the horsemen thereof.** That is to say: Thou, O Elisha, art my father and the father of Israel, inasmuch as thou didst defend me and my kingdom more effectually than a great multitude of horsemen and chariots; thou therefore, while living, didst as it were drive and govern the kingdom of Israel as its charioteer; now that thou art dying, who shall defend me and mine against Hazael and the Syrians? These same words Elisha himself had cried out to Elijah when he was carried up into heaven, chapter 2, verse 12 — see the notes there. **Verse 16: And he said to the king of Israel: Put thine hand upon the bow. And when he had put his hand upon it, Elisha put his hands upon the king's hands** — the Regia edition wrongly reads *supposuit* [placed beneath] rather than *superposuit* [placed upon] — in order to signify that his own assistance would be with him, even when absent, yea, even when departed from this life; for God, on account of his own merits, would grant him victory; and that he himself, in limbo, would pray to God for that victory and would assuredly obtain it. After death, therefore, the power and virtue of Elisha remained living, and the wonders which he had been accustomed to work while alive, he continued to work even after death. So Abulensis, Cajetan, and others. From this let heretics learn that the Saints — especially those who reign in heaven after Christ — are mighty through God, so that they are both able and willing to aid us when we invoke them. Let princes and other men also learn from this example how greatly the prayer and merits of holy men avail, and that unless they themselves place their hands upon our hands and our works, we labour in vain. *Tropologically:* Let the Doctor apply the hand of good works to the mouth of preaching, so that what he teaches in word he may teach first by example. So shall his preaching be efficacious, and shall persuade his hearers of what he proclaims. So Angelomus and Eucherius. **Verse 17: Open the window toward the east** — that thou mayest shoot the arrow of victory against the Syrians, who lie to the east of Israel. *And when he had opened it, Elisha said: Shoot an arrow; and he shot it. And Elisha said: The arrow of the Lord's deliverance, and the arrow of deliverance against Syria* — that is to say: I pray God to prosper, direct, and strengthen thine arrows and the arrows of thy soldiers (which I represent by this my arrow and its flight), and thy arms, thy fighting, and thy battles, so that they may strike down, slay, and subdue the Syrians; nor do I merely pray this, but as a prophet speaking from the mouth of God I now foretell it to thee and promise thee victory over Syria. Hence he adds: *And thou shalt smite Syria in Aphek.* He alludes to the ancient custom by which men declared war upon enemies and commenced battle by casting an arrow or javelin into hostile territory, as the Romans did — as Servius attests on those words of Turnus in Virgil, Aeneid 9: *Behold, he cries, and whirling his javelin launches it into the air — the opening of battle.* The Greeks did likewise; whence, as soon as Alexander the Great first touched Persia, he cast a javelin as though into enemy soil, as Justin records in Book 2. See also Gellius, Book 16, chapter 4, and Alexander ab Alexandro, Book of Genial Days, chapter 3. # GEM *Tropologically,* Angelomus writes: *When the Lord, by his own examples, directs and strengthens the actions of Teachers, he commands the eastern window to be opened and the arrow to be shot, because he urges his own first to be illumined by the light of knowledge and of true doctrine, and to send forth words like darts.* And a little later: *The arrow of the Lord's deliverance, and the arrow of deliverance against Syria — and thou shalt smite Syria in Aphek until thou hast consumed her. The arrow of the Lord's deliverance, therefore, is holy preaching: when it is worthily delivered it is the surest slaying of spiritual enemies, if it is pursued with perseverance. Nor is it lawful that at any time the dispensation...*

Such was the will and decree of God, who had so freely disposed and decreed that Joash should smite Syria as many times as he struck the ground; which decree of God Elisha knew, but by God's command was unwilling to reveal to King Joash, so that it might be left to the king's own free choice to strike the ground as many times as he wished. For if Joash had known the decree of God, he would certainly have struck the ground more often with the arrow. In a similar manner, God bound the victory of Joshua against the Amalekites to the extension of the hands and the prayer of Moses (Exodus 17:11), and likewise bound Joshua's victory against the Canaanites to the lifting up of Joshua's shield (Joshua 8:18). Now as to why Joash struck the ground only three times, and therefore smote the Syrians only three times, the Abulensis attributes this to his sins and to his worship of the golden calves. For it was a remarkable and unworthy thing that a king, so often admonished by Elisha, beholding his miracles, and indeed obtaining victories against the Syrians through the working of God, had not abandoned the worship of the calves and transferred himself entirely to the veneration of the true God, who had shown himself so beneficent toward him. It is probable that he worshipped the true God, whom he had come to know through Elisha, and at the same time the ancestral gods, namely the golden calves of Jeroboam. For his father Jehoahaz worshipped both, as is clear from verses 2 and 6. Hence God, thundering against him and kings like him in Israel through Hosea (ch. 10:2), says: *Their heart is divided; now shall they perish.*

That King Joash was present at Elisha's death, and that the sons of the prophets, his disciples, now spread throughout all Israel, were likewise present and received from him his final counsels of holiness and religious life, is not to be doubted. That the prophet Amos also was present — the brother of King Amaziah and father of Isaiah — is asserted by the Hebrews in the Seder Olam. Elisha prophesied for approximately sixty-six years under six kings of Israel, namely under Ahab, Ahaziah, Joram, Jehu, Jehoahaz, and Joash. He lived more than one hundred years, and eighty of those in religious discipline; and he died, says Salianus, in the year of the world 3204, in the eighth year of Amaziah king of Judah, the tenth year of Joash king of Israel, 839 years before Christ. He is inscribed in the catalogue of Saints in the Roman Martyrology on the 14th of June, where we read: *At Samaria in Palestine, the feast of Saint Elisha the Prophet, whose tomb demons tremble before, as Saint Jerome writes.* Hear Saint Jerome writing at the beginning of the prophet Obadiah: *The sepulchre of Obadiah, along with the mausoleum of Elisha and of John the Baptist, is held in veneration at Sebaste, which was formerly called Samaria.* The same Jerome adds in the epitaph of Paula: that there demons howl under various torments, and before the sepulchres of the saints men shriek after the manner of wolves, roar like lions, hiss like serpents, and bellow with the lowing of bulls. Cedrenus reports, under the Emperor Leo the Great, that the relics of Elisha were translated to Alexandria. Hence Julian the Apostate, mixing the bones of Elisha and of the Baptist with the bones of beasts, wished to burn them, as is recorded in the Life of Saint Artemius by Liromanus. For the praises of Elisha, see Ecclesiasticus 48:13: *In Elisha,* it says, *was the spirit of Elijah fulfilled; in his days he was not afraid of any prince, and no one overpowered him.* See what is said there. Finally, Elisha in the prophetic office was immediately succeeded by Hosea, who prophesied under Uzziah the son of Amaziah king of Judah, along with Isaiah, Amos, Jonah, and the other major and minor prophets. # GEM

Wondrous was the power and holiness of Elisha, in that being dead he raised the dead back to life. He was himself in this matter a type of Christ, through contact with whose merits and grace, as He died upon the cross, all shall rise again. So say Angelomus, Eucherius, and others. Hence Ecclesiasticus bestows upon Elisha this praise (ch. 48:14): *And in death his body prophesied.* He prophesied, that is, he performed a miracle by raising the dead. For miracles were the works of the Prophets — that is, of the holy men and teachers of Israel.

Here Abulensis assembles many reasons why in the Book of Kings the names of mothers are always added for the kings of Judah, but never for the kings of Israel. The most important reason is that the kingdom of Judah was more excellent and more distinguished by the Temple and the true worship of God, and by the Messiah who was to be born from it. Add to this that the kingdom of Israel was schismatic and idolatrous, while that of Judah was legitimate and divinely ordained.

That is to say, because Amaziah at the beginning of his reign worshipped the one true God. *Yet not as David his father,* who not only did not worship idols himself, but did not permit his subjects to worship them either — which Amaziah nonetheless did, allowing the people to offer sacrifice on the high places. This is expressed in 2 Chronicles 25:2: *And he did what was good in the sight of the Lord, yet not with a perfect heart.* *According to all that Joash his father had done* — because just as Joash, while the High Priest Jehoiada lived, worshipped God alone, but after his death turned aside to idols, so also did Amaziah his son, who toward the end of his life declined toward the gods of the Edomites, as is plain from 2 Chronicles 25:14. *Except only that he did not remove the high places* — these words are not to be referred to the immediately preceding clause, *according to all that Joash his father had done*, because Joash likewise did not remove the high places; but rather to the words above, *And he did right before the Lord* — the meaning being: Amaziah rightly worshipped God, but did not rightly permit the people to sacrifice on the high places. Therefore the words *according to all that Joash his father had done* form a parenthesis, as Sanchez observes, and being enclosed as a parenthesis, they are to be separated from what follows.

For the Edomites had rebelled against the Jews sixty years before, namely from the sixth year of Joram king of Judah until the twelfth year of Amaziah (so say the Hebrews in the Seder Olam), who subdued them again and compelled them to serve Judah. See 2 Chronicles 21:8 and 25:11. Now after this victory, Amaziah fell into idolatry and worshipped the gods of the Edomites, and for this reason he was struck down and captured by Joash king of Israel, as is related in 2 Chronicles 25:20. *And he took the Rock in battle* — that is, he captured the lofty and fortified stronghold of the enemy, from which he hurled down another ten thousand Edomites, as is said in 2 Chronicles 25:12. So Cajetan. In the same way the slain at Gibeah fled to the Rock of Rimmon, Judges 20:47. Others understand by "the Rock" Petra, a city of Arabia, which was later restored by Ptolemy Philadelphus and called Philadelphia. *And he called its name Joktheel* — that is, obedience or expectation of God, as our translator renders it at Genesis 49:10, so that all might know that Amaziah had conquered and punished the rebellious Edomites by the command and aid of God. So Cajetan. Vatablus gives a different account: he called it Joktheel, he says, because that was the name of the one who stormed it. Pagninus translates Joktheel as: congregation of God. *Then* — when Amaziah, exulting and elated, indeed puffed up with pride over his victory against the Edomites, supposed that he would in like fashion subdue the Israelites — *Amaziah sent messengers to Joash* (king of Israel) *saying: Come and let us look one another in the face* — in battle, that is, let us fight and contend so as to see which is the stronger. So the Chaldean: Come, let us receive one another's faces in war, meaning: come and let us meet in battle on an appointed day. Vatablus: Come, let us face one another openly. The Hebrews, according to Vatablus, believe the cause of the war was that, as narrated in 2 Chronicles 25, Amaziah had dismissed the mercenary soldiers he had hired from Israel for the campaign against Edom at a prophet's command; and these, dismissed in anger, plundered the kingdom of Judah — and so Amaziah wished to recover these spoils from Joash and the Israelites by war. But Scripture indicates no such thing; rather Joash charges Amaziah that, elated by his victory over the Edomites, he challenges him to battle out of sheer arrogance. Hear Josephus, Book 9, Chapter 10: *Amaziah could not contain his good fortune, whose author he did not acknowledge to be God; but swollen with pride, he wrote to Joash king of the Israelites, demanding that he and his people submit to his commands, as that same people had formerly obeyed his ancestors David and Solomon; and if he refused to render this service of his own accord, the rights of empire must be decided by war.* So also Abulensis, the Historia Scholastica, Salianus, and others. Amaziah hoped to be victorious because the prophet had told him in 2 Chronicles 25 that God was not with Israel — but he ought to have reflected on his own condition, seeing that he was now an apostate worshipping the gods of the Edomites; and particularly because God had willed that the kingdom of Israel should remain divided from the kingdom of Judah, as we heard in Book 3, chapters 12 and following. And so Amaziah, by wishing to reunite and subject that kingdom to himself, was acting against the will of God, and for this reason was overcome in battle.

*The thistle of Lebanon sent to the cedar that is in Lebanon, saying: Give your daughter to my son for a wife. And the wild beasts that are in Lebanon passed by and trod down the thistle.* With this fable Joash repels the arrogance of Amaziah who challenges him to war. For contemptuously he calls Amaziah a thistle — lowly, worthless, and thorny — of Lebanon, however, because he was of royal lineage from Judah; while he calls himself a cedar — lofty, noble, and precious — which the humble thistle, that is, Amaziah, can scarcely lift its eyes to behold, much less compare itself to.

Behold, here the fable of Joash is fulfilled: for thus do they deserve to be punished who provoke others to war. So we see not infrequently that those who challenge others to a duel succumb and are slain in it, God chastising their audacity and pride.
Correct this, with the Roman, Chaldean, and Septuagint readings, to 15 years, as is found in 2 Chronicles 25:25.

This was a just judgment of God, that just as Joash the father, having turned aside to idols, was slain by his own people, so also Amaziah his son, declining toward the same, should be killed by his own conspirators, as is clear from 2 Chronicles 25:27. Yet Salianus and others hold it as probable that he repented before his death, and died piously in the worship of the one true God. **25. HE RESTORED THE BORDERS OF ISRAEL FROM THE ENTRANCE OF HAMATH TO THE SEA OF THE WILDERNESS, ACCORDING TO THE WORD OF THE LORD GOD OF ISRAEL, WHICH HE SPOKE BY HIS SERVANT JONAH THE SON OF AMITTAI.** From this it is plain that Jonah had predicted to King Jeroboam that he himself would restore to Israel those cities which the Syrians had taken from them, from Hamath as far as the Dead Sea — even though this oracle does not survive in the extant prophecy of Jonah. Now Hamath is in the tribe of Naphtali, bordering upon Syria, not far from Damascus, which was the capital of Syria. Hamath was the entrance and, as it were, the gateway into the Holy Land. The same city was afterward called Epiphania by Antiochus Epiphanes when he reigned there; see what was said on Amos 6:2. Hear now Josephus, Book 9, Chapter 11: *The prophet Jonah foretold to Jeroboam that, having conquered the Syrians, he would extend his dominion, pushing his borders to the north as far as the city of Amatha, and to the south as far as the Asphaltite Lake; for by these limits the land of Canaan had formerly been bounded, according to the definition of Joshua the commander. Roused by this oracle, Jeroboam recovered all those territories from the Syrians and added them to his kingdom, the prophecy of Jonah proving in nothing false.* From this it is clear that Jonah the prophet prophesied under Jeroboam king of Israel, and Azariah, or Uzziah, king of Judah, and was therefore a contemporary of Isaiah (whom the Hebrews record as being the brother, or the brother's son, of King Amaziah, on the testimony of St. Jerome), as well as of Hosea, Joel, Amos, and Nahum. Wherefore the Abbot of Ávila is not correct in his judgment that this Jonah is a different person from the Jonah who is the fourth among the Minor Prophets.

How were these cities of Judah within Israel? For the kingdom of Judah was one thing, the kingdom of Israel another. Setting aside the opinions of the Abbot of Ávila and others, I say briefly that David had taken these cities and assigned them to his own tribe of Judah, because he himself was descended from it, and therefore these cities, although situated outside the allotted portion of Judah, were nevertheless called cities of Judah. But when the kingdom of Israel was divided from the kingdom of Judah, these cities, being adjacent, fell to Israel. The meaning therefore is, as if to say: Jeroboam caused Damascus and Hamath, which had been subjugated by David and attributed to the tribe of Judah, but afterward occupied by the Syrians, to be restored to Israel, of which he was king, and to be reckoned again among the other cities of Judah that were under the dominion of Israel, just as they had been reckoned by David — that is to say, Jeroboam caused Damascus and Hamath, once subdued and made tributary to David, to be tributary now to himself and to Israel. So Salianus and Sanchez. **29. AND JEROBOAM SLEPT** (that is, he died a natural death) **WITH HIS FATHERS.** One might object: in Amos 7:11 it is written, *Jeroboam shall die by the sword.* The answer is that these words are not those of Amos, but of Amaziah the false prophet, who, in order to discredit the prophet Amos before Jeroboam, lies and feigns that Amos had said this. For Amos had not said, *Jeroboam shall die by the sword*, but rather, *I will rise up against the house of Jeroboam with the sword* — meaning not the slaying of Jeroboam himself, but of his son Zechariah. --- *CHAPTER FIFTEEN*

Azariah is here called by another name, Uzziah. For "Azariah" in Hebrew means the same as "help of God," while "Uzziah" means the same as "strength of God"; and where there is the help of God, there is strength. For God is most strong and most powerful. Hence in 2 Chronicles 26:7, it is said of him: "And God helped him against the Philistines, and against the Arabs, and against the Ammonites," etc. From this passage, Francis Ribera in his commentary on Amos, chapter 1, verse 1, considers that there was an interregnum of thirteen years between Amaziah and Azariah, on the grounds that Azariah was but three years old when his father Amaziah died. He supports this by noting that in the fifteenth year of Amaziah, as was stated in the preceding chapter at verse 23, Jeroboam began to reign, and that in Jeroboam's twenty-seventh year, as is stated here, Azariah began to reign. Now Amaziah reigned for twenty-nine years. Therefore his twenty-ninth year was Jeroboam's fourteenth year. Therefore the kingdom of Judah was vacant from Jeroboam's fourteenth year until his twenty-seventh, that is, for a period of thirteen years. The true answer, however, is that this twenty-seventh year of Jeroboam is the twenty-seventh year reckoned from the time when Jeroboam began to reign jointly with his father Joash; for in terms of the reign which he held alone after his father, this was only the fourteenth year, in which Amaziah, dying in the twenty-ninth year of his own reign, left as his successor his son Azariah. That this is so is clear from 2 Chronicles 25:25, where Amaziah is said to have lived fifteen years after the death of Joash. He died therefore at the end of the fourteenth year and the beginning of the fifteenth of Jeroboam, who succeeded his father Joash immediately. So hold Salianus, Cajetan, and others. For interregna must be avoided here, lest the continuous series of chronology be disrupted and rendered uncertain and doubtful. Hence Eusebius, Josephus, and others generally place the reign and years of Azariah immediately after and in connection with the reign and years of Amaziah.

In the time of Azariah there lived Sardanapalus, whom pagan historians place as the last king of the Assyrians, saying that, being effeminate, he was besieged in Nineveh by Arbaces, the governor of Media, and there burned himself together with the royal treasury. For this reason Arbaces made himself king of Media around the tenth year of Azariah, when Proca Silvius was reigning among the Latins, Aegemon among the Corinthians, and the lawgiver Lycurgus among the Spartans. So say Eusebius, Diodorus, Justin, Ptolemy, Strabo, and following them Torniellus, Salianus, Gordanus, Samerius, and others. But not long afterward Nineveh and its kingdom were restored; for, as we shall presently hear, not long after, under this same Azariah, Pul king of the Assyrians reigned, to whom succeeded Tiglath-pileser, to him Shalmaneser, to him Sennacherib, to him Esarhaddon, under whom — on account of the Assyrians who had shortly before been slain by an angel while besieging Hezekiah and Jerusalem — Merodach-Baladan the Babylonian raised his head and made himself king of Babylon; he was the grandfather of Nebuchadnezzar, the first monarch of the Babylonians. For all these kings already named are called kings of the Assyrians, not of the Babylonians, and before Baladan no one in Scripture is called king of Babylon. It seems therefore that Pul the Assyrian divided with Arbaces the prefect the empire of Sardanapalus, so that Arbaces should rule over Media and Persia, while Pul should rule over Assyria and Babylonia; so from Metasthenes of Annius hold Genebrardus, Torniellus, Samerius, Gordanus, Salianus, and others. With this chronology agrees the time of the prophecy of Jonah, who, as we heard in the preceding chapter at verse 25, prophesied under Jeroboam, who was contemporary with Azariah. Jonah therefore, sent by God, rebuked the license and luxury of Sardanapalus and the Ninevites. Sardanapalus repented together with the Ninevites, but not long afterward, having relapsed into their accustomed vices, they were besieged and devastated by Arbaces the Mede, as I said in my commentary on Jonah.

Because, namely, he was usurping the office of the High Priest by burning incense upon the altar of incense, which was in the Holy Place, as is stated in 2 Chronicles 26:16, concerning which more is said there.

This man was the great-grandson of Jehu; and so the royal line of Jehu came to an end in Zechariah, as it were in the fourth generation, as God had promised him in chapter 10, verse 30. In the thirty-eighth year of Azariah — that is, reckoned from the time when he himself began to reign together with his father Amaziah; for from the time when Azariah reigned alone after his father, this was the twenty-sixth or twenty-seventh year. If therefore one counts the thirty-eight years of Azariah from the eighteenth year of his father Amaziah, in which he took him as co-regent, this thirty-eighth year of Azariah will be the forty-first of Jeroboam of Israel, inasmuch as Jeroboam began to reign alone in the fifteenth year of Amaziah, Azariah's father. For this forty-first year was the last of Jeroboam; hence, upon his death, his son Zechariah succeeded him for six months. But because Azariah reigned alone after his father Amaziah for fifty-two years, which are here individually recorded — for in verse 37 the fifty-ninth year of Azariah is numbered, and in verse 23 the fiftieth year, and in verse 27 the fifty-second and last — so that a consistent and certain chronology of the times may be woven together through the years of the kings of Judah: hence it is better to say, with Richard of St. Victor, Lyran, Abulensis, and others, that Jeroboam king of Israel died in the twenty-seventh year of Azariah king of Judah, and that from that point for eleven years, namely until the thirty-eighth year of Azariah, there was an interregnum and the kingdom of Israel lacked a king, because Zechariah son of Jeroboam, either on account of his more tender age, or on account of the rebellion of his subjects, or for some other cause, was unable to prevail and secure his father's kingdom. The reason is that the years of Jeroboam are to be counted from the fifteenth year of Amaziah, so that Jeroboam reigned fourteen years concurrently with Amaziah who reigned twenty-nine years; and therefore he must have reigned the remaining twenty-seven years — for Jeroboam reigned forty-one years in total — together with Azariah son of Amaziah, so that he died in the twenty-seventh year of Azariah, and his son Zechariah then succeeded him. Therefore, when it is said of any of the kings of Judah that he reigned so many years, understand this of his reign alone after his father; because the chronology here is woven through the kings of Judah, which would otherwise be uncertain and ambiguous. But in another sense, when it is said "such a king reigned in such a year," especially in Israel, this can be taken not of a sole reign but of a co-reign, namely when he reigned not alone but together with his father. Note: Under Azariah the reckoning of the Olympiads began, though the chronologers differ as to which year of his reign this occurred. Eusebius, in book 3 of the Praeparatio Evangelica and in his Chronicle, places the beginning of the Olympiads in the fiftieth year of Azariah, and consequently places the founding of Rome — when Romulus built it — in the fourth or fifth year of Ahaz; for the beginning of the Olympiads preceded the founding of Rome by twenty-three years, as all historians and chronologers agree. Now add the two remaining years of Azariah (for he reigned fifty-two years), and sixteen years of his son Jotham, and five of Ahaz, and you will have the twenty-three years we are seeking. Others hold that the Olympiads began in the fourth or fifth year of Azariah. Serarius places it in the same year. Pererius, in book 11 of his commentary on Daniel, places it approximately in the eighth year of Ahaz. Torniellus, Salianus, and others place it in the fifty-second year of Azariah, or rather in the first year of Jotham, since Jotham succeeded his father Azariah. For this difference of a few years is slight given the great distance of those ancient times. According to the chronological table which I prefixed to the Pentateuch, the Olympiads began in the fortieth year of Azariah; and Rome was founded in the tenth or eleventh year of Jotham, concerning which see verse 33. Moreover, before the Olympiads, nothing among the Greek and Latin secular historians is certain, but all is uncertain and confused; hence they themselves call that age mythikon, that is, fabulous; but after the Olympiads, just as after the founding of Rome, all things are established by fixed years thenceforward.

This is Phul, who is called by another name Tiglath-Pileser, who together with Arbaces the Mede besieged Sardanapalus in Nineveh for two years, and compelled him to construct a very lofty funeral pyre in the royal palace, into which he cast himself along with his wives, children, and ten thousand myriads of gold and a hundred thousand myriads of silver, and was consumed by the flames. The fire burned for fifteen days. With this event the monarchy of the Assyrians came to an end, which had stood from Belus down to Sardanapalus for thirteen hundred years. Furthermore, Phul divided the monarchy with Arbaces: he reserved for himself Assyria and Babylonia, and ceded to Arbaces Media and Persia. Moreover, Phul in Hebrew means the same as "bean," as Pagninus notes — just as at Rome certain families were surnamed from the bean, among whom Fabius Maximus stood out as the deliverer of Rome and the tamer of Hannibal. Scaliger observes that the names of the Assyrian and Babylonian kings are partly simple, such as Phul, Asar, Tiglath; and partly composite, as Tiglath-Pileser is compounded from three elements, namely from Tiglath, Phul, and Asar. Shalmaneser is compounded from Salman and Asar, and Esarhaddon is formed from Asar and Haddon. Nebuchadnezzar from Nebo, who was the god of the Babylonians, Chad, and Netser. See what was said there on Isaiah 66, verse 1. And Menahem gave Phul a thousand talents of silver, that he might be to him a help against the rebels of Tirzah, Thapsah, etc., as was said at verse 16. This was the occasion why Tiglath-Pileser, the successor of Phul, invaded Israel and carried it away into Assyria, as will be told at verse 29. Phul, therefore, was called upon for aid by Menahem, not by the men of Tirzah, as Josephus writes.

He was the son of Phul, of whom verse 19 speaks, and therefore he is called Phul-Asar, or Tiglath-Phul-Asar. Behold, he laid waste Israel, and from it carried away five tribes into Assyria, namely the inhabitants of Gilead — that is, the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half tribe of Manasseh — and of Galilee, namely the Zebulunites and the Naphtalites, concerning whom Isaiah speaks in chapter 9, verse 1. For when he had shortly before been called to the aid of Ahaz king of Judah against Rezin king of Syria and Pekah king of Israel, he had come to Damascus; and after slaying those kings, as is told in the following chapter, verses 6 and 7, he also oppressed Ahaz himself, by whom he had been summoned, and plundered his dominion, as recorded in 2 Chronicles 28, verse 8. This very thing Isaiah had foretold to Ahaz himself a little earlier, in chapter 7, verse 17. This, therefore, was the first captivity of Israel, that is, of the ten tribes. Hence aptly does the name Tiglath-Pileser in Hebrew (to which the Assyrian and Chaldean languages are closely related) signify "a wondrous captivity of binding," as Pagninus notes in his Hebrew Nomenclature. And Hoshea reigned in his place (that is, Hoshea in place of Pekah) in the twentieth year of Jotham the son of Uzziah. You may object: Jotham reigned only sixteen years, as is said at verse 33; what then is this twentieth year of his? The answer is: it was the twentieth year from the time when Jotham began to reign together with his father Azariah; for this man, having been struck with leprosy by God and therefore separated from the company of men, entrusted the governance of the kingdom to his son Jotham, as was said at verse 5. So say Cajetan and others. You may object again: Hoshea could not have reigned simultaneously with Jotham, for Pekah, whom Hoshea succeeded, outlived Jotham by three years, as is plain from verse 27 here compared with verse 1 of the following chapter. The answer is that Hoshea began to reign and to encroach upon the kingdom of Pekah while Jotham was still living, but only after his death, some years later, fully overcame and slew him in the fourth year of Ahaz king of Judah, and from that time reigned alone in full right. So Cajetan. Secondly, and more satisfactorily, the Hebrews in the Seder Olam, together with Abulensis, Genebrard, Torniellus, and Salianus, explain it thus: the twentieth year of Jotham means the year which would have been the twentieth of Jotham reigning alone after his father, had he still been alive — he having in fact died four years before. It was therefore the twentieth year from the time Jotham began to reign; but since he reigned only sixteen years, this was in reality the fourth year of Ahaz, who succeeded Jotham. However, Scripture preferred to name Jotham rather than Ahaz, because it had not yet made mention of Ahaz. Furthermore, Scripture reckons the years of Hoshea in two ways: first, from this fourth year of Ahaz, as is done here; second, from the twelfth year of Ahaz, as is done in 2 Kings 17, verse 1. The reason is that during the first eight years Hoshea was not a peaceful king, but was still struggling with the house of Pekah, or was rather a servant of the Assyrians than a king, or was impeded from the full exercise of royal power by other causes. Thus from the fourth year of Ahaz he reigned more by precarious tenure than by full right. So say Abulensis, Serarius, Salianus, and the others already cited. Finally, this twentieth year was equally the final year of the reign of Pekah (for he reigned twenty years) and the beginning of the reign of Jotham. For Jotham, had he lived to that year, would have entered upon his twentieth year of reign, since he himself began to reign in the second year of Pekah, as follows below.

For Pekah began to reign in the fifty-second and last year of Uzziah; wherefore this was the first year of the reign of Pekah; the second was the year in which, upon the death of Uzziah, Jotham succeeded him. And therefore in the seventeenth year of Pekah, upon the death of Jotham, his son Ahaz succeeded him, as is said in the following chapter, verse 1; for the last year of Jotham was not a full year but a divided one, so that the seventeenth year of Pekah was at the same time the last year of Jotham as he was dying and the first of Ahaz who succeeded him. Wherefore one year added or subtracted in chronology makes no difference, since half-years are sometimes counted and sometimes not. Moreover, concerning Jotham, the Author of the Opus Imperfectum, homily 1 on Matthew, says that his name means "perfect" or "immaculate lord." And truly perfect was this Jotham, and as regards the kings who preceded him, an immaculate lord; for he did what was pleasing to God, and no reproach of him is found in the Scripture of the Kings. # GEM

He governed well and piously; wherefore he is praised above others by Scripture and by Josephus. In the time of Jotham, Isaiah and Micah prophesied, as is clear from the titles of their books. In the eleventh year of Jotham, Romulus, being eighteen years of age, laid the foundations of the city of Rome on the twenty-first day of April — from which day this date was thereafter celebrated annually as a kind of birthday of Rome — on the very feast of the Palilia, that is, the festival of the goddess Pales, patroness of shepherds, who was worshipped by them on that day for the propitious birth of their flocks. From this the feast was called the Palilia or Parilia. This occurred in the third year of the sixth Olympiad, according to Varro; or in the fourth, according to Cato, followed by Cicero, Pliny, Tacitus, Gellius, Censorinus, Eusebius, Saint Augustine, Orosius, and Cardinal Baronius. Wherefore the Olympiads precede Rome by twenty-three years, as I have said: for so many years are reckoned from the six Olympiads already mentioned, each one containing four years. By this reckoning, the beginning of the Olympiads must be assigned to the fortieth year of Azariah, as I have said, and according to the same reckoning Rome was founded in the year 751 before the birth of Christ. Others, because they assign the beginning of the Olympiads somewhat earlier or later, consequently assign the building of Rome somewhat earlier or later as well. Hence Torniellus and Salianus, because they align the beginning of the Olympiads with the first year of Jotham, accordingly align the founding of Rome with the eighth or ninth year of Ahaz. But in so ancient a matter, after so many centuries, this difference of time is slight. Here is that verse of Propertius: "It was a feast day for the City; the fathers called it the Palilia. From this day the first began to be a day within the walls." Hence also Saint Jerome, in his commentary on Isaiah chapter 1, verse 1, says that Hezekiah began to reign in the twelfth year of Romulus; the first year of Romulus therefore falls in the fourth year of Ahaz, given that Romulus himself, having laid the foundations of Rome in the eleventh year of Jotham, completed the construction over the following seven or eight years and erected the royal throne therein in the fourth year of Ahaz. So also Saint Augustine, in book 18 of the City of God, chapter 22, says: "Hezekiah, who is well known to have been an excellent and most devout king, reigned in the times of Romulus."

...that he might help him against Rasin king of Syria, and Pekah king of Israel; and therefore he sent all the gold and silver both of the temple and of his own palace. Ahaz sinned in this matter, because God had promised him aid against Rasin and Pekah through Isaiah, chapter 7, verse 4; but he, distrusting God, put his trust in Tiglath-pileser, and therefore was himself despoiled and oppressed by that same king. Hear 2 Chronicles 28, verses 19 and 22: *For the Lord had humbled Judah on account of Ahaz king of Judah, because he had stripped it of its defense and had treated the Lord with contempt. And he brought against him Tiglath-pileser king of the Assyrians, who afflicted him and laid him waste with none to resist. Therefore Ahaz, having plundered the house of the Lord and the houses of kings and princes, gave gifts to the king of the Assyrians, and yet it profited him nothing. Moreover, in the time of his distress he increased his contempt toward the Lord — Ahaz himself, the king, personally offered sacrifice to the gods of Damascus who had smitten him, and said: The gods of the kings of Syria help them; therefore will I sacrifice to them, that they may help me — when in fact those same gods were his ruin and the ruin of all Israel.*
...not that Cyrene which is in Ethiopia, nor that which is in Africa, but the one which is in the middle region. Hence Josephus says that the Damascenes were transported to Media.

...wrought with outstanding form, material, and workmanship, it pleased him; wherefore he commanded Uriah the High Priest to have a similar one made at Jerusalem in the temple according to its design, in the place of the altar fabricated by Solomon. Whether Ahaz erected this altar to idols — as the Damascenes did — and sacrificed upon it to them, or whether to the true God, Scripture does not explicitly state; yet it is probable that he erected it to idols, because he worshipped those same idols just as the Damascenes did, and everywhere commanded that altars be raised to them, seeking thereby to win the favor of Tiglath-pileser and bind him to himself by venerating his gods and idols — but to no avail: for this is precisely why he was plundered and subjugated by him, as I have said. So hold Abulensis and others. # GEM

Uriah the High Priest sinned in erecting this impious altar, preferring to please King Ahaz rather than God. Far otherwise did Saint Ambrose act; for as he himself relates in Book 5, Epistle 32: *When it was imposed upon me that I should deliver the vessels of the Church to the Emperor, I returned this answer: that if anything of my own were demanded — land, or house, or gold, or silver — whatever was within my own right, I would freely offer; but that I could take nothing from the temple of God, nor deliver that which I had received to guard, not to surrender. Furthermore, I must also consider the Emperor's own salvation, because it is no more expedient for me to deliver than for him to receive. Let him therefore receive the voice of a free priest, if he wishes to be well counseled; let him withdraw from injuring Christ; and far be it from me to deliver the inheritance of Christ, and the inheritance of Dionysius who died in exile for the faith, the inheritance of Eustorgius the confessor, the inheritance of Mirocles, and of all the faithful bishops of former times.* Furthermore, the bronze altar — fabricated by Solomon — *which was before the Lord* (that is, before the Holy Place and the Holy of Holies, in which God dwelt giving oracles) *he removed* with contempt, as is stated in 2 Chronicles 28, verses 19 and 22; for Ahaz was utterly impious, and therefore a despiser of God, of His altar, and of His temple. For this reason he moved the altar of God from its place and substituted his own Damascene altar in its stead, setting the altar of God beside his own altar, as if in a secondary and subordinate position, relegated to a corner.

Ahaz did all these things, as I have said, out of contempt for the altar (for the lavers and the bronze sea had served it), and so that it might be rendered useless for its purposes, namely for sacrifices; and thus the Jews would be compelled to sacrifice not upon it to the true God, but to his idols.

Supply from the preceding verse: he took away and removed, that is, he lifted off and took down. What was the Musach? Various opinions exist, but two are more probable. The first: Musach in Hebrew means the same as a covering or roof; it therefore signifies either a covered throne in which kings sat on the Sabbath and other feast days to be present at the sacrifices and hear the Law of God—whence in chapter 23, verse 3, it is called a step, as Rabbi Solomon holds. Hence the Septuagint renders it: the base of the sabbath throne, by which they appear to understand the pedestal upon which the king's throne was placed when he came to the temple on the Sabbath to pray and hear the Law, as in Ezekiel 46:1. Musach sabbati in Hebrew therefore signifies a sabbath canopy covered with a splendid canopy-cloth, which stood next to the court of the priests but outside it, in which the king sat on the Sabbath and feast days watching the sacrifices. This is the tribunal upon which Joash sat when he was made king by Jehoiada the High Priest (2 Kings 11:14); or alternatively, Musach signifies a pulpit, as Vatablus renders it, or a covered platform and dais on which the Levites and priests sat while they expounded the Law and the Prophets to the people, as Villalpando holds, as Ezra did in Nehemiah 8:4. The latter opinion is that the Musach was a covering—namely a veil or a boarded structure—prepared for the Levites and other guardians of the temple who on each Sabbath fulfilled the sacred duties in the temple in rotation, as Vatablus says. Or the Musach was an enclosed place in the court of the people, so that beneath it those who flocked to the temple to pray or hear the Law might dwell sheltered from heat, rain, and wind. Hence Marinus in his Lexicon renders Musach as a portico. All these interpretations are plausible; for the impious Ahaz appears to have removed all these things, as indeed in 2 Chronicles 29:24 he is said to have closed the temple of the Lord. However, since the Musach of the Sabbath appears to pertain to the king's going out, which is contained in this very verse, another interpretation, which I shall presently set forth, seems more probable. AND THE OUTER ENTRANCE OF THE KING HE TURNED TOWARD THE TEMPLE OF THE LORD ON ACCOUNT OF THE KING OF THE ASSYRIANS. First, some explain it thus: that Ahaz profaned the temple by making it serve as a vestibule, so to speak, to his own royal palace, so that access to the palace could not be had except through the temple—namely that he might be safer from the king of the Assyrians, who would have had to take the temple first before proceeding to the assault of the palace. Yet on another side, which did not adjoin the temple, the palace could be attacked and more easily taken than the temple. Second, Theodoret and Lyra hold that Ahaz blocked up the passage by which one went from the palace to the temple, with the purpose that the king of the Assyrians might understand that he had entirely forsaken the true God and the worship of God, in order to follow and worship his gods. The Chaldean paraphrase favors this, rendering convertit as averted (for to convert means to turn elsewhere or in the contrary direction). The Hebrew is SCH heseb, that is, he caused to go around, so that if anyone wished to go from the palace to the temple, he would be forced to make a great circuit, descending from Mount Zion and crossing the whole of Ophel, to come to the foot of Mount Moriah, and from there to ascend to the temple by nine hundred steps—so that the burden of so long a circuit and so laborious an ascent might deter those of the king's household from approaching the temple, as Salianus says. Yet the phrase he turned toward the temple does not mean to turn away, but to turn toward the temple. Hence, thirdly, some explain it more plainly thus: that Ahaz annexed to the temple the vestibule of his palace through which one passed into the temple, and which had previously been outside the temple, making what had formerly been exterior to the temple now be within the temple—so that in this way he might imitate the king of Assyria.

This year, the twelfth of Ahaz, was the eighth after the death of Pekah; for Pekah had reigned twenty years and died in the fourth year of Ahaz, as is clear from what was said in chapter 16, verse 1. Therefore there was an interregnum of eight years in Israel, namely from the fourth year of Ahaz, in which Pekah died, until the twelfth year of Ahaz, in which Hoshea succeeded Pekah. It seems therefore that the reign of Hoshea in Israel was interrupted for eight years by the arms either of the Syrians or of the rival family of Pekah, as the Abulensis says in question 17, until in the eighth year Hoshea, having recovered his strength, began to reign in the twelfth year of Ahaz. So Richard of Saint Victor in his Concordance of the Years of the Kings of Israel and Judah, Cajetan, and others. See what was said at chapter 15, verse 30. 2. AND HE DID EVIL (in Israel as was customary, namely by worshipping the golden calves erected there by Jeroboam, as if they were household gods or gods peculiar and ancestral to Israel) IN THE SIGHT OF THE LORD, BUT NOT AS THE KINGS WHO HAD BEEN BEFORE HIM; because, as the Hebrews relate in the Seder Olam, chapter 22, Hoshea, although he himself worshipped the golden calves, nevertheless permitted those of his subjects who wished to go to Jerusalem and there to worship the God of the Jews in the temple — something which the other kings of Israel had not permitted; indeed, they had intercepted those who attempted to go by means of guards posted by themselves. Hence Hoshea permitted the Israelites, invited by Hezekiah, to proceed to Jerusalem to celebrate the Passover, as is clear from 2 Chronicles 30, verses 11 and 18. 3. AGAINST HIM CAME UP SHALMANESER, the son of Tiglath-pileser, son of Pul. Genebrard thinks that Shalmaneser is the one called Nabonassar by Ptolemy. But in truth Shalmaneser was an Assyrian, whereas Nabonassar was a Chaldean, as the names themselves indicate. So Serarius. Therefore Nabonassar was Saladan, of whom we read in chapter 20, verse 12. AND HOSHEA BECAME HIS SERVANT AND PAID HIM TRIBUTE for five or six years; when these had elapsed he refused the tribute, relying on the help of the king of Egypt. Wherefore Shalmaneser invaded Israel, bound Hoshea in chains, besieged Samaria for three years, and in the third year captured it; which was the ninth and last year of Hoshea, in which Shalmaneser carried away the remaining five tribes of Israel into Assyria and Media, just as his father Tiglath-pileser had carried off the five nearer tribes to the same place fourteen years before. This was the second and complete captivity of Israel, that is of the ten tribes, from which they never returned, accomplished in the sixth year of Hezekiah king of Judah — and this, says Cajetan, in order to be a testimony that Christ, the awaited Messiah, had long been known to them from the books of the Old Testament; for no hope remains to them of restoring an earthly kingdom, since they are utterly scattered and dispersed, but only the hope of a spiritual kingdom and eternal salvation which Christ has brought to all peoples who are willing to believe in him. Furthermore, at that time Tobias was carried away from Israel into Assyria; for he was of the tribe of Naphtali: wherefore his story must be appended here, for it occurred at this time under Shalmaneser.

Josephus says they were led away into Media and Persia. Scaliger holds they were transported to the Colchians and Iberians. But I myself, says Genebrard, think that they went into ancient Tartary as far as the wilderness of Belgium in the great northern region of the East, from which place they burst forth around the year of Christ 1200, under the leadership of the Khans — being previously unknown both in place and name — and established the empire of the Tartars, which is remembered to this day. For in the Hebrew annals, according to Rabbi Solomon on Isaiah 27, the ten tribes were lost in the East, and there they readily embraced Mohammedanism, since they had already been circumcised and were imbued with a certain corrupted semblance of Mosaic religion. Genebrard confirms this from the fact that certain Tartar hordes are said to this day to have retained the names of Dan, Naphtali, and Zebulun, and that among them are to be found other monuments of Hebrew law and customs; so that it is no wonder that Sarmatia, Lithuania, Livonia, and Muscovy abound with Jews, who even now, as also in Europe, are most tenacious of their ancestral superstition. Genebrard adds that most of them penetrated into America; for subterranean tombs with Hebrew inscriptions have been found there, and that expedition is indicated in 4 Esdras 13, verse 40: These are the ten tribes, it says, which were led captive from their own land in the days of Hoshea the king, whom Shalmaneser the king of the Assyrians led captive, and he transported them beyond the river, and they were carried into another land, etc. Finally, Genebrard judges that this dispersion and, as it were, infiltration of the Israelites throughout the East and North gave occasion to the fable that they are shut up and confined within the Caspian mountains. Here, therefore, the kingdom of Israel, or of the ten tribes, comes to an end; it had endured from their first king Jeroboam, who made the schism from Rehoboam king of Judah, for two hundred and fifty-six years. This is the Assyrian captivity, which preceded the Babylonian captivity — in which the tribe of Judah was carried away and Jerusalem was destroyed under King Zedekiah — by one hundred and thirty-four years. Furthermore, many, foreseeing this captivity, had fled into other provinces; many also who were true worshippers of God migrated into the tribe of Judah and joined themselves to it, as is clear from what follows.

By words, that is, by deeds and actions — namely, idolatries and superstitions. For the word "words" frequently signifies in Scripture the thing signified by the word, by metonymy, as here in verse 11. FROM THE TOWER OF THE WATCHMEN UNTO THE FENCED CITY. He calls "the tower of the watchmen" a rustic tower, which is erected in fields and vineyards so that watchmen may dwell in it and guard the grapes, fruits, and harvests — meaning, as if to say: the Israelites set up idols in places and settlements both small and great, namely as much in hamlets and villages as in fortified towns and cities. So Vatablus.

For then God sent prophets, namely Hosea, Isaiah, Joel, Amos, Obadiah, and Micah, as is clear from their prologues.
