For under Azariah, or Uzziah the king, Hosea and Isaiah began to prophesy, who on God's behalf bore witness to Israel and Judah concerning the injury and impiety committed against God — namely that, having abandoned God, they were worshipping idols — and concerning His chastisement, namely that God would lay them waste through the Assyrians and lead them away captive; for this is what they threatened and prophesied to them.

Behold, this was the occasion and cause of the Israelites' idolatry: namely, that through commerce, intermarriage, and familiarity with neighboring nations, they learned and absorbed their customs and superstitions. So in our own age, Germany, France, Sweden, Denmark, England, Scotland, etc., absorbed the heresy of Luther the Saxon from neighboring Saxony, through mutual commerce, especially that of heretical merchants.

That is, the sun, the moon, Lucifer, and the other stars, which are as it were the army and ordered battle-line of God, ever moving in circuit and fulfilling their appointed stations, serving God as soldiers and, at His command, hurling storms, thunder, and lightning against the faithless and the impious. [On verse 20:] AND THE LORD CAST AWAY ALL THE SEED OF ISRAEL (that is, both the ten Tribes and the Tribe of Judah, of which mention was made above), UNTIL HE HAD CAST THEM OUT FROM HIS FACE — that is, until He had fully and irrevocably rejected them from Himself. Let the Jews take note here: namely, that they have been plainly cast off and rejected by God, so that they ought not to hope for the liberation of their people from the captivity which they have now endured for 1,600 years, nor for the restoration of the Synagogue; but, mingled and mixed among the other nations, they ought together with them to believe in Christ, if they wish to be freed from servitude both temporal and eternal. [On verse 24:] AND THE KING OF THE ASSYRIANS BROUGHT MEN FROM BABYLON. Hence it is clear that Shalmaneser held dominion not only over Assyria but also over Babylon, for he transferred colonists from there into Samaria. AND FROM CUTHAH. Hence these colonists transplanted into Samaria were called Cutheans, just as from Samaria they were called Samaritans. Wherefore they were Babylonians and Assyrians, and not Jews or Israelites.
That is, they are ignorant of the rite and ceremonies by which God wills to be lawfully worshipped in Samaria; for this reason He, being angered, sent lions against them, as was related above.

Each people fashioned its own image and idol. Vatablus notes that these words are Syriac and Chaldaic, and that they signify the following: Succoth-benoth is the same as a hen with her chicks; Nergal is a rooster; Ashima is a wild goat; Nibhaz is a dog, so called because it is accustomed to bark, or because when enraged it bares its teeth; Tartak is a donkey; Adrammelech and Anammelech are a mule and a donkey. Behold and marvel here at the stupidity of the Cutheans or Samaritans, who worshipped as gods the most brute of animals, such as the mule, the horse, and the donkey. God permitted this in order to show into what absurd deities those fall who abandon the true divinity of God.

This was the folly of the Cutheans: that they worshipped God together with their idols, as though God were the Lord of the land of Israel which they inhabited, while their idols were the ancestral gods of their own lands from which they had been brought to Samaria. These nations, therefore, were fearing (that is, adoring, sacrificing to, and worshipping) the Lord, yet nonetheless serving their idols as well. For their sons and grandsons, as their fathers had done, so do they unto this present day. These, then, are the Cutheans, or Samaritans, who worshipped the God of Israel simultaneously with the idols of the nations, and were therefore held in abomination by the Jews — who worshipped God alone — as though they were accursed, as is evident from the Samaritan woman conversing with Christ in John chapter 4. For out of the whole confused mingling and promiscuous mixture of the gods and religions of the nations, they fashioned a certain religion compounded from many sources, as it were a monstrous patchwork. Hence they fell into various errors, which St. Epiphanius, Philastrius, and others enumerate in their works on heresies. From the Jews they borrowed circumcision, the observance of the Sabbath, and the ceremonies prescribed by Moses. For they admitted only the Pentateuch of Moses, but rejected the other prophets and the hagiographa. From other nations they borrowed their idols, gentile rites, superstitions, and the like. Philastrius adds — and from him Gabriel Prateolus in his work on the heresy of the Samaritans — that they: first, do not look forward to a future judgment; second, deny the resurrection of the dead; third, deny the immortality of the soul; fourth, do not believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, promised by the prophets; and fifth, St. Epiphanius, in heresy 9, towards the middle, says: "They have," he writes, "certain other ancient customs of a more degraded kind, in that they wash themselves with urine, and plunge themselves into water together with their garments whenever they have come into contact with someone from another nation" — plainly regarding such contact as a pollution. Concerning other errors of the Samaritans, see Epiphanius, heresies 10, 11, 12, and 13. Furthermore, the Samaritans, when things went prosperously for the Jews, wished to be counted as Jews; but when things went badly for the Jews, they turned away from them — after the manner of Aesop's bat, who wished to be a bird on account of the wings it has, and a mouse on account of its body; for in body it is a mouse, but in wings it is a bird. Hence Josephus, in Book 11 of the Antiquities, chapter 8, and in Book 12, chapter 7, writes that the Samaritans, when they saw Alexander the Great favourably disposed toward the Jews, boasted that they were Jews and professed the same religion as they; but when they saw Antiochus Epiphanes hostile and threatening toward the Jews, they denied being Jews and turned away from them entirely.

You will object: From the preceding chapter it is clear that Ahaz lived only thirty-six years; therefore Ahaz begat Hezekiah in his eleventh year of age: for since he died in his thirty-sixth year of age, and Hezekiah who succeeded him was twenty-five years old, subtract twenty-five from thirty-six and eleven years remain. Cajetan, Sanchez, and St. Jerome in Epistle 132 to Vitalis admit this consequence, where Jerome proves by several examples that this is possible. The Abulensis, however, thinks that Ahaz was fourteen years old when he begat Hezekiah; because, he says, his incomplete years are passed over in silence by Scripture. For Ahaz, says the Abulensis, when he began to reign, was twenty years of age reckoned as complete, plus one incomplete year which Scripture does not count. Again, he reigned sixteen complete years, plus a seventeenth incomplete year: wherefore he died in his thirty-eighth year of age. Hezekiah likewise was only twenty-four complete years old when he began to reign, but had entered upon his twenty-fifth year. AND HE BROKE IN PIECES THE BRONZE SERPENT WHICH MOSES HAD MADE (to heal those who had been bitten by serpents, Numbers 21:8); forasmuch as UNTIL THAT TIME THE CHILDREN OF ISRAEL HAD BURNED INCENSE TO IT as to a beneficent deity, inasmuch as it had healed their fathers from the bite of serpents. Philastrius, Bishop of Brescia, a contemporary of St. Ambrose, adds in his book On Heresies, chapter 21, that Hezekiah ordered certain impressions or inscriptions to be erased and scraped away with iron, and utterly cut out — inscriptions which the people of the Jews had made upon a certain great stone, like an obelisk, which had been erected in the Temple in place of a column: from which stone they would take various characters resembling certain letters, place them on a tablet, and carry them about the neck, using them for certain incantations and sorceries; and they venerated these things as certain amulets, bearing them upon their breasts — things which the Lord had everywhere forbidden by law to be done, this being the impiety of pagan blindness. AND HE CALLED ITS NAME NEHUSHTAN, that is, a little piece of brass, namely something cast from small, cheap, and trifling copper. For Nehushtan is a diminutive name, which Hezekiah uses to express contempt for the idol, as if to say: What do you suppose, O Jews, that a small piece of cheap and worthless brass possesses divinity? You are mistaken; there is nothing divine in it, nothing great; for it is nothing other than a small portion of base and blackened metal. Anastasius of Nicaea adds from Eusebius: The books of Solomon, he says, which had been written concerning Proverbs and odes, in which the nature of plants and every kind of animal was treated, and the curing of all diseases — these Hezekiah removed from circulation; because the people derived from them remedies for diseases, and thus thought nothing of seeking cures from God. Verse 5. HE TRUSTED IN THE LORD GOD OF ISRAEL — and therefore it is said in verse 7: And the Lord was with him, and in all things to which he proceeded he acted wisely. Hence also Hezekiah was aptly called in Hebrew חִזְקִיָּהוּ, Chiskiahu, that is, the Lord is my strength, says Pagninus, as though this were his motto and symbol. THERE WAS NONE LIKE HIM — that is, like Hezekiah, in this respect namely, that he removed the high places and broke the bronze serpent, things which all other kings before him had permitted. For otherwise David seems to have been more devout and holier than Hezekiah: for David was a man after God's own heart, to whom accordingly God made the promise concerning Christ who would be born from him; and thus David was established by God as the exemplar to be imitated by all kings who would be born from him. So the Abulensis. Or certainly it is a hyperbole, meaning: Hezekiah was so pious and zealous in defending the worship of God that he seems scarcely to have had an equal or a like. The same judgment applies.

To whom his father Ahaz had subjected himself and paid tribute — which Hezekiah refused, judging it unworthy that a faithful king of Judah should serve an unfaithful king, and trusting in God that He would liberate him from this servitude, as indeed He did, through a miraculous victory.
In the rural regions, as I said in the preceding chapter.

Instead of "cities," Serarius and others think one should read "river." Hence Vatablus translates: beside the river Gozan, and this is how our text renders it at chapter 17:6. For in Hebrew it is נְהַר, nehar, that is, river, in the singular; or certainly cities situated beside rivers are called by the name of rivers through metonymy; for men dwell in cities, not in rivers — unless among the Chinese, where the multitude of men is so great that the land cannot contain them: wherefore many are compelled to live on the bridges and boats of the rivers, as our Nicolaus Trigault testifies in his History of China.

He had now succeeded Shalmaneser, his father, who had died, in the kingdom of the Assyrians. He ascended against Judah because Hezekiah had rebelled and refused tribute, v. 7. This came to pass by the providence of God, who, notwithstanding the piety of Hezekiah, wished to punish the tribe of Judah on account of the idolatry and crimes committed by them.

Note here the treachery of Sennacherib, who, having received from Hezekiah three hundred talents of silver and thirty of gold, nevertheless sent an army against him — an army which was accordingly struck down by an Angel, and while he invaded the possessions of others, he lost his own at home. *In the way of the fuller's field.* The field was so called because in it the fullers washed their cloths with the water of the pool.

Rabshakeh lies, claiming that he invades Judea by God's command, in order to terrify the people and compel them to surrender. So says the Abulensis. The remainder of this chapter is transcribed from Isaiah chapter 36, where I have explained it: for Isaiah was the intermediary between God and Hezekiah, and foretold and promised him victory against Sennacherib. # GEM

Why did they remain silent? St. Cyril of Alexandria answers first, in the cited chapter of Isaiah: "Because," he says, "against the unskilled speech and barbarian arrogance and the tongue so insolently directed against God, it was fitting to weep rather than to answer back." Procopius answers secondly, at the cited passage of Kings: "The pious king," he says, "permitted nothing to be answered to these blasphemies, lest he provoke them to greater ones." The Abulensis answers thirdly, in the same place, question 44: "Because," he says, "it belonged to the honor of the king that affairs should be conducted prudently, in an orderly manner, and with deliberation; but if any one of the people were able to answer Rabshakeh as he pleased, this would be a great disorder, and Rabshakeh might mock the lack of order which Hezekiah maintained among his subjects and in his affairs." CHAPTER NINETEEN.

Genebrardus in his *Chronology*, Pererius, Arias Montanus, and Serarius in chapter 1 of Judith, verse 1, hold that Asarhaddon is Sardanapalus, for the following reasons: first, just as Sardanapalus is placed by the pagan historians as the last king and monarch of the Assyrians, so too in Scripture Asarhaddon is placed as the last king of the Assyrians — for after him are named Baladan, Merodach, Nebuchadnezzar, Evil-Merodach, and Belshazzar, all of whom were princes and kings of the Babylonians. Second, because the name Sardanapalus appears to be formed from *Asar* and *Don* or *Haddon*, with the name Phul added; therefore he is the same as Asarhaddon. Third, because what Jonas and Nahum foretold concerning the future destruction of Nineveh by the flooding of the river Tigris, Diodorus testifies came to pass under Sardanapalus. However, Eusebius, Torniellius, Salianus, and the other Chronologists place Sardanapalus at an earlier point, namely under Azariah king of Judah — for it was then that Jonas prophesied and foretold the ruin of Nineveh to Sardanapalus, as I have noted at chapter 15, section 2, and at Jonah chapter 3, verse 6 and following. HERE BEGINS CHAPTER TWENTY.

Baladan was the first prince of the Babylonians; for until now we have heard nothing of their name in Scripture, but only of the Assyrians. It therefore appears that Baladan, prefect of Babylonia, or rather his son Merodach Baladan, seeing that Sennacherib (to whom he was subject and tributary) and the forces of the Assyrians had been cut down by an Angel in Judaea and their strength broken, shook off the yoke and made himself absolute prince and king of the Babylonians; indeed, he deprived Esarhaddon of both kingdom and life around the twenty-sixth year of Hezekiah, as Salianus maintains. And at that time the kingdom of the Assyrians collapsed, so that henceforth in Scripture no kings of the Assyrians are named, but all are called kings of the Babylonians, of whom the first was Baladan. Hence Baladan in Hebrew means the same as "without a Lord," as Pagninus states. Furthermore, that Baladan's father was not Shalmaneser, king of the Assyrians, as Genebrardus maintains, but rather Nabonassar, who is placed by Ptolemy in the Almagest as the first prince of the Babylonians, is the view of Scaliger, Serarius, Bellarmine, Torniellus, and Salianus in his Chronology, drawing on Eusebius, Berossus, and Josephus. From Nabonassar was established the Nabonassarean era, from which the Babylonians and others thereafter reckoned their years, just as the Persians reckoned from Cyrus, the Greeks from Alexander, and the Romans from Augustus. Hence in Chaldean, Baladan is derived from Bal and Haddon. For Bal, or Bel and Baal, was Nimrod, that is Jupiter Belus, the first builder of the Tower of Babel after the Flood, as I explained at Genesis 10; who (or whose associate) was called by another name Nebo, that is, prophecy or divination, as I noted at Isaiah chapter 46, verse 1, on those words: "Bel is broken, Nebo is crushed." For as Tertullian says in the Apology, chapter 20: "The truth of divination is a testimony of divinity; for it is proper to God to divine, to foreknow, and to foretell future things," according to that passage of Isaiah 41, verse 23: "Announce the things that are to come... and we shall know that you are gods." Hence from the god Nebo and Assar was formed and named Nabonassar. In turn, the son of Baladan, that is of Nabonassar, was this Merodach, or Berodach, who was the first to call himself king of Babylon and who sent ambassadors to Hezekiah. Merodach was succeeded by his son Ben-Merodach, as Eusebius affirms in book 9 of the Praeparatio, Berossus in Josephus, Metasthenes of Annius, Josephus himself, Scaliger in book 6 of the De Emendatione Temporum, Torniellus, Salianus, and others, who assign to Baladan 27 years, to Merodach 52, and to Ben-Merodach 44, which together make 123 years. Accordingly, chronologers count 123 years from Nabonassar to Nabopolassar, whom Berossus calls Nabolassar, who succeeded Ben-Merodach and was Nebuchadnezzar the Elder, as Eusebius, Berossus, Scaliger, Bellarmine, Torniellus, Salianus, and others affirm. To Nebuchadnezzar the Elder, after 19 years of reign, succeeded Nebuchadnezzar the Younger, surnamed the Great, who captured Jerusalem, led the Jews into Babylon, transferred the monarchy from the Assyrians to the Babylonians, and reigned for 45 years; he was succeeded by his son Evil-Merodach, called by another name Belshazzar, for 34 years, as I explained at Daniel 5, verse 1. This chronology of learned men is probable, though others assign in certain particulars a different reckoning from the one just stated. For nothing certain can be asserted concerning times so ancient and lacking reliable historical record. See what is said at the beginning of Ezekiel 1. Finally, he who in private life was called Baladan, upon assuming imperial power was called Nabonassar Baladan; and his son was called Merodach Baladan. Furthermore, many scholars, following Ptolemy, assign the first year of Nabonassar to the first or second year of the eighth Olympiad, that is, the thirtieth year from the beginning of the Olympiads, which Torniellus maintains to be the thirteenth year of King Ahaz.

That is to say: Manasseh had first constructed a grove, or sacred wood, consecrated to the gods, and had placed an idol therein; this idol he subsequently transferred from the grove into the Temple. But because he could not transfer the grove itself together with the idol, he therefore made, in place of the grove, an image or likeness of the grove, fashioned from gold, silver, or bronze, and placed the idol within it, then brought it along with the grove into the Temple — so that henceforth it was not God who was worshipped in the Temple, but a demon in the grove. Both of these things were afterwards destroyed by Josiah, the grandson of Manasseh, in chapter 23, verses 6 and 7.

That is, through Hosea, Amos, Joel, Nahum, Jonah, Obadiah, and most especially through Isaiah, who was the father-in-law of Manasseh, and who was therefore sawn asunder with a wooden saw by Manasseh's order — being thus a type of Christ crucified upon the wood of the Cross, as I noted in the preface to Isaiah — and through many other prophets as well. So much so that Josephus, in book 10, chapter 3, asserts that from among their number some one prophet was put to death by Manasseh on each successive day. # GEM

The measuring line signifies metaphorically the same measure of punishment: that is to say, With the same measure by which I punished, laid waste, and carried the Israelites away from Samaria into Assyria, with that same measure shall I punish, lay waste, and carry the Jews away from Jerusalem into Babylon. For just as they were equal in guilt and idolatry, so shall they be equal in punishment and captivity. The metaphor is drawn from builders, who make use of an iron instrument or plumb-line, which they extend with a weight of lead, in order that they may, according to that line and by the perpendicular, either construct a building or demolish and destroy one already built. That is to say: I will destroy Jerusalem and level it to the ground as though by a measuring line, just as a builder is accustomed to demolish houses and to level and flatten the earth by means of a measuring cord. And explaining this same thing by another comparison, more lucid still, He declares: I will wipe out Jerusalem as tablets are wiped clean; and wiping it clean I will turn it and draw the stylus more repeatedly over the face of the land. That is to say: I will wipe out Jerusalem as letters are wont to be wiped from a tablet inscribed with wax. For in former times, before the invention of paper, men were accustomed to write on tablets coated with wax, and to form letters thereon with a stylus, as one would with a pen. Wherefore the stylus was sharp at the lower end, but blunt at the upper end, so that with the same instrument they might, when they wished, obliterate letters already written in the wax, and, having smoothed the wax flat, engrave other letters upon it. Thus, if but a single word or letter was to be erased, this was accomplished by one or two strokes of the stylus through the wax; if more, by more strokes; if all, by strokes covering the whole, until the letters were fully erased and the wax was restored clean and smooth. In precisely this manner did God act here in the destruction of Judaea and Jerusalem. For first He drew against it the hostile stylus of the king of Babylon, when He led Manasseh and the chief men of the kingdom away as captives to Babylon, 2 Chronicles 33:11. Second, when Pharaoh Necho slew Josiah, who was the entire hope of the Jews, and carried off his son Jehoahaz into Egypt, chapter 23, verse 33. Third, when Nebuchadnezzar seized and bound Jehoiakim, the brother of Jehoahaz. Fourth, when the same king sent Jehoiachin, the son of Jehoiakim, together with a great number of nobles and people, into Babylon. Fifth and last, when the same king carried away Zedekiah, the final king, along with the remainder, and burned the city and the Temple. The meaning therefore is: I will stretch the measuring line over Jerusalem — that is to say, not only shall I demolish the city, but I will dig up its very foundations and level them to the earth as though measuring by a line, so that no vestige of it shall remain, nor any trace of its former outline; no differently than is done when waxed tablets are erased of their lines and characters. In place of "tablet," the Hebrew has נְשָׁלַּחַת, isallachat, which is rendered variously as dish, frying pan, cauldron, or caldron. Hence Vatablus translates thus: I will scour Jerusalem, as one scours a dish and turns it over. And he explains it thus: that is, I will purge Jerusalem of its wicked inhabitants, and when I have driven them away, I will overturn it as a scoured dish is overturned by countryfolk. But our own Jerome and Prado, in their commentary on Ezekiel chapter 5, verse 11, translate and explain it thus: I will wipe Jerusalem clean, as a caldron is wont to be cleansed by wiping and overturned upon its face — that is, as when a vessel, once it has been thoroughly wiped out, is turned upside down so that nothing of its contents may remain, but every last drop may drip down to the ground: in this manner will I cleanse Jerusalem, wiping from it all its inhabitants and houses, and overturning it from its very foundations.

Most especially those who resisted his impiety and rebuked him for it, such as Isaiah and the other prophets; so that, as Josephus says, he put to death some one of the prophets every day. Hence Manasseh is fittingly interpreted to mean "from insurgence" or "from oblivion," as the Author of the Incomplete Work states in Homily 1 on Matthew. For the Providence of God so dispensed that he should be called by this name; because he was destined to forget all the holy conduct of his father and all the benefits which God had bestowed upon him for his father's sake, and, being stirred up by the insurgent devil — who is wont to rise up against the human race in order to subvert it — he would do all things that might provoke God to wrath. The same Author adds something novel and remarkable concerning Manasseh, saying: Finally, when Hezekiah was ill at a certain time and Isaiah the Prophet came to visit him, Hezekiah summoned his son Manasseh and began to charge him regarding his duty to fear God, how he ought to govern the kingdom, and many other things. And Isaiah said to him: "Truly, because your words do not descend into his heart, it is fitting that I myself should be put to death by his hand." When Hezekiah heard this, he wished to put his own son to death, saying: "For it is better for me to die without a son than to leave behind such a son, who would both provoke God and persecute His saints." But Isaiah the Prophet barely restrained him, saying: "May God frustrate this counsel of yours" — seeing the piety of Hezekiah, because he loved God more than his own son. Yet this appears to be a Rabbinical fable. For when Hezekiah fell sick, Manasseh had not yet been born; since after his illness Hezekiah lived on for fifteen years, and Manasseh, who succeeded him at his death, was then twelve years of age. Manasseh was therefore born in the third year after the illness and recovery of his father Hezekiah.

Now in 2 Chronicles 33, verse 11, the punishment inflicted by God upon Manasseh is recounted, namely his captivity in Babylon, and thence his earnest repentance and conversion to God. Moreover, Manasseh appears to have been carried away to Babylon by Merodach-Baladan; for he at that time reigned in Babylon, and to him had Hezekiah, the father of Manasseh, shown his treasures, thereby enticing him to seize them — which God had also foretold would come to pass in chapter 20, verse 17. And this very thing he shortly after brought about, causing Manasseh, on account of his crimes, to be led away in chains to Babylon together with his treasury by that same Baladan. So hold Bellarmine, Salianus, and others. Manasseh is nevertheless said in 4 Kings [2 Kings] chapter 22 to have been captured by the king of the Assyrians — either because the Babylonians are called Assyrians, or because Merodach was a tributary of Esarhaddon, king of the Assyrians, whose yoke he afterwards threw off. AND HE WAS BURIED IN THE GARDEN OF HIS OWN HOUSE, IN THE GARDEN OF OZA — that is, in the place where Oza was struck dead by God because he had rashly touched the tottering ark, as related in 2 Samuel 6:8. This garden was near Jerusalem, joined to the royal gardens, in which Manasseh, on his return from Babylon, built for himself a royal mausoleum, and in which both he and his son Amon were buried.

# GEM Thus does Scripture call idolatry, as though it were the chief evil of all evils; for as the Wise Man says in chapter 14, verse 27: *The worship of abominable idols is the cause, the beginning, and the end of all evil.* Amon imitated his father Manasseh in his sinning, but not in his repentance. Glycas adds that he said: *My father committed many crimes from his youth, yet in old age did penance; wherefore I too, at this age, shall conduct myself according to the pleasure of my soul, and thereafter I shall turn to the Lord.* For this presumption God punished him with a swift death; for after two years he was slain by his own servants. Hear Saint Clement, in book 2 of the *Apostolic Constitutions*, chapter 23: *Such,* he says, *was Amon, the son of Manasseh, concerning whom Scripture declares: Amon deceived himself with the evil thought of transgression, and said: My father committed many sins from his youth, and when he was old he repented of what he had done; and now I will walk as my soul desires, and afterward I will turn to the Lord. And he acted wickedly before the Lord beyond all those born before him, and God swiftly removed him from his good land, and his servants rose up against him and killed him in his own house, and he reigned only two years.* Let young men take heed of this — those who in youth give themselves to pleasure and luxury, and say that youth belongs to the devil, promising that old age they will dedicate to God; for the greater part of such men are overtaken by the various misfortunes of human life and die, and by the just judgment of God they do not reach old age, nor therefore arrive at amendment of life and eternal salvation. This name was foretold and divinely appointed for him 327 years in advance; for so many years elapsed from Jeroboam and the first Jeroboam until Josiah, as recorded in 3 Kings 13:2. For "Josiah" in Hebrew means, as it were, that is, "gift of God" or "oblation," or alternatively, that is, "fire of God," on account of his zeal and his fiery sacrifices, and because he burned idolaters with fire. Hear the Author of the Opus Imperfectum, cited by Chrysostom, Homily 1 on Matthew: "Josiah is interpreted as 'victim to the Lord' or 'salvation of the Lord.' By the Providence of God, in accordance with what Josiah was to become, it was arranged that this name should be given to him — 'victim to the Lord' or 'salvation of the Lord' — because truly in him was the victim of the Lord. For he offered to the Lord God such a sacrifice as neither any king before him, nor any after him. And he himself was a victim to God, according to the Apostle's command: 'That you present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy, pleasing to God.' And he himself was to his people in his time the salvation that comes from God." And further: "For Josiah is the third whose name was prophesied before he was born. These three are: Samson, Josiah, and John."
In the restoration of the Temple, Josiah imitated his great-grandfather Joash, King of Judah, and followed his method in carrying out the work of restoration.

That is, no account or reckoning was to be demanded of them, but all the money and expenditure was to be entrusted to their fidelity — which Joash had ordered in different words at chapter 12, verse 15, saying: "No accounting was required of those men who received the money to distribute it to the workmen, but they dealt faithfully." As for the Book of the Law of the King found in the Temple of the Lord — this was the Deuteronomy, as Chrysostom says in Homily 9 on Matthew chapter 2, as do St. Athanasius in his Letter to Marcellinus, and Abulensis; or it was the entire Pentateuch, as Josephus states, who adds that it was found in the treasury while gold was being taken from it for the repairs to the Temple roof. For during the reign of the impious Manasseh, who reigned for fifty-five years, through so long a span of years the Law had been so utterly neglected and the worship of God so profaned that the Book of the Law is described as something newly discovered, as Cajetan observes. Lyran adds, following Rabbi Solomon, that the impious Achaz, who was the grandfather of Manasseh, had burned the books of the Law, lest he be convicted by them of transgressing the Law and of idolatry. For this reason the priests had secretly hidden the Book of the Law within a wall of the Temple which they had hollowed out and sealed shut; and the workmen, while restoring the Temple roof at Josiah's command, found the book and brought it to the High Priest, who then carried it to King Josiah. They went therefore — Hilkiah the priest, and the others — to Huldah the prophetess, wife (the Septuagint of Sixtus has "mother") of Shallum. This Shallum, according to Hebrew tradition, was the uncle of Jeremiah's father. Why did they not go to Jeremiah, who was prophesying at that time? Perhaps because he was absent from the city. St. Jerome adds, in Book 2 against the Pelagians, that in the fact that a woman is consulted there lies a hidden reproach of the king, the priests, and all the men — that no holy man could be found who was capable of foretelling the future. In sum, Huldah was celebrated and venerable among all for her age, her prudence, her holiness, and her oracles, just as Deborah was in Judges 4, and Hannah the mother of Samuel in 1 Kings 2. # GEM Who dwelt in Jerusalem in the Second Quarter — that is, in that part of the city called the Second Quarter, situated near what was universally regarded as the house of teaching, that is, the schools (Vatable says it lay near the second wall of the city, and near the second or middle gate, which was called the Fish Gate, through which one passed to the Temple, and through which the Chaldeans later invaded Jerusalem and the Temple — so say St. Jerome in the passage already cited, Villalpandus, Ribera, Salian, Serarius, and others; see Adrichomius in his Description of Jerusalem; the Chaldean renders "Second" as a place of second dignity after the Temple, where men devoted themselves to learning and instruction). For in that quarter dwelt the prophets and teachers, and among them the Rechabites. Hence too the Talmud is divided into the Mishnah and the Gemara; for Mishnah signifies "second," as though the first were the Law of Moses, and the second were the Talmudic repetition and application of the Law.

That is, of male prostitutes dedicated to Venus and Priapus, just as Asa had likewise destroyed them, Book 3, chapter 15, verses 12 and 13. FOR WHOM THE WOMEN WOVE (from threads, or branches) as it were little houses in the grove, so that with these as curtains and hangings the effeminate ones might veil and conceal their obscenities in the grove which they had erected in the temple, for unspeakable lusts.

That is, the profane temples and shrines erected in high places — by profaning them, destroying them, and filling them with filth and the bones of the dead, as is said in verse 14. Hence, explaining this, he adds: And he destroyed the altars. What the high places were and of what kind I have discussed in Book 3, chapter 3, verse 2. Nevertheless, the priests of the high places did not go up to the altar of the Lord in Jerusalem, but ate unleavened bread among their brethren. That is to say: Josiah did not permit those priests who had sacrificed impiously or illicitly in the high places to go to Jerusalem and minister in the temple, inasmuch as they were polluted by the victims offered to idols or in superstitious rites; yet he did permit them to be sustained by unleavened bread and other foods upon which the other priests and Levites fed from what was offered to them by the people. So say the Abulensis and others.

The place was in Gehenna, that is, in the valley of the sons of Hinnom, near Jerusalem, in which parents burned their children to the idol Moloch; and lest the children's cries be heard, they would beat the toph, that is, the timbrel — whence the place was called Topheth. See Isaiah 30:33 and Jeremiah 7:31. This place, therefore, together with the idol Moloch to whom it was sacred, Josiah defiled and profaned, destroyed and filled with bones and dung.

Who were these horses of the Sun, and of what kind were they? In answer: the Persians and other nations worshipped the Sun on account of his surpassing splendor, beauty, and usefulness as a god, and called him Mithras and Apollo, and dedicated horses to him — partly painted and sculptured, partly real and living — because the horse, swiftest of animals, represents the most swift motion of the Sun. Witnesses to this are Xenophon in Book 8 of the Cyropaedia; Herodotus in Book 1, at the end; Justin in Book 1, and others. Hence Ovid in Book 1 of the Fasti: "Appease with a horse the Persian Hyperion girt with rays, lest a slow victim be offered to the swift god." From this the poets tell the fable that the Sun is drawn in a four-horse chariot by four most swift horses — as Ovid says in Book 2 of the Metamorphoses: "Pyrois, Eous, and Aethon, horses of the Sun, and Phlegon the fourth." By these, the Abulensis thinks are signified the four parts of the year and of the day. The image and idol of the Sun is depicted thus by Macrobius in Book 1 of the Saturnalia, chapter 30: "Now indeed," he says, "that this same Jupiter and the Sun are one and the same is recognized both from the very rite of the sacred ceremonies and from their outward form. For the golden image of youthful beardless appearance stands with the right hand raised holding a whip in the manner of a charioteer, while in the left hand it holds a thunderbolt and ears of grain. All these things demonstrate the combined power of Jupiter and the Sun. The religion of this temple is also most powerful in divination, which is referred to the power of Apollo, who is the same as the Sun. For the image of the Heliopolitan god is carried on a litter, just as the images of the gods are carried in procession in the Circensian games; and the leading men of the province commonly take their place beneath it, with shaved heads, pure from long-practiced chastity, and they are borne by the divine spirit not by their own will but wherever the God impels those who carry him." Macrobius adds that for this reason the God is called by the Syrians Adad, meaning "the one," because the Sun is one even as God is one in the world. The same author, in Book 1 on the Dream of Scipio, says: "Cicero asserts that the Sun is the guide, and prince, and regulator of the remaining lights" — whom Heraclitus calls the source of heavenly light. "He is called guide," the author continues, "because he precedes all others in the majesty of his light. He is called prince because he so excels that, since he alone appears as such, he is called sol — the sun. He is called regulator of the rest because he governs the fixed extent of their courses and returnings." And shortly after: "He is called the mind of the world, just as natural philosophers have called him the heart of the heavens, in that the Sun's course and reason dispenses all these things — the day and night, then the mild temperature of spring, the scorching heat of Cancer and Leo, the softness of the autumn breeze, and the force of cold between the two temperate seasons. Rightly therefore is he called the heart of the heavens, through whom all things come to pass that we see come to pass by divine reason." You ask secondly: By what rite did they worship the Sun by means of horses? First, the Historia Scholastica holds the opinion that the Jews represented by these horses and chariot the fiery chariot and horses by which Elijah, as it were the sun — that is, Helios — was caught up into heaven, as we heard in chapter 2. So say Eucherius and Angelomus, who add: "The Greeks, hearing from the Israelites that Elijah had been translated to the heavens by a fiery chariot and fiery horses — or certainly having seen this very thing depicted among other things on a wall — were deceived by the proximity of the name, and believed that the passage of the Sun through the heavens was here signified; and what was a miracle divinely wrought by God they transformed into a basis for error, invented by human folly. The Jews themselves, imitating these, took great pains lest in anything they should appear less foolish than the most foolish of the gentiles." Secondly, and better, Rabbi Solomon says: "In the early morning they would lead forth horses to meet the rising Sun, to greet him and show him reverence." The Abulensis criticizes this, but without good reason; for the horses —

That is, of the Mount of Olives, which was called the Mount of Offence on account of the idols and shrines erected there by Solomon. For idols and the worship of idols constitute the supreme injury and offence against God. Hence it is immediately added: *And Chemosh the offence* (that is, the idol that offends God) *of Moab.* From this, an idol in Scripture is called an offence. So say Eucherius and Angelomus. *And he filled the places with the bones of the dead.* For by this means the place sacred to idols was profaned, contaminated, and rendered vile, unclean, and abominable—especially among the Jews, who contracted legal uncleanness from contact with bones and corpses. Thus also the Gentiles shuddered at unburied bones and corpses. Hear Virgil in the sixth book of the Aeneid: *Moreover there lies before you the lifeless body of your friend—alas! you know it not—and defiles the whole fleet with death.*

*Moreover also the altar that was in Bethel, and the high place which Jeroboam had made* (namely, the shrine in which he had placed his idols—to wit, the golden calves—that they might be worshipped by all Israel), *he destroyed.* For although Bethel was in Israel and not in Judah, where Josiah reigned, nevertheless, since the Israelites had already been carried away from Israel into Assyria by Shalmaneser, and their king and kingdom had been overthrown, the land of Israel appeared to revert to its ancient lord, namely to the family of David and to Josiah king of Judah; especially because God had foretold and promised three hundred years before, in Book 3, chapter 13, verse 2, that Josiah would do this in Bethel—and by this very fact had given Josiah the right and power to do so, indeed had ordained and commanded that he should do it.

The title marked the sepulchre of the prophet who had prophesied against Jeroboam and the golden calves placed in Bethel, and had foretold that their destruction by Josiah was to come. It is called a *title* because the tomb was distinguished by some sign, so that Josiah might know that a worshipper of the true God lay buried there, and would therefore spare it. What this sign was Scripture does not explain. Rabbi Solomon fancifully relates that from the bones of the prophet there grew herbs beautiful to behold, fragrant in scent, and wholesome in use, from which Josiah gathered that those bones were the remains of a holy man and prophet. Others more plausibly conjecture that the sign was the sign of the cross, or the name Jehovah, or something similar.

For when the Assyrians departed after laying Samaria waste, many Israelite fugitives and exiles returned to their homes, and there, as before, worshipped their idols in the high places; and therefore they appointed priests to sacrifice to them—priests whom Josiah, as a zealot for the divine honour, accordingly slew, and sacrificed as it were as victims upon those very altars to divine justice, consecrating them thereupon. *And he burned human bones upon them.* These were the bones both of the priests slain by his own hand and of those of earlier times, which he drew out from their sepulchres and burned upon the altar, thus contaminating and defiling it. For this had been foretold by that prophet who was slain by a lion, in Book 3, chapter 13, verse 10: *He cried*, it says, *against the altar by the word of the Lord, and said: O altar, altar, thus saith the Lord: Behold, a son shall be born to the house of David, Josiah by name, and he shall sacrifice upon thee the priests of the high places who now burn incense upon thee, and he shall burn human bones upon thee.* From this it is clear that the dead can be punished for their crimes by the burning of their corpses, by public infamy and ignominy. Hence, when it was inquired at the Fifth Ecumenical Council whether it was lawful to strike those long deceased with anathema, Eutychius, the respondent of the Bishop of Amasia, said that the matter required neither a council nor any deliberation: because Josiah the king had not only slaughtered the living priests of demons, but had also dug up the sepulchres of those who had been buried long before. So Nicephorus, Book 17 of his History, chapter 27. # GEM

That is: *Sacrifice the Paschal lamb* and the other Paschal victims, which by law are commanded to be offered throughout the seven days of unleavened bread, Numbers 28, verses 16 and following. *Allegorically:* Josiah, in instituting this most solemn Passover, was a type of Jesus Christ, who instituted the Eucharist—indeed, who made Himself the Passover, that is, the Paschal Lamb, according to that word of Paul in 2 Corinthians 5: *Christ our Passover is sacrificed. Therefore let us feast upon the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth.* *There was no king before him like him*—namely, with respect to the total extirpation of idolatry both in Israel and in Judah, and the reformation of religion, in which Josiah surpassed all his predecessors, even Hezekiah; for otherwise, speaking in absolute terms, David appears to have been better and holier than Josiah, says the Abulensis.

*In his days Pharaoh Necho* (that is, the lame) *king of Egypt went up against the king of the Assyrians*—to avenge the wrongs inflicted upon Egypt by Sennacherib, and to recover the spoils taken by him; especially because he saw that, following the disaster inflicted upon Sennacherib's camp by the Angel in Judaea, the affairs of the Assyrians were going from bad to worse, and that the Medes and Babylonians had revolted against them. Pharaoh therefore marched with his army to the Euphrates and to Nineveh, in hope of taking possession of it. But since his route lay through Judaea, Josiah went out to meet him, fearing lest he invade and plunder Judaea; and although Pharaoh declared that he intended nothing against Josiah and Judaea, Josiah, giving him no credence, drew up his battle line against him, in which engagement, while fighting with more boldness than caution, he was pierced by archers and fell. Now the Abulensis entirely acquits Josiah of fault, on the ground that he feared Pharaoh would, if he entered Judaea, devise some scheme against it—indeed, occupy it so as to be impossible to expel. Yet Scripture in 2 Chronicles 35:22 hints that he sinned with some degree of rashness: for although he was not bound to believe Pharaoh when he spoke truth, namely that he had been sent by another God, nevertheless he ought first to have consulted God before undertaking so doubtful and perilous a war; so says Cajetan. And therefore God permitted him to be defeated and slain. *And he was slain at Megiddo.* For although Megiddo lay in the lot of Manasseh and Israel, not of Judah over which Josiah ruled, Josiah had nevertheless subjected that land to himself as well, for the reason I gave at verse 15. To which the Abulensis adds another reason: namely, that the remaining Israelites, after the captivity under Shalmaneser, had joined themselves to the kingdom of Judah and accepted Josiah as their king, that united with the Jews they might be stronger and more able to resist the Syrians, Egyptians, and other surrounding enemies. *When he saw him*—namely in battle, that is, when he had engaged him in combat. Thus *to see* is taken by catachresis for *to fight*, as in chapter 14, verse 8: *Come and let us see one another*, that is, come and let us fight. Furthermore, the judgement of God was marvellous in that He permitted so pious a king to be slain in so just and godly a defence of Israel: for with Josiah slain, all the hope of Israel perished, and the kingdom of David and Judah was destroyed. Truly, *the judgements of God are a great deep.* God willed to take Josiah away lest he should see the calamities of his people—namely, the destruction of the kingdom and the Temple, now already decreed by God on account of the sins of the Jews. Another cause is offered by St. Justin Martyr, Question 79 to the Orthodox: namely, that Josiah had not obeyed the command of the prophet who had told him by the mandate of God not to advance to meet the king of Egypt in battle, as Jeremiah relates. Wherefore, that the Lord God might receive him pure from sins from this life, He permitted him to pay the penalty of his disobedience with the sword of Egypt—as a lesson to men of later ages, that they should not be disobedient to prophets.

After slaying Josiah, Pharaoh advanced against the Assyrians, and having conducted that affair successfully, he returned victorious and subjugated the remainder of Judah. Therefore, removing Jehoahaz, whom the people had established as king, he appointed Jehoiakim, and imposed upon Judah, as a land now subdued by himself, a fine of one hundred talents of silver. For that war had been a bloody one, inasmuch as Jehoahaz and the Judeans had wounded and slain many Egyptians in it, as Ezekiel indicates in chapter 19, verse 4; see what is said there, as well as Jeremiah chapter 22, verses 10 and following. Moreover, Pharaoh feared Jehoahaz, lest he should rebel again. **Verse 34.** *And he changed his name to Jehoiakim.* It was the custom of kings and princes to change the names of those subdued in war and to impose new ones as a sign of subjection, as is evident from Daniel chapter 1. Add to this that Jehoiakim and Eliakim are the same name, for both signify in Hebrew *God will establish*: for the divine name is both *El*, meaning *the Mighty One*, and *Ia*, which is an abbreviated form of *Jehovah*. **Verse 37.** *And he did evil in the sight of the Lord* — that is, he worshipped idols. Moreover he greedily plundered his subjects, and was arrogant and ambitious. For this reason, having been slain by the Chaldeans, he was buried with the burial of an ass — that is, he was left unburied and cast upon the dungheap after the manner of an ass, as Jeremiah teaches in chapter 22, verses 13 and following. See what is said there. **CHAPTER THE TWENTY-FOURTH**

Brigands is the term applied to soldiers who maraud and sally forth on plundering raids, as it were like petty thieves. These Nebuchadnezzar sent, so that, joined with Syrians, Moabites and Ammonites, they might intercept Jerusalem, and capture and kill the rebellious Jehoiakim; which they indeed accomplished, as I have already noted. *And Jehoiachin his son reigned in his place.*] Note well. Jehoiakim the father and Jehoiachin the son are distinct persons — the latter being also called Jeconiah and Jechoniah (Matthew 1). For the father, Jehoiakim, is written in Hebrew with the letters Koph and Mem, whereas the son Jehoiachin is written with Koph and Nun. Jehoiakim in Hebrew means "God will establish"; Jehoiachin, however, means "God will prepare" or "God will direct." Jehoiachin, or Jechoniah, is shortened by Jeremiah at chapter 22 in the Hebrew text and called simply Coniah, that is, "one prepared" — namely, for destruction.

You may object: in 2 Chronicles, final chapter, verse 9, it is said that he reigned in his eighth year. The answer is: in his eighteenth year he reigned alone after his father, but in his eighth year he had begun to reign jointly with his father Jehoiakim. For it appears that Jehoiachin was proclaimed king in the third or fourth year of his father Jehoiakim, that is, when Nebuchadnezzar seized his father and carried him away to Babylon. So says the Abulensis. *And he reigned three months.*] How then does 2 Chronicles, final chapter, verse 10, say that he was carried away by Nebuchadnezzar after a year? Cajetan answers that several months of interregnum elapsed between Jehoiakim and Jehoiachin before Jehoiachin assumed the kingdom. I shall offer another reply at 2 Chronicles, final chapter, verse 10.

— at the urging of Jeremiah, that he should surrender himself to Nebuchadnezzar, as is evident from Jeremiah 38:2 and following. And therefore, though he was indeed led into Babylon, yet after the death of Nebuchadnezzar he was freed from prison by his son Evil-merodach, and was treated with the highest honour in the thirty-seventh year after this event, as is recorded in the following chapter, verse 27. *And he carried away all Jerusalem,*] that is, all the citizens and inhabitants of Jerusalem. This was the second captivity. For the first had taken place some eight years earlier, in the fourth year of Jehoiakim, when he himself, having rebelled, was carried away to Babylon along with the chief men, as I noted at verse 1. The second was this present captivity, in which Jehoiachin was carried away with his princes, his soldiers, and the Prophet Ezekiel. The third and final captivity occurred eleven years later, namely in the last year of Zedekiah, when Jerusalem together with the Temple was captured and overthrown by Nebuchadnezzar, as will be related in the following chapter. *And every craftsman and locksmith,*] that is, the locksmith — *clusorem*, as our text renders it, following Jeremiah 24:1 — by which St. Jerome in that same place understands one who sets precious stones in costly metals and fastens them within the bezels of golden rings. Others, more aptly, understand by this those who are skilled in laying out military camps and know how to enclose them with their own ramparts, and to fortify and defend both themselves and cities. So say Vatablus, Sanchez, and others. Hence the Chaldean version renders it: "gatekeepers," that is, guardians of cities and closers of gates. For the passage treats of soldiers and warriors, whom the Chaldeans removed from the Jews lest they should rebel again — just as the Philistines did, as recorded in 1 Samuel 13:19. So says Angelus. Thus by "craftsman" understand a smith, as our text renders it at Jeremiah 24:1, namely one who forges swords and other weapons. The devil employs this same stratagem when he strives to remove zealous men from the commonwealth, so as to lay it waste by depriving it of its defenders. # GEM

So that Zedekiah himself might be perpetually reminded by his very name of the fidelity and submission he had promised to preserve toward Nebuchadnezzar; for "Zedekiah" in Hebrew means "the Lord is my justice." So says Cajetan. The pious king Josiah had given pious names to his sons, names that would admonish them of their duty of piety toward God. Hence he called one Jehoahaz, meaning "the seizure or possession of God," so that whenever he heard himself named he might reflect that he was seized and possessed by God; another he called Eliakim, meaning "the strength of God," so that he might reflect that he was strengthened by none but God; a third he called Mattaniah, meaning "the gift of God," or, according to the Syriac, "expectation," so that in all adversities he might flee to the Lord as to a sanctuary. But all of them, forgetting God equally with their own names, fled to idols, and therefore perished by God's avenging hand.

That is, he contended with him in judgment and justly condemned him, saying: "You have broken the faith pledged to me and have rebelled; therefore you justly deserve to be blinded by me." Rabbi Solomon adds other details, but after his custom he deals in fables.

Jeremiah in the final chapter gives the tenth day; reconcile both accounts thus: that Nebuzaradan came to Jerusalem on the seventh day, but burned the Temple on the tenth. So Cajetan. Or rather, he departed from Riblah, where Nebuchadnezzar was, on the seventh day, and arrived at Jerusalem on the tenth — for the journey takes three days.

# GEM The Septuagint renders it *archimагirus*, that is, "chief of the cooks"; in Hebrew, "prince of those who slaughter"; in Chaldean, "master of the slayers." Hence St. Gregory mystically understands by him gluttony, which overthrows the temple of our soul. Hear him in the *Pastoral*, Admonition 20: "The prince of cooks demolished the walls of Jerusalem. For the prince of cooks is the belly, upon which great care and service is lavished by cooks, so that it may itself be filled with delectable foods. But the walls of Jerusalem are the virtues of the soul, elevated toward the desire of heavenly peace. The prince of cooks therefore casts down the walls of Jerusalem; because when the belly is distended by gluttony, the virtues of the soul are destroyed through lust." Wherefore Abbot Poemen in the *Lives of the Fathers* says: "Unless Nebuzaradan the prince of cooks had come, the Temple of God would not have burned"; that is, unless the desire of gluttony had crept in, the soul would not have been consumed by lust. Angelomus, however, understands by Nebuzaradan the devil, who is accustomed to plunder all the goods of the faithful. Regarding *trullae*: these are not trowels but trullae, that is, lids of pots, called *trulla*, says Abulensis, from *trudendo*, because it thrusts, that is, closes the pot, so that the trullae are as it were "thrusters" — for our author joins trullae with pots. Indeed in Hebrew, Chaldean, and the Septuagint, no word corresponds to *trullae*. Yet ancient authors understand by *trulla* a vessel for pouring water. So Nonius Marcellus says: "*Trulleum*, with which hands are washed." Titinius in the *Setina*: "Wisdom, he says, is the helmsman who steers the ship, not strength; the cook, when a great pot boils strongly, checks it with a small ladle." Varro, Book 4: "Trulla, he says, by analogy with *trua*, which because it is large, and this small, is as it were a *trulla*. Hence the Greek *trublion*, we say *trulla*. Trullae are those which pour water from the kitchen into the lavatory. *Trua*, because the water flows abroad from it, is named from the same source." Varro therefore thinks *trulla* is named from flying across (*a travolando*); Nonius Marcellus from rubbing, as if *terula*; Isidore from pushing (*a trudendo*). Hear him, Book 19. Origen, chapter 18: "The name *trulla* is formed because it thrusts and pushes away, that is, encloses stones in lime or mud." The allegory and tropology here is easy and fitting. For Jerusalem is the Church, and equally the faithful soul contemplating God through faith; idols are created and vain things which it pursues against the law of God — for example, the idol of the covetous man is gold, of the lustful man is lust, of the proud man is honor. Nebuchadnezzar is the devil, who, having captured Jerusalem, that is the soul, blinds its king Zedekiah, that is the mind — for Zedekiah in Hebrew means the same as "the justice of God," which the mind ought to contemplate, fear, and imitate — then despoils it of all graces and goods, and burns the Temple, that is, all fear and worship of God, and thereafter takes it captive, depriving it of its liberty, and leads it bound into Babylon, that is, into the uttermost confusion of all things, such as exists in a soul subjected to its lusts, especially when it is cast into hell. Yet before that comes to pass, Cyrus — that is, Christ — liberates many from this captivity. So Angelomus and Eucherius.

Jehoiachin was therefore in Babylonian imprisonment for as long as Nebuchadnezzar survived, that is for 37 years; but when he died, Evil-merodach, succeeding his father, freed Jehoiachin and exalted him. The cause is given in Jeremiah, chapter 52, toward the end of the chapter. **SYNCHRONISM OF THE KINGS OF JUDAH, THE PROPHETS, AND THE KINGS AND HEROES OF THE GENTILES.** Contemporary with Solomon were the prophet Nathan, Hiram king of Tyre, Hesiod, and Homer among the Greeks. Contemporary with Rehoboam were the prophet Ahijah, Jeroboam the first king of Israel, and Laosthenes king of the Assyrians. Contemporary with Asa were the prophet Jehu, Baasha king of Israel, and Ophralsaeus king of the Assyrians. In the time of Jehoshaphat flourished the prophet Elijah, who was caught up to heaven in a chariot of fire. Under Joram, Elisha prophesied for sixty-six years, and died under King Amaziah. Under Joash lived Jehoiada the High Priest, and his son Zechariah the martyr. Over Israel there reigned in succession Jehu, who cut down the house of Ahab, Jehoahaz, and Joash. The brother of King Amaziah was Amos, father of the prophet Isaiah. Therefore Isaiah had Joash as his grandfather, and Amaziah as his father's king; at this time Lycurgus gave laws to the Spartans. In the time of Azariah, also called Uzziah, Isaiah began to prophesy, and prophesied for sixty years under the kings Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, Hezekiah, and Manasseh; at that time Hosea, Joel, Amos, Jonah, and Obadiah also prophesied. Around the fortieth year of Azariah, the Olympiads began to be reckoned, that is the years of Iphitus the king, who instituted them. The Olympiads precede the founding of Rome by 23 years. Therefore around the tenth year of Jotham, Rome was founded by Romulus, who was the first to reign in it for 36 years. He was succeeded by Numa Pompilius for 44 years; by Tullus Hostilius for 32 years; by Ancus Martius for 14 years; by Tarquinius Priscus for 38 years; by Servius Tullius for 44 years; by Tarquinius Superbus for 25 years. Under Jotham prophesied Nahum and Micah. At the same time, Sardanapalus, besieged in Nineveh by Arbaces the prefect of Media, burned himself together with the palace and all the treasury. Wherefore Arbaces made himself king of Media; but Baladan, who was also called Nabonassar, seized Babylonia, from whom was established the Era of Nabonassar, which begins from the fifteenth year of Jotham. Under Ahaz, Damascus was captured by the Assyrians, and their king Tiglath-pileser carried off half of the ten tribes of Israel into Assyria. Contemporary with Hezekiah were Merodach son of Baladan — or of Nabonassar the first king of Babylon — and Shalmaneser king of the Assyrians, who carried off Israel, that is the remnants of the ten tribes, including among them Tobit, into Assyria. Likewise his son Sennacherib, who while besieging Jerusalem was put to flight by an Angel with 185,000 slain. At that time also flourished the Erythraean Sibyl, as St. Augustine witnesses in *The City of God*, Book 18, chapter 23. Manasseh cut the prophet Isaiah, his own kinsman, asunder with a wooden saw. At that time among the Lydians flourished Gyges, who, having slain Candaules, obtained the kingdom; his great-grandson was Croesus, whom Cyrus defeated in battle and subjugated. At that time also in Egypt reigned Psammetichus, whose son Necho defeated and slew Josiah. At that time too shone Thales of Miletus, one of the seven sages of Greece, and after him in succession the remaining six sages. Under Manasseh likewise Numa Pompilius reigned at Rome, and the Samian Sibyl flourished. At the same time many hold that the history of Judith occurred. Under Josiah Jeremiah began to prophesy, and in the time of Jehoiakim, Jehoiachin, and Zedekiah he prophesied for 44 years, and at length in Egypt was overwhelmed with stones by the Jews and died a martyr. Under the same king prophesied Baruch, Zephaniah, and Huldah the prophetess. At Rome reigned Tullus...
